Great to be here after a long night trying to push this article. But yeah, I'm excited it's out. It's a big one.

It's a pretty cool one as always. And there's some real interesting things happening to Cerebris.

Need for Speed: Why Fast Tokens, Why Now

Basically, like you said, there's a need for speed. Fast tokens are a thing now. Before we kind of just dismissed it as it's cool, but not necessary. But now we're seeing that people are really willing to pay more for speed. And that's what Cerebrus is really good at.

Yeah, people are willing to pay more for speed just with tuning for interactivity on existing HBM-based accelerators like GPUs and GPUs, where the trade is like two to two and a half times faster for six X the price. But Cerebris kind of extends that to like potentially 10 X faster, maybe more depending on the model and depending on the profitability you want to run the endpoint at, and potentially exponentially more.

I mean, we don't know exactly what the profitability of the 4 5.3 codec spark endpoint with OpenAI is, as the biggest public example of people using Cerebris for inference. But if you say conceptually, what are the SRA machines promising? It's generally 10 times faster at some exponential on top of 10 times the price, maybe 20 times the price or up to like 50 times the price per token. So it'll be interesting to see them play with that part of the Pareto curve as this gets rolled out.

Yeah. So I mean, we've played around a bit with the endpoint, right? And then we got, I think, on OSS 120B, you know, like 1100 tokens a second. And if you look at our inference X benchmarking for all the NVIDIA and AMD GPUs, they top out interactivity wise at 500. It's literally off the charts performance, right? GPUs can't do this.

Yeah, at any price. But Howie made the point earlier that there's some upper bound and some lower bound at which people are willing to pay for stuff, and then it just doesn't become worth it anymore — or willing to save money, and then it doesn't become worth it anymore.

Yes, exactly. So one of the points that CEO Andrew Feldman was making is that in the future, everyone would want fast tokens. And he put an analogy to like the internet, right? Where if you gave me — I mean, I'm based in Singapore — so if you gave me even 100 meg internet, you would have to pay me and I still wouldn't take it, right? Like I'll take gigabit and above, right? So his analogy was that eventually people won't even bother paying for slow tokens. And so everyone will move up the stack in terms of the tokens per second.

But of course, there's definitely a bound to that. You know, you will never run video generation on Cerebra, so there'll still be plenty of market for that. But at least, you know, he had a solution looking for a problem and now the problem has come. And we'll see how far that goes.

The Marquee Customers: OpenAI & Amazon

Yeah. I mean, there's so much demand. It's making everybody look good right now. Like clearly, the OpenAI deal is the marquee customer they're going to sign, but also the Amazon deal where, you know, they've got like clearly two blue chip customers to show significant interest, back it with finances and not be necessarily dependent on G42 or the MBZ University for all their business.

Like when they initially filed the S1 in 2024, it was a lot of questions about the concentration of the business amongst their investors. And still seems like investors are buying a lot of chips, but it's less concentrated — or it's still concentrated, but the total business has grown so much that it's a different company. OpenAI instead of G42 that is concentrated towards now.

Yeah, exactly. I think, like you said, up until now, it was pretty much — I don't know — almost like a hundred percent of the revenues were going to these Middle Eastern labs or research institutions. But now you have proper frontier labs using Cerebris, right?

Yeah. Amazon serving probably anthropic tokens on bedrock, or they will, with a disaggregated setup with Tranium for the pre-fill and then Waiverscale engines for the decode. So yeah, I mean, it's been a huge turnaround. And I think like Howie said, the problem has finally come to them, the one that they can really address. And it's a big market. You only need a few percent to make billions of dollars. So yeah, it's the time.

And then I think similar, with your fast tokens, it's also been at the same time, right? At the same time, the OpenAI deal was announced, it was when NVIDIA bought GROK, right? Same idea, different kind of execution, right? Different way of going about it, but still leveraging the strength of really fast SRAM to deliver tokens fast.

The Wafer: Reticle Stitching & Yield Recovery

Yeah. So maybe we could dig into the chip now. Like, we love the wafer. For the general audience here, what's it mean to have a wafer scale chip, and maybe how is it different than GROK? Like, they're both SRAM machines, but can one of you guys break down — what is the wafer? Why do we love the wafer so much?

Yeah, Myron, could you share a screen of the article and then you can pull some images?

Yeah, yeah, I'll throw it up. Yeah, so Cerebra's special sauce is truly in the radical stitching. So as you know, your ASML lithography machine can only pattern a certain size of area, which is 26 by 33 millimeters. So that's what's known as a radical limit. So as you can see here, the Cerebras wafer, what they've done is they've patterned the wafer with 84 of these patterns, right, these dye radicals.

And their special sauce is they're able to stitch them together with a special method that is done in collaboration with TSMC, where the upper metal wiring layers — they are connected in such a way that not only are they horizontally and vertically connected to each other, but they also have ways to route around defects, right, which helps them to yield the wafer. Otherwise, you know, if they use a traditional method, one catastrophic defect can basically — you have to throw away the whole wafer.

So these two key innovations, right — the radical stitching and the yield recovery — are what enables them to have such a huge amount of SRAM, huge amount of compute, on a single wafer, on a single chip.

And then the fundamental benefit of this is, of course, as you know, in AI, we are extremely data movement bottlenecked. And so the most efficient way to transfer data is to just stay within the chip, right? Even your HBM or like your chiplet solutions over the Coors Interposer, even that is still nowhere near as efficient as just staying within the die. So if you can have a huge die, then you can have a huge amount of compute without needing to transfer a lot of data in and out.

So the analogy they like to give is that one wafer almost represents like a whole NVL72 rack, right? It's like having 84 chips in the same network and you don't have to move off the wafer. So huge savings in terms of communication power. And also you get a lot of benefits when you're able to parallelize a model across an entire wafer. So yeah, a lot of techniques there as well, which some of our guys have more experience with in terms of how the models can perform so well on a wafer.

Tiny Cores & Defect Routing

Yeah. So this next image goes through just what it looks like, right? So one of the ways that they achieve this high yield, or like not catastrophically bad, is to use a very small core.

So if you imagine your core, if it's like six square millimeters, which is like the size of a NVIDIA SM — if that is defective, if there's one defect on that, I have to disable the whole SM. Whereas if I have a really tiny core, which in this case is, you know, around 200 by 200 microns in width and height, then if there's a defect there, I just have to disable a very, very, very small part of the dye.

With this, they have this grid, right? So you have 10,700 cores. In fact, there's more than that. One of the presentations Cerebrus gave was that there's around 970,000 cores on the wafer and they enable only 900,000, right? So that's the number that they market — but they have over-provision, they have extra cost on the die, on the wafer, so that they can get the yield and ship enough wafers.

So this routing mechanism, right, in order to bypass the defective cost is also another reason why they can achieve the good yield. Because for the software, right — if you imagine I have a defect in the core, in the wafer, my software, I don't need to, or I don't want to have to make my software aware of this defect, right? Like the software should just want to see a continuous two-dimensional Cartesian grid.

So this innovative routing system allows them to bypass a defective call, right, while still showing to the software that it is still a grid. So that means, for example, if I have a defect, I will shift the entire routing grid offset by one call so that — if you actually draw the grid out topologically — it still looks like a continuous 2D mesh without any missing gaps. Right. So that's one of the innovations there.

Building the System: Power, Cooling & Mounting

Yeah. So that's just the wafer. And then the next part, if we scroll down to the hardware and how they are able to build this right into a machine.

So, you know, the traditional packaging, it's a dye on a substrate, like your ABF, Ajinomoto substrate, and then that gets attached to I.O. Whereas for the wafer scale system, you have a whole other problem with regards to temperature expansion and power delivery, because how are you going to get the power in and out when the whole thing is going to generate a huge amount of power, talking 25 kilowatts and even more in the next generations.

So here we see the current implementation, which actually is quite old — it's from 2018, when they were very first designed and they've kept it since then, but this will change in the future design. But you can see here the WSE3 engine block on the top right, the wafer is actually placed vertically, right? And one side faces the front, one side faces the back, with power coming in from one side, right, and then cooling coming from the other side.

Yeah, power's the purple there on the left side, right?

Yeah. The PSUs, yeah. This is in fact the first true example of vertical power delivery, right? Because the power is coming in perpendicular to the silicon, which is necessary for this kind of wafer scale design. So there's also some of the things that they've had to innovate, right, by necessity to achieve this practical implementation of a wafer scale design.

And then in order to mount the wafer onto the PCB for power delivery, because the wafer will expand and contract with temperature at a different rate to the PCB and the surrounding system, they needed to implement this custom flexible connector, right? It's almost like an interposer, but it has pins that can move around so that when the wafer heats up and cools down, the connections are maintained. So this is another proprietary innovation that they have to come up with, right?

And cooling — you have a really complex cold plate that distributes the water onto each of the 84 dyes and then does the direct impingement downwards. So you end up with a pretty interesting design that's not seen anywhere else. And that's how they're able to achieve this.

Networking & I/O Limits

So then the last one, which I will talk about, is networking and how much I.O. goes out of the wafer.

So as you know, for the wafer scale design, you can see the wafer here, you only have the perimeter to have I.O. out of the wafer. So in this case, there's a north side and a south side with special FPGAs in order to convert the wafer signal into something that the outside world can understand, which in this case is Ethernet over optical modules.

In this particular instance, Cerebras has gone with 1.2 terabits per second worth of bandwidth of the wafer, which was also a design decision from 2018 in the original CS1. And we'll talk about the implications of that in the inference section later.

If you look at the bandwidth to compute ratio, it seems to be far, far lower than our current modern accelerators. So why can't they add more bandwidth — or I guess this is just a kind of natural limitation of doing this radical stitched wafer scale implementation, right?

Yeah, so firstly for wafer patterning, right, you can't really pattern different reticles or different masks onto the same wafer, meaning that I can't have half of my wafer be compute reticles and half of my wafer be I.O. reticles, right? Like it all has to be uniform based on the manufacturing flow as well as some of the chemical deposition and intensity, things like that. So this means that if you wanted to have a lot of I.O., that means all 84 dice must have provisioned some of the area for I.O. But then only the perimeter ones will actually get used. So that's not a very efficient use of the silicon area, right? So that's one thing.

And then the other thing that we should explain in the article was to do with the routing, right? Your 2D routing, where if you placed I.O. in the middle, as with the example we mentioned, then you have to route around these I.O. elements which would introduce additional complexity.

Yeah. So in the case of Cerebras, they don't really want to mess with the design, which they've, you know, perfected with the CS1. So, but we'll see if they make any changes in the future, right? Regarding some of the things they were talking about regarding optical wafers and DRAM wafers, which is quite aspirational, but it seems like the next logical roadmap.

How Do They Scale From Here?

Yeah. Before we move on, one thing I would like to address is — some people have been asking, okay, so now they've made a chip the size of a wafer. How do they scale from here? Right? Like how do they improve performance? You can't increase the area anymore. Like your TPUs and your NVIDIA GPUs, you're getting bigger and bigger, but they see Cerebrus is already at the limit, right?

So one way is to go vertical, which is what we've shown here with IO. And then another way is, of course, to go to panel level systems where you can essentially have four of these side by side, which is another kind of moonshot aspirational thing, but it is one of the possible avenues of scaling.

So what about just — sorry — what about just like traditional Mosul scaling? Right, like, although it has slowed down?

But I think one of the big things is that SRAM scaling is dead. And then a lot of the area on a wave scale engine is SRAM. So it's 50 percent. So that means that you're just going to the next node, say from N5 to N3, a lot of — they just lose a lot of the benefits and it's pretty hard for them to increase on chip memory capacity because of this.

Yeah, that's true. So in the investor roadshow, they did disclose that the next generation system, CS4, will use the exact same WSE3 wafer, which is on 5 nanometer. So clearly they agree as well, right? If not, they would also jump to the 3 nanometer bandwagon.

But they did mention other ways of increasing the amount of memory, which is to do with the area efficiency. So if I can get my compute with a new node, if I can get my compute area down, I have more room to place my SRAM. So there's one way to increase SRAM by maybe like 20, 30%. And then on top of that, you have these scaling factors such as the vertical SRAM, like AMD's 3D cache, or even a future 3D DRAM, right, which is like 10 to 15 times the bit density per area of SRAM. So these are some of the more difficult ways, but still ways to achieve this scaling, right?

So, but on the other hand, this is kind of a good thing, because by not moving to 3nm, Ceribus is kind of like — they're not going to face the shortages, right, on the wafer, which our accelerator model has already shown, right? So it's almost like a blessing in disguise where they're able to grow and to scale from hundreds to thousands of wafers without facing kind of bottlenecks on the supply chain, right? The other one being, of course, this has no HPM and no DRAM. So it is quite easy to scale, right?

The Bottlenecks: Drilling Holes & Becoming a NeoCloud

Yeah. What's the trade-off there? What bottleneck do you think Cerebers is exposed to that others may not be, based on how they produce this or how they test it or something like that?

Yeah, so right now their main bottleneck as a startup is just scaling, right? So this is the biggest question for them, right? How can they scale to wafer production of thousands of wafers per month?

And even beyond that, because their current setup is — they do a lot of, they do some of the assembly in-house, means they have their own factory office, right, next to their design house in California, where they receive the wafers and they do some of the proprietary assembly, like the connector that I mentioned earlier, as well as some processing step.

One of the steps is this drilling of the holes, right? So in order to mount the wafer with enough mounting pressure, you can't just like sandwich the wafer between the two, the PCB and the co-plate, you need to have a lot of screw holes in the wafer, you know, to get the even mounting pressure. So this is something that they do themselves, which you can't like — you can't just say, oh, drill me a hole 10 times faster, right? Like, this is a slow process. They're really careful not to crack the die when you're trying to drill a hole in the middle of an active wafer. So this is something that they will need to figure out how to scale.

So cool, man. The bottleneck is drilling holes.

Yeah, and a bunch of other stuff. But that's like one of the unique challenges for them, right?

Yeah. As opposed to any other chip, right?

Yeah. So they do have a partner, Flextronics, that does some of the other assembly. But they also have a lot of proprietary setups, right, which they still want to do in-house. So scaling this production, right, is going to be a huge thing, right?

TSMC has absolutely no issue supplying them with the wafer, right? Their radical stitching, it is not any more complex than like what they already do with the co-wass, how TSMC manufactures a co-wass interposer, also with radical stitching. So that side is not an issue, right? It's more on the production, on the wafer assembly.

And then the other thing that the data center model shows — the other question is how quickly can they ramp their own data centers? Because as we talk about in the article, they are almost becoming their own NeoCloud, right? Instead of selling the wafers and systems, they are selling it as a service. So ramping the data center capacity is also another thing that is gonna be another thing they need to look out for.

Yeah. We talked about this a little bit, but yeah, basically there's likely going to be some difficulties with them. Like, we've been through a little bit on the wafer itself, on the design of the cores, the bet on being able to make a wafer scale chip in general, then the system, the power delivery, the cooling, the software — we didn't even talk about the crazy compiler that they need to use to be able to run this stuff. And then it's like, let's also become a Neo cloud. Let's do the whole process of getting data center space, building out an operations team, fitting out the data center with power and cooling and running it themselves.

I mean, the fact that the 750 megawatt deal with OpenAI, that comes in three tranches of 250, is done on a token as a service basis, which implies that they're gonna have upside similar to Amazon Bedrock, where the economics of the deal have to work at the beginning for them to even go for it, right? But they're exposed to improvements in software over the life cycle of the deal. So five years from the time they signed the deal, if they're producing more tokens out of the same number of wafers on the same workload, they're gonna be making more money, which is exactly why we see other businesses that sell tokens like this.

I'm not sure. I think it's kind of fixed for just compute they deploy. But I think one of the big questions for Cerebrus is they have 750 megawatts with OpenAI. OpenAI has an option to upsize that deal to two gigawatts — that's more than double. And that's obviously like the next big chunk of upside. And I think the question is if they do it. And I think how well they can deliver tokens economically is going to be the big question as to whether OpenAI decides to exercise that.

So yeah. But overall, I mean, Cerebrus is a really impressive company. I don't think there's another hardware startup that has delivered as much innovation as they have, from silicon to hardware — they do some of their assembly in-house — and now they have an API endpoint setting up in NeoCloud. So it's actually just really — I mean, despite some of the trade-offs which we'll talk about, it's genuinely a very impressive success story from that.

Moonshot came through.

Moonshot came through, and now they're going public. Yeah. Like the wafers are here. You can use the tokens from the wafers. Like, we did not expect this in, what, 2018?

Yeah. Yeah. Back — yeah. It was a different world then. And they were completely focused on training.

And even before that, right? Like before — yeah, this is HPC.

Yeah. Yeah. We're going to do CFD. We're going to do like molecular and dynamic simulations and stuff really fast. Because it's just lots of CPU cores on there, right? I mean, it's not like they're doing specialized tensor cores with the level or low precision math or anything.

The Token: Roofline & Arithmetic Intensity

Let's talk about the token. So, you know, we just kind of glossed over a little bit of like the need for speed, where we said — you know, I'll start with this chart, the trade-off between running the bus a GPU at low interactivity.

A factory tour bus. A leather jacket for everybody on there.

And then, I think the guy — Feldman's Ferrari.

Feldman's Ferrari. Exactly. He needs to sponsor a team, man. He needs to sponsor an F1 team. That's what happens when you go public with a chip company. You get an F1 team as your prize. They have to sponsor the winner. Otherwise it's like Aston... they're really partnering with AMD. AMD is sponsoring Mercedes.

Oh, there we go. Yeah. So really important that the chip sponsors pick the winner, the fastest car. You can't be bringing up the rear with your Haas sponsorship next to the — you could do less than Haas.

Haas is doing okay. Yeah, this year. Yeah. Sorry. I'm going back too far to Mazepan. Anyway. Urikali — not Ukrainian, Russian. Okay.

Let's talk about fast tokens and what it means. So maybe first where the wafer wins and then second where it has some problems.

So the key thing that we tried to point out here is really the structure of the chip itself. And tried to introduce the concept of a roofline plot for people who aren't familiar. It's basically showing the ratio of compute performance measured in teraflops to arithmetic intensity of a given kernel, which — you know, it moves up linearly with the flops until it hits some limit in terms of how much memory bandwidth you have in the system.

And what's arithmetic intensity? Can you remind us?

Arithmetic intensity of a kernel — a kernel being a piece of software that runs on the device side, so runs on the GPU or runs on the TPU or in this case on the wafer — is, yeah, it's a way of describing how many flops versus how many bytes are being moved in the kernel. So how much bandwidth you need versus how much compute you need.

And these ratios are pretty well understood by performance engineers all the way back for HPC workloads, as we were just talking about, but obviously in AI workloads too. Because that like for loop that you're running on the device side has some — like the canonical example is matrix multiplication, a gem, it's got some shape. And if it's a nice big square, you know, the input matrices are like nice and big in terms of their dimensions, then you have very high arithmetic intensity because you always have to do a lot of flops on every byte that you move, right?

But a lot of machine learning is not this way. And it's not always even matrix math. A lot of it involves vectors. So matrix with a dimension of one. And what that means is that relative to the amount of data you move, you don't actually need to do that many flops per amount, per byte that you move onto the, into the registers on the chip.

And so if we add Cerebrus and Grox and really anybody else that is building an SRA machine to these plots, what you'll see for the viewers is that on the very far left side of the plot at very low arithmetic intensity, that optimal ratio of the workload shape that should run on the GPU or the TPU or the wafer scale engine best is actually really low arithmetic intensity.

And the reason to point this out is that basically what it means is that no matter how many GPUs you add to the theoretical system that has eight H100s in an NVLink system, or 72 GB200s in a GB200 NVL72 system, the amount of flops that you can realize on a very low arithmetic intensity kernel is not high. You hit some limit in terms of usually memory bandwidth, but at the system level, the network overhead and things like that.

And so the summary, Myron, that you said earlier is like SRA machines like Grok and Surrey Rift and many other chip startups out there — the concept of them is that they can do things that the others can't at any price. And that's specifically run these kernels with low arithmetic intensity.

So we add these and we try to explain like, you know, what the ratios actually are in terms of flops per byte. And we go through some example gem shapes for LLM inference, and the final chart in the article shows like the arithmetic intensity pointed out of some common gem shapes, where decode batch one — meaning a decode kernel, when the model produces output for a single user — is very low arithmetic intensity. And that's what Cerebrus is built for: really fast performance for relatively low amounts of users.

Where the Wafer Wins, and Where It Doesn't

Okay. So now that we've talked about like the good stuff — going really fast and how it works in terms of getting to the point where you have a thousand tokens per second or something on decode, the output portion — where does it not work?

And generally this is something along the lines of running really big models for a whole bunch of users at really high concurrency and playing really high batch size, which is the paradigm of LLM inference today — the non fast mode LLM inference.

Yeah, on screen we've got a screenshot where we show an estimation to say like the DeepSeqv4 model. We talked about how expensive these wafers are. Let's put a single WSC — like the whole system — as costing a million bucks, capex, right? Much more expensive than a $20,000 wafer because you've got to add in a whole bunch of other stuff, a whole bunch of other expenses after you just buy the wafer from CSMC or something, right?

And the result is — like you can run DeepSeq v4, this new model, on one of these really expensive systems: if you use 4-bit weight quantization and 16-bit KB cache for this model, and you want to support 256 concurrent users or concurrent requests at a time with an agentic coding workload at a full like million token context, you're going to need 24 systems. 24 million dollars of capex just to support 256 concurrent users, right? Which is like eight racks of cerebris in the data center, just to run one copy of the model. You probably want to run multiple copies of the model.

So a pretty significant upfront investment for somebody to commit to the wafer technology is like probably measured in the minimum price of 100 million dollars if you want to even start trying out how to run these really big models on these wafers. And with $100 million, you can get so many GB300 racks, right? You'll have tons more memory, you can have multiple copies and a lot more KB cache to support huge context.

It's a big bet to start the development process, right? Because the way that you start optimizing a model — and the way that you get that curve that we've tried to show improves over time with inference X — is by getting developers access to these systems. And if you need a team of developers working on how to optimize one of these models, and the buy-in is 25, 50, a hundred million dollars to get in, there's just a limited set of customers who are even gonna explore that.

And so we think there's a feasibility problem here, right? It's not that like technically there's no way possible for you to run a trillion parameter model at a million token context on a set of wafers and get really great performance — 1000 tokens per second or something per user. It's just that the upfront cost is going to be a lot, and then potentially the cost per million tokens is also going to be a lot, that you're going to need to pay for by having the users pay a significant premium.

The Proof in the Pudding: Who's Actually Buying

You guys got any more thoughts about this?

I mean, maybe the last thing to talk about in terms of the wafer is like the proof is in the pudding, right? Like who is buying this right now? Like the most clear public endpoint that we can go use right now is GPT 5.3 codec Spark. We know this is a 120 billion parameter model. It's the GPT OSS architecture, right?

I believe it's even smaller than that. It's like distilled from that.

Well, it's just the weights are different than the GPT OSS 120B. The architecture should still be 120 B, but it's distilled from the bigger 5.3 codex model to get the frontier coding performance at that speed that they're attempting for.

But the point is, they are not today — we've seen the success of Opus 4.6 fast mode, 4.7 fast — now, neither of these companies today are serving the frontier trillion plus parameter model at million token context on a wafer. So what's the limitation?

If you talk to Suriris, they may claim like they just can't produce enough for them. They can't plug them in — which fair enough, right? They probably can't produce enough to plug them in.

But demand is making everybody look good. And I think they will have a great business serving the small to medium model range at small to medium token context — 120 billion parameter models at 128K context. And that's a multi-billion dollar business per year for some of the biggest companies in the world as buyers. And they probably don't need to serve trillion parameter models at million token context to be able to do anything bigger than that.

But I think the storytelling has become a little bit weird looking at this where, you know, there's a lot of claims about making the absolute biggest models in the world go the fastest. It's hard to see that realized yet. We're still waiting on 1000 token per second Opus, or 1000 tokens per second GPT 5.5, like full base model, a million token context. We'd love to try it out.

Super-Linear Pricing & Fast Tokens as Insurance

We'd love to try. How much would you pay for it? How much would you pay for those tokens?

Me or my boss Dylan?

Well, who's paying for your tokens now?

I am currently paying zero for my tokens. So how much?

How much is Dylan willing to pay for those tokens?

I am maybe on the negative token spend.

Yeah. Dylan is currently paying six times more for fast tokens than he pays for normal tokens. And the measurement of fast tokens that we've seen — I'll try and find this one up here — puts the fast mode... zoom in on this. If you look at open router data, the standard mode, it depends on the provider that you're using, but let's say the throughput per user on Google Vertex, 45 tokens per second, and Amazon Bedrock is 38 tokens per second for the standard model, about 40. And then for the fast mode, the average over the week that we grabbed this data from for Opus 4.6 fast was 66 tokens per second. Like that's not even 2x for six times the price, right?

I think we got some questions about what super linear pricing looks like when you go 10 times faster. If you go 10 times faster, can you command 10 times the price of normal speed tokens?

I mean, we think Anthropic's margin is quite healthy on this 4.6 fast. We don't think that it costs them six times more to serve these tokens two times faster. But the claim from Jensen's slide when the Grok announcement was made with the AFD disaggregation at GTC this year was that 10 times or a hundred times faster tokens are going to be super linear. They're going to cost a hundred times or a thousand times more. And it's just a chart on the slide.

But, I mean, if you just compare SRAM to HBM, it's not six times more expensive per bit. It's more expensive per bit than that. And that's an oversimplification. Like if you're renting a wafer for 43 bucks an hour and you need 42 of them to run one copy of the model, you need high concurrency to make this stuff work, to get your selling price per million tokens below the price at which people will pay.

We're not going from paying like a blended $1 per million tokens when we consider all of the prefix cash hits, to having Dylan pay, you know, six bucks for fast mode. I guess like you can go from some developer spending a thousand bucks a day to spending 6,000 bucks a day, but you can't pay a hundred times more. You're not going from spending a thousand bucks a day...

Yeah. So this super-linearity argument is like — eventually the world's going to run out of money, and there'll be a value point where you might not be able... like no one is willing to pay 100 times more for 10 times faster. So there's going to be a limit somewhere.

But like when we first started using fast mode, we didn't... you know, there were capabilities and things you could do that we didn't realize we could, with agents and with things that's enabled from faster tokens. So who knows what is the application here, right? And if it ends up being some sort of arms race where whoever can iterate the fastest with these fast tokens systems, right — and then the value of all the opportunity cost of not doing fast tokens becomes significant, right — then it will be like a huge explosion in demand for this kind of stuff, right? But we have to see what the market does, yeah.

That's a nice business. That's really interesting. If your business is tied to being downside protection for a frontier lab to not miss the fast token boat, I think that's an interesting place to be — a machine to make sure you don't miss out.

Yeah. And that's why I'm so glad that, you know, Cerebras hasn't been bought out yet. Yeah. Plenty of demand on the IPO. I wonder what sort of offers they've been getting.

Closing Thoughts

Let's see. All right, guys, anything else you want to cover here? What has been left unsaid about Cerebras?

I think that's the main thing. But yeah, it's exciting times for them. I'm keen to see how the IPO flows out. They built really impressive technology. And I'm also looking forward to seeing what kind of capability that OpenAI can bring to the system, or what kind of models they'll serve on it. And also how much they're going to cost. Are they going to charge 100 times the price, and who's going to pay for it? That's exciting times.

We're about to see some extreme co-design out of OpenAI. Not just co-design, extreme co-design.

Howie, any closing thoughts?

Yeah. So for me, I'm really excited, you know, on the hardware side, this is something that I've been following for many years now, since 2018 regarding this wafer scale solution. And yeah, kind of pretty excited to see what they'll come up with, right?

Because now with this IPO, they finally have more engineering — we're able to get more engineering resources to really innovate much further. Because currently their iteration is quite slow in terms of like, it takes, you know, multiple years for them to get a new generation of wafer. You know, like we said, the next generation is still using the same wafer.

So in the longer term, I can't wait to see when they have more resources to develop. And then, you know, you're finally able to do some extreme co-design, right? Like have a certain version of Compute Engine for a certain company or whatever. Really excited, right? Especially the stuff with hybrid bonding, waveform, wafer, and some of the — TSMC has been like peddling this SOW. So this kind of technology is, yeah... I'm just really excited to see someone finally getting a good use out of it, and the HBM hegemony as a competitor now. So yeah, that's good.

I'm a hedgeman. Love it, man. Yeah. Good job.

All right. Well, thanks everybody for listening. I'm excited for the IPO. Hopefully this episode comes out before — let us know in the comments how much you're spending on fast tokens, how much would you be willing to spend for a hundred times faster tokens? We'll take a poll in the comments.

Awesome. Okay. Great job guys.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、晶圆的拓扑结构，正在变成公司的组织结构
最容易被忽略的一点：Cerebras 这块晶圆和 Cerebras 这家公司，是**同构的**。
过去四十年，半导体行业做的事情叫"拆解"——把芯片拆成 chiplet、把内存拆成 HBM、把连接拆成 interposer 和 NVLink、把机器拆成机架。Cerebras 对这条主线说"不"，它把一切重新焊回**一整片硅**上：84 个 reticle 不出晶圆、不过网络、数据永远留在 die 内。
而现在请看这家公司在做什么：自己设计硅、自己在加州工厂做专有组装、自己钻孔、自己建数据中心、自己开 API endpoint、自己当 NeoCloud。**它把行业花了四十年拆开的产业链，又重新捆回了一家公司里。** 产品的架构哲学和组织的架构哲学，是一模一样的"反拆解"。这是 Conway 定律的镜像版本——通常是组织结构决定产品结构，而 Cerebras 是产品的物理信仰倒灌进了商业模式。这也解释了它真正的风险：晶圆的好处是"一个东西全包了"，公司的风险也是"一个东西全包了"——硅、系统、供电、散热、编译器、数据中心运维、API

item.title: [YC Root Access] Circle CEO: 3 Things That Will Transform Stablecoins in 2027
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最让人意外的观察：稳定币不是"金融创新"，而是一次"互联网协议层的补完"

大多数人把稳定币当成金融科技产品来讨论，但 Jeremy 在访谈里其实悄悄揭示了一个更激进的框架：USDC 的原始设计目标不是"更好的支付工具"，而是"HTTP for money"——补齐互联网当年缺失的那一层基础设施协议。

这意味着稳定币的真正参照系不是 PayPal、Visa 或 SWIFT，而是 TCP/IP、HTTP、SMTP。这种重构有一个隐藏的推论：协议层一旦确立，应用层的爆发速度通常比所有人预测的都要快一个数量级。HTTP 在 1991 年定型，到 1998 年雅虎、亚马逊、eBay 已经定义了整个互联网经济。如果 USDC 类资产真正在 2025–2026 取得"联邦统一认证"的协议地位（CFTC 把它当作合格抵押品就是信号），那么我们离"稳定币原生杀手级应用"的爆发，可能只剩 24–36 个月。

二、被忽略的跨领域连接：稳定币 × AI Agent = "经济操作系统"的诞生

Jeremy 谈 agentic payment 时，刻意避开"commerce"一词，而用了"agentic economic activity"。这个用词替换值得放大解读——他实际上在偷偷宣告一件事：劳动与资本的关系正在被 AI 重写，而稳定币是这场重写中唯一"足够低延迟、足够可编程"的结算介质。

让我做一个出人意料的跨领域连接：

  • 生物学视角：单细胞生物之间需要 ATP 作为通用能量货币才能完成代谢分工；多细胞生命的复杂度由 ATP 的"瞬时可交换性"决定。
  • AI 经济视角：Agent 之间需要一种"瞬时可结算、可编程、可附带合约"的能量货币，才能涌现出超越人类组织复杂度的协作形态。法币做不到（T+1、T+3 太慢），加密原生资产价格波动太大，只有稳定币恰好同时具备 ATP 的三个属性：通用性、瞬时性、可计量性。

换句话说，Jeremy 描述的不是"AI 用稳定币付款"，而是"AI 经济体的新陈代谢系统正在被铺设"。这也解释了他为什么强调保险市场会成为下一个爆点——因为任何高频代谢系统都必须有"风险耗散机制"，正如生物体必须有免疫系统。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：监管不是阻力，而是"反向放大器"

普通人看监管，会觉得这是稳定币的天花板；但 Jeremy 透露的 GENIUS Act 的"互惠条款（reciprocity provision）"暗藏一个出乎意料的博弈结构：

谁的国内立法越接近 GENIUS 框架，谁的本币稳定币就越能进入美国流通。

这不是技术外交，而是金融领域的"安卓系统效应"——美国不再需要派遣美元出去做势力扩张，而是把"规则模板"开源出去，让各国自愿对齐。其结果是：

  • G20 国家会被迫在 2026–2028 内完成本土稳定币立法，否则将被排除在"美元互联网"之外；
  • 新兴市场（拉美、东南亚）会出现一个反直觉现象——它们的稳定币监管可能比欧洲更先进，因为它们没有遗留货币主权的政治负担；
  • 真正的赢家或许不是 Circle 或 Tether，而是那些搭建"跨稳定币互操作层"的初创公司——类似 90 年代不是浏览器赢了，而是 DNS、CDN、SSL 的中间层吞下了价值。

一个让人意外的预测：未来三年最值钱的 fintech 不会是某个 stablecoin 发行方，而是某个"stablecoin 间路由 + 司法管辖识别"的协议。这就是为什么 CPN（Circle Payments Network）今天才 55 家机构却如此关键——它在悄悄占位"稳定币时代的 BGP 路由协议"。

一句话总结打破常规的视角：稳定币的真正意义不在 fintech 3.0，而在于它同时是"互联网缺失的协议层"+ "AI 经济的 ATP"+ "美国规则的安卓化分发机制"——三件事在同一时间窗口共振，这就是为什么 YC 此刻 all-in 的真正逻辑。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Hi everyone. I'm super excited to have Jeremy Allaire, the chairman, CEO, and co-founder of Circle. Circle is a stablecoin issuer and the issuer of USDC, a nearly $80 billion stablecoin. He's a serial entrepreneur, and he's also someone that I've been excited to collaborate with over the years.

The reason he's here is YC is insanely excited about stablecoins and fintech 3.0. Stablecoins have grown from $0 about a decade ago to nearly $300 billion today. We see everyone from shopkeepers in Argentina to big Wall Street banks in New York using stablecoins. And we see builders from developers in San Francisco to policy makers on Capitol Hill paving the way for stablecoins to be used.

YC has a request for startups out on stablecoins. We're super excited to fund and support stablecoin startups. And we recently announced that we're offering schemes the ability to receive their funding in USDC. Really excited to have you here today, Jeremy.

Jeremy: Thank you. It's really awesome to be here.

早年相识与 Circle 的起源

I first met you actually in 2016. I don't think I've shared this — in 2016 at the MIT Bitcoin Conference.

Jeremy: Oh, okay. Yeah. I met a lot of people there and it was a bunch of nerds, I think, on a Saturday morning in a winter in Cambridge.

Totally. But I think what's really inspiring for me was just seeing your founder journey from the early ideas for Circle to where you ended up with stablecoins. And I think a lot of people don't know that you're a serial entrepreneur, have taken a company public before, were head of product at Macromedia for a while. Just really excited to hear a little bit about the Circle founding journey — especially as it relates to other founders who are starting their own idea.

Jeremy: Yeah. Very happy to share that. I think a couple things. The first is, I got very involved in the early internet infrastructure back in like 1990, so before the web. I became very interested in open networks, open software, distributed computing models — kind of the early DNA of the internet got me really excited because it was a new paradigm for how computing could work.

I spent really a couple decades building internet infrastructure software — lots of different stuff: programming languages, virtual machines, client software, server software, content, media, communications. A lot of the stuff that was going on those first couple decades. Always interested in how can we apply that kind of DNA of the internet to solve broad kind of problems in society. And that kind of led me to my work in crypto.

But along the way I had co-founded and taken two companies public prior to Circle. In 2012 I became really enamored with crypto and Bitcoin as a technology at the time. But I was also interested, from prior experience and interests, in understanding how the global financial system worked — or didn't work — after the great financial crisis.

I had originally had a background in international political economy. That was my academic background. So I found, after the great financial crisis, I just became obsessed with: what is the nature of money? How does the international monetary system work? What is central banking? What is fractional reserve banking? Like, what is all this? And what went wrong? And is there a better way?

Yeah.

Jeremy: I didn't know anything about crypto because there was no crypto at the time. I mean, I guess there was Satoshi's white paper and stuff, but I was not aware of it until later.

And then, really crystallizing the founding of Circle — there was this long-standing interest in how could the international monetary system evolve with what I saw with Bitcoin, which was sort of the kernel of a new technology infrastructure layer of the internet, like a missing infrastructure layer of the internet. As someone who'd worked on infrastructure layers, it was very apparent to me: this was a computer science breakthrough.

At the time, people were writing papers about smart contracts or extensibility of blockchain networks. I looked at that as a technologist and said: wow, this is going to allow for a whole new layer on the internet — and it's going to be a layer where we're going to be able to build protocols for dollars on the internet. We're going to be able to build basically self-running machines running software that are programming and intermediating economic activity on top of those protocols for dollars on the internet.

I had this big 10–20 year vision of: you could actually rebuild not just the financial system but the broader economic system on the internet natively. And that's what motivated the founding of Circle.

"HTTP for Money" 的最初构想

Jeremy: The core idea of what we think of today as USDC was the founding idea — an HTTP for money. How can you build a protocol for dollars on the internet?

Given the grounding in monetary philosophy, the belief was: this needs to be full reserve money, not fractional reserve money. It needs to be very, very safe. That's the right base layer. And the asset itself, the way it's expressed, is more like an internet protocol where anyone can connect to it, anyone can build on it.

None of that was possible in 2013. We developed ways to express that idea on top of the Bitcoin network, which was very problematic. And then obviously when Ethereum came out, it was like: okay, we have the building blocks, we can actually execute this. We started work on that in 2017 and then got together with Coinbase in 2018 and got this really off the ground as well.

I think one thing I don't think people realize today is how controversial the idea was of dollars versus Bitcoin.

Jeremy: Yeah. Well, I got a lot of hate for a very long time. We talked about hybrid models — where we can connect the regulated money system to these public computing infrastructures, to these public networks, and that we can have both the technological benefits and utility of this, and even permissionless access to use it and develop on it.

That was controversial too. When I was sort of out there saying "we're going to do this USDC and we're going to do this thing that's on public networks," everyone said no one will let you do that. The governments are going to shut it down — because it was pretty radical even that. But yeah, I was booed out of a lot of rooms because I was not a Bitcoin maxi.

当下最令人兴奋的用例

I'm curious — for builders, what are the types of use cases that you're seeing that have been really compelling? You mentioned people just using it day-to-day in different parts of the world. What are the ones you're most excited about?

Jeremy: We are seeing so many builders working on what I'll broadly call neobanking type of products. There was a period where everyone was like, "I'm going to build the consumer wallet with stablecoin native, with a Visa card and that bundle." And by the way, that continues to proliferate — there's tons of those in lots of countries, lots of places.

But now we're starting to see more and more builders who are saying: actually, I want to build this for businesses. This is really valuable for businesses and organizations. So it's essentially abstracting away the features that are needed for treasury management and payment flows more broadly. We're seeing a lot of that — and that's also from big companies too, like Stripe or Ramp or lots of others.

There's been a big crop of companies, some of whom have matured and been bought and sold, that have sort of said: hey, this is the ideal settlement infrastructure for cross-border settlements. That continues unabated. We continue to see so many companies and so many regions and markets building solutions around that.

It's also interesting to see more and more platforms that are involved in capital formation using stablecoins and wiring stablecoins in. Y Combinator is a great example.

Agentic Payments：新的爆发点

Jeremy: Recently — we started work last year on agentic payments with USDC. We were an early contributor at x402 as well. I've always thought machine-intermediated would be the future. That's gone into overdrive now.

Just in the last three or four months, it's just been like whoosh. Tons of projects now are using our infrastructure alongside various types of products and services for agents. To me, that's one of the most exciting things happening in the world. So that's a major new area for builders.

可编程的"忠诚度协议"

Jeremy: From a startup perspective, those are some of the themes. Programmability of money is a new concept for a lot of people — abstracting away higher-order protocols or services that take advantage of that.

For example, we've seen startups that are basically working on rewards protocols that go alongside stablecoins. You want to be able to have mechanisms for businesses that accept payments with stablecoins to also have fungible, redeemable, but also bespoke forms of loyalty systems. That's a replacement for card loyalty systems — something that you can only really do with that programmable surface.

I think both Deel and Gusto have announced stablecoin payouts. Payouts are probably one of the biggest use cases we're seeing.

Jeremy: Yes. Absolutely. I view that mostly as a cross-border example. But it's not just cross-border, because it works for people. I could be an American business paying out to an American end user as well.

消费者驱动还是企业驱动？

Do you feel like this first phase is more consumer? Or is it more like — we heard this term "stablecoin sandwich" where basically stablecoins are behind the scenes but on both sides it's fiat. For the next year or two, are you seeing more in one or both?

Jeremy: I mean, both are really growing. That reflects a couple facts. One is, especially in emerging and developing markets, the businesses that want to use this — in many cases they do want to hold working capital in these digital dollars. They don't necessarily want to go to their local currency. They may need the ability to go to their local currency because they may have payment obligations in their local currency, but the store-of-value component of it is very real. And I think that's the case for individuals as well.

We've seen with CPN (Circle Payments Network) — that's almost all B2B and it's almost all international, and it's amazing. We've seen rapid growth in the number of [institutions]. We basically created a network model. As of last quarter, we had like 55 financial institutions on the network, with a lot more coming on. They're kind of wiring it up and they're able to go from fiat to stablecoin to fiat, or stablecoin — they can go across these options.

It's like memes around the world. It's south to south, it's north to south, all these different models. And businesses are just figuring out that this is a better, faster, cheaper way to do things.

On the end-user side: very clearly, what I sort of say "households" or people are realizing — I can hold dollars, I can hold digital dollars, I can get passive yield on them, I can transact with them, and I have interoperability to a local payment method as well.

机构拥抱：从消费端到资本市场

One thing that seems really different from years in stablecoins has been — for a lot of the early stablecoin journey it was a mass movement, a lot of consumers were using it around the world. What's really changed maybe in the last year, especially in the US, has been the institutional embrace of stablecoins. Beyond payouts, are there other sets of use cases?

Jeremy: Yeah, absolutely. We've spent years trying to get regulatory classifications so that something like USDC is treated as cash-equivalent money in the US financial system. We're now there — it's happening.

What's important about that is that once you get that federal treatment, and once you get the SEC, the CFTC, and the bank regulators to say "this is, from a federal statutory level, what this is, and therefore from a prudential risk perspective, this is how we'll treat this and how you can use this" — that's enabling all these participants in capital markets and in banking to actually say: okay, we can now start to use this as an infrastructure. They'll look at it as an infrastructure that is about speed, efficiency, and interoperability.

CFTC、衍生品与抵押品

Jeremy: We're now seeing, for example, the CFTC, which oversees all the derivatives markets in the United States, authorizing market participants — the derivatives exchanges and the market participants that sit on those — to actually use USDC as eligible collateral.

What's interesting ironically is that USDC's eligible collateral on Coinbase or Binance for different things in the crypto world — of course, because that's the only thing that's available. But doing that for oil futures on a classic derivatives exchange is really powerful.

全球系统重要性银行（GSIB）的内部应用

Jeremy: We're seeing global systemically important banks who are using this as a way to do internal global treasury management — their own capital movement across their international divisions or branches using USDC. Which is wild. That's like plumbing of capital movement in a global bank.

We're seeing more and more traditional asset managers that are creating digital asset products, tokenization products, where essentially USDC is the cash layer for how you create and redeem all these different types of products.

We're seeing global systemically important banks basically realizing that they can do intraday FX across major currencies and major boundaries using stablecoins in a way that they can't with the existing currency system and currency settlement system. Risk moves continuously. So the ability to collapse the time between what's happening in a market and ultimate settlement is —

Yeah, like the T+3 to instantaneous —

Jeremy: Or even next-day. We can do continuous stuff. For some people that's like, "I don't know what that is." But if you actually look at it, these are really critical pieces for how capital moves, how risk is managed, how markets are established. These are all beginning to happen, but with the regulatory clarity, there's the potential for this to deeply penetrate all these things.

That's why I think when people think about what's the TAM for stablecoins — there's the money supply TAM, which is many trillions of dollars. But then there's all of the utilities that use that money. All of these are big use cases that are turning over and using this, and those obviously are in scope as well.

国际格局：从 G20 到 GENIUS Act 的互惠条款

So you alluded to just the regulations and how much of a sea change that is in the US. I'm curious how you see that internationally — at YC we have companies in Latin America, Asia, the Middle East that are all excited to build on stablecoins. Do you see other countries as advanced as we've come in the last few years in the US? What do you think needs to happen?

Jeremy: It's really interesting. Most people don't realize, but about five years ago, the G20, through the financial regulatory body the Financial Stability Board, sort of said: hey, we need policies on stablecoins because they're going to happen. They came up with a set of recommendations and the G20 adopted it. So all the presidents and whatnot signed and said: we're going to adopt this.

What that basically said is: here's a set of policies that we think G20 members should adopt. And then it goes down to the actual parliaments and governments and congresses, and how laws were made. Governments started doing it. G20 members started doing it. Japan was the very first — Japan had stablecoin regulations years ago. Then Europe was next — Europe passed the stablecoin regulations. Then you had Singapore, Hong Kong, UK, UAE — all basically drafting, doing development on this.

And then finally the US. The US was actually late to this. You may remember, essentially the heads of all the financial apparatus in the US — the President's Working Group on Financial Markets — basically said, here's our recommendations for stablecoins. That was in 2021. That was five years ago, and it took almost five years to get a federal law passed that did this. But it just takes years to make laws.

So we got to that point here in the United States. In some parts of the world they had already put a pole in the ground saying "this is what this is." Now we have the US come in — but because dollars dominate this, it's kind of forcing the rest of the world to revisit all of that.

灰色地带与 GENIUS 的互惠机制

Jeremy: What we're seeing happen now is not just G20 countries but huge numbers of other countries in emerging and developing markets all around the world working on rules on stablecoins.

One thing to note is that there's gray area. In many places it's gray area — it's not defined, it's not clearly regulated. It might be regulated as "these are basically like other digital assets" and you need to have VASPs or intermediaries that deal with this and have money-laundering obligations and the like.

But in other cases they're saying: no, we need to actually have laws for what are permitted. Like in Brazil, what's a permitted stablecoin in Brazil? What's a permitted international stablecoin? Are they going to define that on the basis of the GENIUS Act and say things that are defined here —

There's a strong incentive because of GENIUS to do that, because GENIUS has a reciprocity provision in it. If a regime basically adopts a framework that is substantially similar to GENIUS, then currencies issued under that regime could be recognized in the US as well.

So really, the next two to three years, you're going to see a huge number of laws passed around the world on stablecoins in local markets. You're going to see efforts from the top — coordinated G20-type stuff — as well as key individual markets to create uniformity (might be too strong of a word), but basically various forms of interoperability, reciprocity, etc. so that this can work in a fairly consistent way. It's big moves happening internationally right now.

Are there particular countries you're super bullish on at least in the next year?

Jeremy: Yeah. The places where we've seen concentration have been things like Southeast Asia, including Hong Kong — huge amounts of activity there. Latin America — huge amounts of activity. The number of startups we see in Latin American countries that are building new products around this — it's got to be in the hundreds.

AI Agent 与稳定币：经济操作系统的诞生

We've spent a lot of time talking about largely the existing financial systems and how stablecoins can make that better. We're in San Francisco and there's tremendous excitement about AI, and you mentioned agentic commerce earlier. I'm curious about the types of use cases you're seeing in these early years. Is it micro-payments that you see as the initial killer use case for stablecoins? Are there other things you're seeing that would be interesting for builders?

Jeremy: Yeah, so I'm super excited about it. Our view — or my view I should say — is a couple things. We're in a period of very significant technical and economic disruption. My own view is that we're going to see a huge explosion in the number of AI agents that are both conducting work and consuming the work of other AI agents. An agentic economic system is needed. New economic OSes, blockchains, etc. are needed to support that — the requirements are really different.

From a use case perspective, a lot of people when they hear "agentic commerce" think "my agent is going to shop for me." Okay, that's a looking-backward view. My view is that basically labor and capital — the relationship of labor and capital — are changing radically. We now have work conducted by these intelligent agents, and decomposition of work into agents, and agents as a service. More and more discrete things are going to be consumable as intelligence and work at an agent level.

In that environment, you're effectively going to need to have contracts and economic execution amongst potentially billions or even tens of billions of agents that are conducting work. That to me is what agentic — I don't like to use the word "commerce." I like to say "agentic economic activity" because it's broader.

不只是支付，而是经济活动的实质

Jeremy: It also reflects the fact that in such systems, part of this is going to be empowering agents to coordinate work, to have proof of work, to have ways to store and manage capital, to make decisions together, to provide governance, to have human-in-loop governance. All this stuff is what the agentic economic system needs to look like.

So to me that's not just about payments. It's not just about money movement. It's about the substance of how economic activity is organized. That's a huge opportunity.

My high-level view, stepping back, is: even if you just look at this from a payments perspective, the velocity of economic activity because of AI agents is going to explode. That economic activity is going to be a huge increase in the velocity of money and the use of money, and the only viable way to manage that is with stablecoins and blockchains.

We're designing a ton of stuff at an infrastructure level to be ready for that, enable that, etc.

缺失的基础设施：身份、争议、保险

And last question on that one — what infrastructure components do you think are missing? Identity is I think one example, maybe chargebacks and things like that might be other examples. What of those pieces do you think really have to lead to unlock that economy?

Jeremy: Look, clearly know-your-agent, identity proofs is really critical. Mechanisms for disputes and failed transactions — other things like that — are very important. There's some really interesting things that could be done there in terms of dispute resolution conducted by AI agents themselves. That's a really interesting space.

What that actually also calls for — what I believe will be needed — are insurance markets. Insurance markets are going to be critical. You're going to want to have insurance against all kinds of stuff that's happening in the agentic economy. Not just "a payment settled or not" or "there was fraud" — but insurance against a number of different types of outcomes. So I think insurance markets are ripe and tie to agentic economic activity as well.

收官：三件值得期待的事

Last question. Three things you're excited about for the next year that will really transform the stablecoin industry — what are those?

Jeremy: One is one we've been talking about, which is just agentic. I'm very, very excited about that — and part of it is the unknown. We know this is going to be significant, and the unknown of everything that's going to happen because it moves so fast. That's totally something I'm really excited about.

Two — I think we're going to see product user experiences that really make this beautiful and simple and seamless, so it doesn't feel like a crypto app. I think we're going to have the breakthrough UX experiences that happen, and we're going to see a ton of those — but we're going to see some that get liftoff, that really start to get PMF and growth.

Three — this is more mundane but I think is really exciting — we're going to see stablecoins become essentially approved and usable in the core guts of the financial system. That is kind of like reaching the summit. Like: okay, we've made it. This is now considered money in the most important infrastructure in the world. So I think that's pretty amazing too.

Cool. Well, Jeremy, it's been so exciting to be on the journey with you. I think we first met when USDC was maybe less than $100 million in market cap.

Jeremy: Yes.

Really excited to see what's ahead for USDC and the entire stablecoin industry. Thank you so much.

Jeremy: Thank you. Great.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最让人意外的观察：稳定币不是"金融创新"，而是一次"互联网协议层的补完"
大多数人把稳定币当成金融科技产品来讨论，但 Jeremy 在访谈里其实悄悄揭示了一个更激进的框架：**USDC 的原始设计目标不是"更好的支付工具"，而是"HTTP for money"——补齐互联网当年缺失的那一层基础设施协议**。
这意味着稳定币的真正参照系不是 PayPal、Visa 或 SWIFT，而是 TCP/IP、HTTP、SMTP。这种重构有一个隐藏的推论：**协议层一旦确立，应用层的爆发速度通常比所有人预测的都要快一个数量级**。HTTP 在 1991 年定型，到 1998 年雅虎、亚马逊、eBay 已经定义了整个互联网经济。如果 USDC 类资产真正在 2025–2026 取得"联邦统一认证"的协议地位（CFTC 把它当作合格抵押品就是信号），那么我们离"稳定币原生杀手级应用"的爆发，可能只剩 24–36 个月。
## 二、被忽略的跨领域连接：稳定币 × AI Agent = "经济操作系统"的诞生
Jeremy 谈 agentic payment 时，刻意避开"commerc

item.title: [YC Root Access] Meet The DevTool Founders Building For AI Agents
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"代码不重要"是这场访谈里最大的谎言——而且每个人都在用行动拆穿它

所有创始人嘴上说 "code doesn't matter anymore"，但仔细听，他们几乎全都悄悄回到了亲手写代码的状态。有人维护内部工具是为了"能可信地谈论产品该怎么改"，有人说"像很多创始人一样，不得不亲自回来写代码，just to rebase my assumptions"。注意那个词——rebase。他们不是在拿 git 术语开玩笑，而是在描述一件严肃的事：人类正在把自己当成一个需要定期重新校准的传感器。代码从"产出物"降格成了"感觉器官"。这很像影像诊断普及之后，资深医生反而更坚持亲手触诊——不是因为触诊更高效，而是因为不亲手摸，临床直觉会悄悄漂移而你毫无察觉。真正稀缺的不是写代码的能力，而是"知道此刻模型能做到什么"的体感，而这个体感的半衰期只有几周。

二、Conway 定律正在反向运行——公司在把自己改造成一个 API

经典的 Conway 定律说：软件结构会镜像组织的沟通结构。但访谈里发生的恰恰是反过来的事。一个创始人说他们"一个接一个地在内部造 agent——marketing、sales、infrastructure、DevOps"；另一个说要"把一切都变成 CLI、让数据更可导出"；还有人把文档重写到"能轻松塞进 coding agent 的 context window"。组织架构图正在变成一张工具注册表（tool registry）。这里有个少有人点破的系统真相：整个行业现在都围绕"上下文窗口"这一稀缺资源在重新布局——文档为它瘦身、产品为它做 CLI、公司为它拆成一个个 agent。Context window 是新的"土地"，所有人都在抢这块地的临街面。

三、他们正在退守的"护城河"，恰恰是 agent 下一个要淹没的高地

这是最让人意外、也最少人愿意承认的一点。创始人们反复说，未来人类的价值在于 context、judgment、taste、deeply understanding problems、relationships。但同一段转录里，另一位创始人兴奋地讲：他把客服工单和近期 PR 喂给 agent，让它"critique my plan"，结果 agent 的判断力"remarkably good"，能精准指出"第 4 点根本说不通"。也就是说——他们撤退到的那块"判断力"高地，水已经漫到脚边了。这在结构上和神学里的"缝隙之神"（god of the gaps）一模一样：人类不断被定义为"机器还做不到的那部分"，而那部分一直在缩小。诚实的人其实已经说出口了——"you just cannot predict that far out"、"get used to change"。他们描述的不是护城河，是一条不断后退的海岸线。

四、新的"分销渠道"是模型的权重，新的 SEO 是"被推荐"

Recall 的创始人讲了一件连他自己都震惊的事：他们发现"所有 coding model 都在不停推荐 Recall"——客户是被 AI 介绍来的。把这句话和另一位说的"如果你被 Claude、Gemini、ChatGPT 推荐，你就处境极好；如果没有，就很难脱颖而出"放在一起，一个跨领域类比就浮现了：营销正在经历"口碑 → 搜索引擎 → 模型心智"的演化。你的产品需要存在于模型的潜在空间里，需要成为某种"正典"（canon）——这更像学术引用，而非广告投放。再往前一步，已经有人在设想"agent 自己注册、自己付费、根本不需要告诉人类"。那意味着一个 agent 推荐给 agent、agent 卖给 agent 的闭环经济正在成形——结构上酷似高频交易市场：机器之间高速成交，人类只负责设定参数、偶尔充当"熔断器"。访谈里那个略带自嘲的词 "idea guy programming"，其实精确描述了人类在这个市场里的最终岗位：口味的提供者。

附赠：一个被一笔带过、却最锋利的观察

最值得停下来的一句话是："要有勇气无情地删掉不再有用的东西——这块肌肉要更早练。" 当产品的半衰期变成几个月，软件第一次拥有了新陈代谢，而不只是架构。这和生物学里的细胞凋亡（apoptosis）是同一个道理：健康的有机体必须主动杀死自己的一部分。过去我们夸一个工程师"能写"，未来也许要先看他"舍不舍得删"。

📝 转录文本

Meet The DevTool Founders Building For AI Agents

YC Root Access — https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RMmEB9ajmdo

Cold Open

Code doesn't matter anymore. What's going to matter and what's left is context, [music] is understanding problems deeply, building relationships with customers.

If you're not accustomed to change, you should get used to change.

The AI agents have taken so much toil out of building software that now we can get a lot better stuff out a lot faster, a lot higher quality. It's going to make uh engineers more valuable, not less valuable.

[music] >>

The Companies

Infracloud is an open-source identity security platform. We're working with companies like Nvidia, LG, Hugging Face to help them manage their secrets, certificates, and other sensitive resources across infrastructure. We make it easy to get up and running with agents and assistants using open models.

Resend is the email API for developers. Uh you can think of it as the Stripe of email.

Recall that AI is the API for conversation data. We help developers record, store, and understand data from video conferences, phone calls, and in-person meetings.

Griptape is building agents that review and test pull requests with full context of the code base. We do this for companies like Nvidia and Coinbase where we review more than 3 billion lines of code a month and flag anti-patterns and bugs.

Firecrawl is a web data API for AI agents and developers.

Porter is a platform that manages compute for startups in any cloud provider.

We create AI-powered intelligent documentation for enterprises and startups.

We provide like local LLMs and we, you know, make it much easier for people to train models.

We make in-app subscriptions easy, help developers make more money.

Who Is Your Audience — Developers or Agents?

Our main audience today is developers using coding agents. The main audience is developers, but increasingly more so it's becoming agents.

Developers. Uh we definitely tailor to agents and we try to create the best experience as possible for them. But we see agents as distribution and developers as the users. The customer is usually agents. So we spend a lot of time building interfaces for agents, for instance, CLIs and skills and so on. We think of our audience as anyone who wants to run high-scale infrastructure. So, that could be developers and increasingly it's also agents.

[music] >>

How Agents Changed Our Product and Company

At time, like even for example with Open Cloud in physical server like becoming the infrastructure layer for Open Cloud to consume different credentials and communicate with each other. Oh, it changed completely. From an organization standpoint, uh, we just started to create one Open Cloud agent after the other internally. So, we started with a marketing one, then we added a sales one, then we have one for infrastructure, one for DevOps. Uh, so it really influenced both our product and our company. We're definitely making all of our products more accessible to agents, which means making everything into a CLI, making data more exportable so that you can use it with Cloud Code.

It makes our output much faster. Previously, you would do like one feature, but now you can do like 10 features all in parallel in one go.

What I'd Do Differently If Starting Today

If I were to start the company today, this is something that we've already done, but I would just do it earlier, uh, which is consider agent experience as a first-class, uh, part of the interface.

One thing we do differently is redesign the whole product experience so it's accessible to agents. We're working on that right now. Whether that's Alomos documentation or it's other capabilities that we offer to humans and developers, uh, making sure that they're immediately accessible to coding agents as well.

If I were to start the company today, probably get much more comfortable with deleting things that we know are not going to be useful after some period of time. I think emotionally detaching oneself from the product because you know that you'll build something today and it'll be useful for a couple of months and then something drastic will change about the world and that part of the product will be useless. I think having the courage to ruthlessly delete that which is no longer useful it would become increasingly very important. I would build that muscle earlier.

Yeah, I think we'd maybe just go all in on some of the agentic infrastructure, things like sandboxes. We care about it and are doing those things now, but there is definitely an advantage to having that a part of your core DNA from the very beginning.

The biggest difference will be to use agents more, um to use code actually use code code more.

I would be 100 times more ambitious. The capabilities that models give you today make the impossible possible. And not only that, they make it table stakes. All of a sudden, because the bar has been raised, you have to do whatever you can to get there.

Oh boy. Nothing.

[laughter] >>

Mistakes We Made and What Developer Experience Really Means

I think anytime we started to drift away from if not the like exact persona of developers, but the spirit of developers, right? Of getting something done quickly, of being very direct, of being very clear with your pricing. All of these things that developers value in an experience is actually just good user experience. And you know, through the various iterations of say, you know, go to market and things like this, we've we've sometimes deviated away from that. And I think, you know, with maybe some small exceptions, those have been mistakes.

I think another mistake we made was that at the beginning we were extremely opinionated about how our APIs and how our our tools should be used. And sometimes you try to make it a little bit more generic and that creates even more conflict. If you're an authority in the field, you should be very opinionated.

I think in the early days, our understanding of developer experience was a lot about, hey, we got to have the perfect UI and the perfect design, which is not actually what developer experience is. And right now, you know, a a lot of what we think developer experience is is how fast can they get to the right, you know, aha moments and the the functionality that they need to use as part of their environments. And so, we're much more optimized for the speed and efficiency and and also how clear it is to use in physical.

For a long time, we didn't trust our own instincts strongly enough. And that was partly because as new college graduates that hadn't spent a lot of time in large companies, while we had, in some regards, the same job title as many of our users, which were developers, we sort of saw that we weren't really like them. They were working in these large companies, and their day-to-day was very different. Their way of approaching these problems is different. And I think we went a little too far in that direction, where we leaned far too heavily on understanding our users, which was important, but not trusting our own instincts for what a good product looks like. When we started to realize this, we realized, "Wow, there's a lot of aspects of our product that have become sort of full of crust, and if we had just trusted our instincts more strongly, we would have ended up with a product that was just more pleasurable to use." Because there's, of course, a level of understanding you can get from using the product yourself and trusting your own judgment on the product that you just can't glean as easily from talking to a person.

Rethinking Documentation for the Context Window

One of the things that we did early on, which we've actually changed very recently, is the way we think about documentation. In the old world of documentation, uh you would have a lot of exposition. Uh you'd have it spread across multiple pages. Um you'd have embedded tutorials. Um but with agents, one thing that we've been really conscious of is managing the context window. Now, we've redesigned a large portion of our docs to be able to fit inside a coding agent's context window very easily. Uh so, when the agent is working through the integration process with the API, uh it gets much higher accuracy, and it makes much fewer mistakes.

Do You Write Code Today?

No, I don't write code. I write prompts for humans and agents. No, our agents do.

[laughter] >>

That's the right answer.

Oh, yeah. Every single day I push a PR, for sure.

Do I write code today? Um good question. Every day. Most of it's coded by uh a coding agent. I I basically review the code that's uh being made by coding agents, and I get to spend a lot more time with the model builders themselves, the hardware companies, and developers building applications.

I don't write production code anymore, but I write code for fun, and I found I'm writing even more code than just a couple months ago because the coding agents have taken all the toil out of programming.

The biggest outlook now is that even managers or engineering managers that used to have the manager scale and they couldn't make themselves be a bottleneck for releasing new features, now have the ability to do the people management and the meetings, but also write production code.

And I think that's going to be the expectation. Yeah. Going forward.

There was a period of time in the middle when I stopped writing code, especially when we were really focused on go-to-market. But as we've developed a go-to-market function with really great leaders, I've been able to spend a lot more of my time writing code. And I'm not contributing too much to the main codebase, but there is a small number of internal tools that I maintain. And the main reason I do so is so that I'm essentially constantly using the product, at least a couple of times a day, and constantly using the new features that we're developing, and I can sort of credibly and legibly talk about what needs to change in the product and what's working well.

So I feel like we still write code, but the total amount of code is much more, and the percentage of the total amount of code is from Claude and Codex. You know, it's like 90% and then self-written code is like 10%. Um so it's not like we're not writing less code, it's just that the total amount of code is more.

Yeah, it's funny. I I stopped writing code for a bit and have now recently, I think like many founders, had to come back to writing code personally just to kind of rebase my assumptions about where the technology is. You hear so much, you see so much, especially online, but you kind of have to use the tools to build an intuition for it, and so literally in the last few weeks I have been heavily, heavily trying, you know, everything that's out there.

No. I use Claude code for everything.

The Unexpected Things Agents Do

The one really unexpected thing for us that agents do for us, uh is they bring customers to us. We looked at the ways customers find out about Recall, and we discovered that all the coding models recommend Recall all the time, and this was super surprising to us.

I was very surprised that given enough context, agents have remarkably good judgment on what we should work on. What I did do early on was give them access to, for instance, some number of customer support tickets, some amount of data on what pull requests you'd opened recently, and then have them critique my plan for what I think we should do over the next few weeks. And uh it was remarkable that they had very clear judgment. I think part of that has to do with the fact that they just have far larger context windows than I have, and they can read, for instance, thousands of support tickets over the last 90 days, and understand deeply what each person in the team is shipping, and also see the world very clearly, and be able to look at my set of ideas that I put forward and say, "Number four just doesn't make a lot of sense based on where we're seeing things go, and wow, there's this thing that a lot of users are asking for, and you're just not sort of pricing that into your plan for the next few weeks." I was very surprised at how good they are at critiquing our plans.

One unexpected thing would be So, we actually are doing a lot of internal tooling with agents. Um one of the tools that we have is this very custom vulnerability detection agent that's able to find and detect any risks or vulnerabilities inside our code before they get merged into production. It's much more uh capable than everything that's been done before, and it's also very very uh custom to our code base, so it works really great.

This one surprised me. It wasn't um wasn't my use case, but earlier uh this year we learned that um there are countries without internet that are getting open models delivered on computers to them, and they're doing this because they don't have access to all this information. And that was kind of unexpected that uh a model that was trained using a lot of data from the internet is now being used in places where the internet doesn't even uh reach. And so, that was really exciting and surprising to me uh that models were an alternative to that.

Strategy in the Age of Agents

I think this is an underappreciated thing, strategy, honestly. Um and this was also possible with older form factors, even with, you know, chat. But I think, you know, agents have taken this to the next level now that they can call these tools and do deeper research. Um a lot of people talk about execution of things. I think strategy, for all the reasons I've mentioned, matters more than ever right now. And it's really helpful, actually. I don't want an agent setting the strategy of the company for me. I want to come with something, but I can prompt it and have it really push back in different places that I wouldn't have expected. Um it's actually helped me reveal gaps in my own thing. So, I think that's like a slightly, maybe [music] undervalued aspect of interfacing with agents.

The Underrated Secret — and What Still Matters

I think it's the proliferation of open models. It's still much of a secret that open models are quickly catching up and are far more efficient to run.
