Lex: Can we give a shout out to some of the people along the way? We didn't really quite talk about the history of FFmpeg. So, uh maybe can you tell me about Freeze? Can you tell me about Michael Nemar? Can you tell me about some of the key figures about the eras of FFmpeg? Because there there's key eras and key people that made this possible.

Guest: Fabis Bard, as you mentioned, creating the concept, and then probably in the 2000s era — I would call the era era as tall of FFmpeg is the 2000 era — was Michael Neidmire. So key things he got done was exhaustive support for DivX and Xvid at the time and all sorts of weird variants of what's known as MPEG-4 part 2. So this predates the MPEG-4 part 10 that we used to. So this was 2000's era video codecs where there were a flavor after flavor of weird weird decoders.

2000 年代：多种播放器并存的混乱时代

At the time in the 2000s, you needed a new player to play every different type of file format. So there was Windows Media Player to play Windows media formats. There was real player to play real media formats. And those were the other — the other key thing in FFmpeg at the time were native decoders for those.

I actually remember being a teenager. There must have been figuring out that there was this one player that could play — could decode these files — without having separate bloated players, 'cause at the time when you downloaded RealPlayer, there was a ton of other stuff in there, a ton of ads, a ton of other things. And just having a simple library that was fast led to that.

2008 之后：H.264 与高清视频时代

And then I think 2008 was — 2008 onwards was a big change because that's when H.264 got its maturity, and I think something hopefully we'll talk about a bit more. This was the beginning of high-definition video. So H.264 was the key decoder of that. So I call that the late 2000 and 2010s, and that's when the big reverse engineers came along and really did astonishing work.

The beginning was a single player that could play Xvid, DivX, Windows Media, and Real Player — was already in a massive achievement in itself, without codec packs, without weird stuff you had to download that had weird ads and weird spyware.

Lex: VC 1.0 was out on those times — 2000, 2009, 2010. And this is like where it exploded.

Guest: Yeah, without codec packs it just works across all these different —

Lex: The fact — so it's just like all the codec packs are FFmpeg inside VC, plus we have other modules for all the type of —

Guest: At the time that wasn't — is there were weird — in the 2000s there were weird codec packs with DLLs coming from this place coming with spyware, with you know what; it wasn't reliable, you didn't know. And having a single player that was open source, or single playback module/player that could do this that was open source — but I think the thing to emphasize is this task in the 2000s that Michael did was Sisyphean, and it was really the number of edge cases are beyond comprehension. In terms of, you could have a Chinese CCTV system that did one weird variant of MPEG-4 part 2 — what's known as MPEG-4 ASP — and that was a weird variant, and you had to fix that without breaking everybody else.

Lex: Times a million.

反向工程的英雄们

Lex: So that's — so you said that that's where a lot of the reverse engineering was happening.

Guest: It started — it started in the 2000s with the Windows Media stuff, 'cause that was proprietary. It started with the real media. So with Benjamin Lson, Kostya Shishkov — Kostya Shishkov era. Those were the key — that was the key groundwork. And then in the 2010s was kind of the Paul Mahol / Kostya era, building, doing some of the most difficult codecs.

Um, JB maybe can talk about GoToMeeting 4 and GoToMeeting 5 and —

Lex: What what's the GoTo —?

Kostya：来自乌克兰的反向工程天才

JB: So, so, so, so like let's talk about this amazing Ukrainian guy called Kostya, who was — at the time living in Germany — and who was in love with Sweden, right? And the guy was the most — he he's like — like a lot of the people in the community are very clever. He's one of those who are like borderline geniuses, right? He was able to reverse engineering extremely complex codecs.

Um, and he — he does that, and we do a bit of engineers with Kieran, but clearly not at this level. No.

Lex: He reverse engineered binary blobs which are 20 megabytes.

Guest: Yeah. So just for reference: uh, 1 megabyte binary blob to reverse engineer is probably order of magnitude a month of work. And this guy is doing 20–30 megabyte blobs. Maybe we'll talk about that in a minute about the subtleties of how you do that. This guy is doing it for very difficult and very obscure codecs.

GoToMeeting 的故事

JB: And did that for fun, right? And um, so GoToMeeting was a big problem with VC because that was like the number one feature request for a long time. So I put a bounty and the guy at some point say: "Okay GB, I'm going to do it." And in a matter of two months — and then he explained how he did it. He was just like: "Oh, I looked at the code; like, this looked like a DCT that I used to see on WMV and so on."

He did that, and the funniest part is that the code he written is a ton of jokes, and there is a ton of JB — right, my name — and Kemp, and Kostya jokes inside the code. The code is beautiful, right?

谦逊的高手们

Lex: So one of the things I want to call — uh, I've gotten a chance to speak to some of the developers, some of the assembly language level people, and they all always make everything sound like it's kind of easy. There's a kind of humility, because — I maybe just the level of what's required to do this stuff is so high that everything else seems easy. I guess is the lesson to take away from that.

Guest: So in the community, like some of the most impressive people are the ones doing reverse engineering and the other ones doing the assembly folks, right? Um, and both of those type of people are amazing.

x264 for example became amazing because of a guy called Lauren Merritt, who was from University of Washington, I think, at the time. Yeah. And who was like — who made everything great and fast doing a ton of assembly. Um, yeah. So this is like the the the the golden era, I guess, where so many things got —

If you look at Kostya, for example, he looked at the world as a binary specification. He didn't need documentation or anything. It's: "I have a binary and I can figure all of this out." And he regularly used the phrase "binary specification — ah, you know, it's not a problem." And he went, and he would go away, and he would come back, and he would do interesting stuff.

反向工程一个 blob 究竟意味着什么？

Lex: Can you actually speak to the details, or any add color and texture to what it takes to reverse engineer a blob?

Guest: Yeah. So so let's look at GoToMeeting for example is a good one, because um — I record a meeting on GoToMeeting, for example. How do I play it back without needing this GoToMeeting player? They may not even be a player. I may need to send a recording of a meeting to someone that doesn't have a player or whatever.

So first of all, there's a ton of other stuff there. There's an actual video conferencing client you need to go and find. It may be easy. It may not be easy to find the actual module doing the decompression. You need a way to actually dump the YUV data from the module. So often it involves opening in a disassembler, trying to guess where the hooks are to incorporate that module and run that module natively to decode a sample file. So figure out where this module is doing the decoding process, and find a way to hook in and output the raw YUV data — because you will need that as a point of comparison for when you actually do the reverse engineering, because you'll need to be bit exact, or in some cases close to bit exact.

And then you open up your disassembler, use a lot of intuition to go and figure out, you know, where the DCT is, where's entropy coding. There is a kind of — not a rulebook, but there's always a pattern of some sort. Um, for example GoToMeeting, you know, it will be a lot of screen codec tools. There's also different variants. So often I think there's — what's GoToMeeting four, five —

JB: Two, three, four, I think.

Guest: Two, three, four. Yeah.

为什么这项工作对人类如此重要

Lex: So I should mention here, go to Perplexity: "GoToMeeting uses its own proprietary codec for older recorded sessions, historically stored in WMV files that require a special decoder to play properly on Windows. Without this decoder installed, Windows Media Player and some editors cannot decode the video track. So you may only hear audio or see a black screen." Boy, do I remember that.

But this is reverse engineering that. This is key, right?

JB: Because the GoToMeeting is something that not many people know anymore, right? You know about Zoom and Teams and so on. But like, now let's fast forward 10 years, 15 years, and like — this is GoToMeeting.exe for Windows 32 bits, right? Which is like, "Oh yeah, but I'm on Android, I'm on an iPad, I'm somewhere else, right? How are you going to do that? I'm going to be on RISC-V" — those are blocked. But there are tons of files we need to support for the future. And this is why those type of work are exceptionally useful for humanity.

数字考古学

Lex: I just have to say though, that reverse engineering process is mind-blowing.

Guest: It's crazy.

Lex: It's like — it's a kind of like, you know, I've been reading a lot, and I interview archaeologists. I mean, you just have so little signal. Yes, yes. You know, over time you get so much experience, you understand the structure of the original code, can kind of start inferring basics —

Guest: But you're like — like an archaeologist with a little brush trying to reconstruct the entire human —

Kieran 与 Cinepak/Synform 的故事

JB: Kieran is too humble, but Kieran has done some reverse engineering.

Kieran: Synform. Yeah. At the time — um — actually led to the open sourcing of that work.

Um, so in parallel to doing the binary side, you obviously have samples. In many cases, you don't have many samples. So you have to figure out what all the different flavors are, and you may have a — so Synform for example is actually a collection of different approaches and toolkits within that codec, 'cause often it grows naturally. And the hard part is finding the sample that gets you kind of somewhere to start without having to implement 10 different other things.

So start there. I think thankfully at the time I found a sample by pure chance that had a lot of flat blocks. It was animation. So that really helped a lot because it wasn't using particularly complex coding tools etc. And you could kind of get somewhere, and then build up and build up, until you figure: "Hey, here's a few bits here. I missed this — I missed this if branch that it does," and go, "Oh —"

Lex: So when we say samples, you mean a sample of videos? And then you're tracking, trying to infer like what is this codec doing by observing the sample and then looking at — at the machine —

Kieran: The machine code, saying "I have by this byte is six, take this branch" — and in a different sample. Oh —

Lex: It's nuts, man.

GoToMeeting 难度的另一个量级

Kieran: And then you go to things like GoToMeeting. It's like — mine was easy. Imagine, right, two orders of magnitude of more complexity. A guy alone somewhere in Germany doing that. Yeah.

And for a long time you're in a black box because — a decoder, for a long time, because there is so many steps: from the entropy decoding, the intra prediction, the motion prediction, the IDCT and so on — for a long time you don't see anything, right? So you're debugging purely in memory. Debugging guesswork. And you may have the buffer that the coefficients are stored in completely wrong, and so you may be going down a complete rabbit hole thinking it's this, and then "oh damn, that's not — that's something else."

Lex: And you're doing that on binaries that are tens of megabytes, millions of instructions, right? So you're stepping through the debugger like one by one, you know, instruction by instruction, going "Hey, this instruction changes this, this does this" —

Kieran: Pausing the program on the CPU level —

Lex: Pausing it on the CPU level, watching what's going on, trying to figure —

Kieran: Sometimes you need to like be in a VM. So yes, you can pause the VM.

Lex: Yeah, pause the VM, dump the memory, 'cause some of the codecs could have encryption. There could be um, like a DRM on there. So you need to dump the memory from a virtual machine.

DVD Jon 的传奇时刻

JB: Like when I joined the central in 2003, Jon Lech Johansen basically broke the DVD specification and created DeCSS. Showed us how he was breaking a DRM which was MP4 FairPlay from Apple — what he did on his laptop. And I was young; I was 21. Was just like mind-blowing because he was basically debugging Windows inside a type of VM with — like, wow! It's incredible. It's mind-blowing and inspiring.

社区如何相互支持

Lex: Does it get — like, from your experience and from what you've seen in the community, does it get discouraging? Does it get —

Kieran: People help you. People send you samples. People are keen. Sometimes you don't have access to an encoder. So this is even more difficult, because you just — you just ask, and you have to ask for samples. I remember VideoLAN used to tweet for samples at one stage. "Hey, I need this obscure sample" — and —

JB: For a long time I was, "Oh, I need this codec, and I need this codec" — and and — if you were really lucky you would find — like, if you were unlucky, you'd get like one or you'd get nothing, or you'd get one or two. And then they would — sometimes you'd find a goldmine, like: "Yeah, my company has a 100,000 of these files because we depend on it for some reason." And so those are kind of the best, because then they can test bit exactness across the huge range of coding tools.

Bit Exactness：位精确性

Lex: Can you explain bit exactness?

Guest: Bit exactness. So um, most but not all video codecs — certainly from about the 2000s onwards — have a bit exact definition. So every implementation must produce exactly the same bits, bit for bits, in exactly the same data that comes out of a decoder for like a large number of samples. For a given sample. So Lex's implementation, JB's implementation, and my implementation of H.264 must match bit exactly.

That wasn't the case in the '90s of MPEG-2. Probably fair to say one of the biggest mistakes the video industry made. And I think people who were in the room in '92 — I don't — most of both of us were in diapers, I suspect — but um, have acknowledged — um, I would give a shout out to Yuri Reznik. He's acknowledged that was one of the big mistakes of the era.

Lex: And you're saying the encoders needed to be able to run tests, and then the bit exactness. I mean, that's a nice thing to guarantee. Like, there's a parallel sort of development here on the way the web browser works, which is — you know, takes HTML and displays it, and there's no bit exactness there across the different engines.

FFmpeg 的"赢家通吃"

Guest: I would point out actually FFmpeg is unique in the sense that it has been a winner-takes-all scenario. You have — browsers is a good analogy, because it has to parse a lot of different content and render it in a particular way, like a decoder. But there still are multiple browser engines. There's Firefox's one, there's Chrome's one, there's a few Japanese ones that are pretty decent. That's not been the case in multimedia in general.

Um, across a wide range of codecs, FFmpeg has kind of won it all, I suppose, in a sense — because of the fact that you can get every new codec added is actually worth more than the value of that codec itself, because it makes the whole thing better.

Yuri Reznik 致敬

Lex: Man, this is really cool. Uh, going to Perplexity: "Yuri Reznik is a multimedia and signal processing researcher, got his PhD in computer science from Kyiv University, with over 150 papers and more than 80 granted US patents. Contributor to major multimedia standards including H.264, MPEG-4 AVC, H.265, MPEG-4 ALS, G.718" —

Guest: G.71 is telco stuff —

Lex: And so he was more connected to —

JB: RealAudio, RealVideo, right? That was —

Lex: Oh yeah —

JB: Very important at that time —

Lex: Coder, BrightCove context. Man, I need to hang out with Yuri. He's legit.

JB: And he's like one of the most nice person ever, right? Like — um, for example, for my startup that I'm doing right now called Kyber, right — I met Yuri because I met him every year at the Mile High Video conference, which is in Denver. Um, and he gave me like so much good ideas and good things. He's like really amazing person. He tells us how, you know, how great it is to be, you know, even know us — and you know, you look at that and it's — I think it's the other way around, Yuri.

FATE：FFmpeg 的自动化测试体系

Lex: That reminds me of a thing that you mentioned to me about FATE testing and like the insanely rigorous process that's used to test everything that's incorporated into FFmpeg. Can you take me through the testing process?

Guest: Yeah. So, FFmpeg has a system called FATE — FFmpeg Automated Testing Environment. Because FFmpeg runs on so many different OSes and can be compiled with so many different compilers, there's been a crazy number of configurations.

So you can see the absurd combination of um compiler variants, operating system variants, instruction sets. You can see at the top, macOS has tons of different variants because it has iOS, it has tvOS. I'm looking at a page fate.ffmpeg.org — 81 minutes ago, 76 minutes ago, looking at the different architectures, the operating systems, different compilers, Apple clang version, uh —

Lex: Combinations of combinations.

Guest: These are all run by volunteers. So these are all volunteer systems. The ones at the top, for example — the macs, I host in my office. For example, I host all sorts of different stuff. Other people host other things. So it's really there to make sure — and because FFmpeg does quite complex C code — for example, you do have miscompilations. So the compiler will sometimes compile C code incorrectly, for example. This happens once in a while. There's like — there's a log of all the compilations.

Lex: Yeah, log of all the compilations, all the tests. I think one of the other ones will show all the tests passing.

Guest: If you click, you can see all the tests.

Lex: Yeah, back: "all tests successfully" — in logs, test, yeah. So you see all those tests are passing, of all the different codecs, all the different filter transformations, all the — the level of scale is quite crazy on all the combinations. It's not just a matrix at this point. It's like a pivot table of different combinations.

Lex: That's nuts.

Guest: And it's a key part of what we do, because you may be able to test something locally — you make a change, but actually that breaks GCC version 11 on Mac or something like that, and you're able to then fix that. We also have miscompilation. So the C code — sometimes the compiler can have a bug in it where it creates the wrong output, and that can have quite a big effect sometimes on a video, because of the way frames have dependencies. Even a small change in the output can cascade to actually quite big glitches.

You see PowerPC, you see RISC, you see PC, there was — there was weird stuff in the past like DEC Alpha, you see Visual Studio, different versions, Intel compiler, Apple Clang, you name it.

痛点与噩梦

Lex: What are some of the pain points? Like, maybe do you have emotional triggers, uh, maybe nightmares about a particular operating system, a particular container-codec combination?

Guest: For me, it's really easy because — so I have a day job. Um, my company builds — the company I started builds equipment for broadcasting sports matches between TV stadiums and studios, for example. We have to work with 10-bit video, and 10-bit video has a set of challenges — that you can't process 10-bit data natively on a CPU. So that means you have to stick it in 16 bits, so that means you have six wasted bits. So there's different packing formats to actually pack the data more efficiently, because when you send that over a network, you lose — 'cause you need to save that 40%.

For example, on PCI Express, you may only have bus bandwidth to do that. And so I think internally we have about some — some are industry ones and some are internal to our own hardware that we build. We have a I think a 5x5 or 6x6 matrix of every single format to every single other format conversion. In fact, one of them I sent you — and they're all written in handwritten assembly, and they all support different CPU generations. So this is really traumatic, handling all these different combinations times a million.

Lex: By the way, the company you're talking about is Open Broadcast.

Guest: No, no relation to the free OBS streaming service.

Lex: Yeah. Yeah.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、反向工程是数字时代的考古学——但比考古更孤独
最让我意外的不是技术细节，而是这段对话揭示的一个**时间反转悖论**：Costa 在德国独自一人反向工程 GoTo Meeting 的二进制 blob，看似是在"破解"一个当下的商业软件，但他真正服务的对象是**未来的考古学家**——是 15 年后想在 RISC-V 上播放某段 2010 年录制的 .wmv 会议记录的人。
JB 那句话点破了一切："GoTo Meeting.exe for Windows 32 bits, right? Which is like, oh yeah, but I'm on Android, I'm on an iPad, I'm somewhere else, right?"
这就是 FFmpeg 真正的隐藏使命：它不是一个媒体播放器，而是一台**数字时间机器**。每一个被反向工程的编解码器，都是为人类文明的"数字遗骸"购买的一份保险单。
跨领域连接：这与亚历山大图书馆的抄写员、修道院里复制古籍的僧侣是**完全同构**的工作。Lex 自己也意识到了——他提到考古学家用小刷子重建人类。但更深

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Liya Palagashvili on the Startup Mindset: How to Build a Career in Economics
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个出人意料的观察：她贩卖的"灵活福利"，本质上是在解构她自己人生的隐喻

Liya 一边在政策圈里推动"把福利从W2雇佣关系中解耦"（decouple benefits from employment），一边在自己的人生里实践着同一件事——把"身份"从单一职业角色中解耦。她是学者、是公共写作者、是政策企业家、是项目搭建者、是新手妈妈，这些角色像可携带的福利一样，挂在"她"这个人身上，而不是挂在某一个机构岗位上。

换句话说，她的政策主张不是中立的学术输出，而是她自己人生算法的外推。她为什么会本能地看到"工人需要可携带的福利"？因为她本人就是一个"可携带福利的人"——好奇心、resilience、startup mindset 是她的便携式福利包，跟着她从Sunni Purchase到Mercatus到国会作证再到育儿。一个人在政策上反复推动的隐喻，往往就是她个人生存策略的放大。这也解释了为什么访谈最后她说自己最焦虑的事是"会不会陷入确认偏误"——因为她隐隐意识到：当一个人的政策主张与她的人生哲学完美对齐时，那种"丝滑的说服力"本身就是一种危险信号。

跨领域连接：startup mindset × 河流的"游荡"（meandering）× Bateson 的即兴艺术

她说"早期职业要像创业公司一样多试错，成熟后再像成熟公司一样专业化"——这听起来是商学院语言，但它其实和河流地貌学里的现象高度同构。年轻的河流是辫状河（braided river），水流分叉、试探、回填，看似混乱，实则是在地形未知时寻找最低能量路径；成熟河流则收敛为单一蜿蜒主道，因为它已经"知道"地势。Liya 的职业轨迹、Bateson 的"composing a life"、以及她描述的"fail fast"，本质上是同一个分布式搜索算法：在不确定环境下，多分叉的探索比单一深掘更具信息价值。

而更出人意料的是：Pete 在访谈中无意中泄露了一个反例——Tullock 的"intellectual jiu-jitsu"教学法。Tullock 不允许失败被温柔地接住，他要立刻摧毁每一个答案。Buchanan 则相反，把蠢问题变成天才问题。Liya 全程在赞美 Buchanan 式的支架，但她的成长路径其实需要两种力量同时存在——Buchanan 给她容错的安全感让她敢于"fail fast"，Tullock 式的对抗给了她"你不是你的论文"的免疫训练。她说的"24小时规则"（难过24小时然后向前看）其实是在自己体内同时安装这两套人格。

隐藏的系统性真相：她推荐的所有方法论，其实都在解决同一个底层问题——降低"身份"的沉没成本

把这次对话里所有的金句串起来看： - "你不是你的论文" → 降低学术身份的沉没成本 - "Fail fast" → 降低项目身份的沉没成本
- "24小时规则" → 降低情绪身份的沉没成本 - "Portable benefits" → 降低劳动者职业身份的沉没成本 - "AI 作为交易成本冲击" → 降低组织对个体身份的锁定

这是同一个操作的五个层级。她真正在研究的不是劳动经济学，而是"如何让人不被任何单一身份绑架"的一般理论。这也是为什么 Emily Oster 那种"用数据解构育儿焦虑"会让她着迷——Oster 做的事情本质上也是把"母亲"这个身份从神圣化的情绪绑定中解耦出来，让它变成一个可以被讨论、被优化、可携带的角色。

最后留一个让人不安的问题：当"可携带性"成为最高美德时，什么是不能被携带的？ 一个完全可携带的人生，是否会失去某些只有在"被绑定"的状态下才会生长出来的东西——比如对一个机构、一个学派、一段不可撤回的关系的深度承诺？Hayek 项目本身，恰恰是一个反"可携带"的存在：它是 Pete 几十年绑定在一个地方、一群人、一种知识传统上才长出来的东西。Liya 受益于这个不可携带的根系，而她推广的却是可携带性。这不是矛盾，但这是张力，而张力正是她自己说的——"keeps me up at night"的那个东西。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

You're listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. This podcast includes audio from lectures, interviews, and discussions from scholars and visitors of the F.A. Hayek Program for Advanced Study in Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. To learn more about the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. To learn about graduate student fellowship opportunities with the Mercatus Center at George Mason University for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org.

I am here today with my colleague Liya Palagashvili. It is March 31st, 2026. Liya, thanks for coming on and talking to me.

Thank you, Pete, for having me. It's a pleasure.

一、把人生当作即兴创作：Composing a Life

So I wanted to start with a little story and then ask you to react to it, because I think you're a perfect illustration of this.

When I was a graduate student, there was a Robinson professor here at George Mason University, very famous, named Mary Catherine Bateson. She was the daughter of Margaret Mead, the famous anthropologist, and she published a book — I think it came out right after I left George Mason, but she gave presentations on it while I was here and I attended them. It's called Composing a Life. It's about an exploration of life as improvisational art form. She uses women in particular as the practitioners of this improvisational art form, and she uses personal stories of herself and four other women and how they navigated complex, multifaceted aspects of their lives — their careers, their family responsibilities — and her central argument reminds me of your own journey in a lot of ways.

So I was hoping that you might be able to share with us different parts of your own experiences: as a student, as a campus organizer, as a professor (both as a scholar and teacher), as a public intellectual in the New York Times and places like that, as well as now the more modern social media, and as a policy analyst who's testified in front of Congress and had all of that.

And as a new parent.

Yes, of course — which is also a big part of your life.

Yeah, when you're new. Um, thanks Pete for the question.

I think I've always resonated with the idea of life as something you can compose rather than follow — and that you just follow your curiosity in your life. So if I'm like, "Oh, I'm really interested in campus organizing," like, why not make that part of who you are, and you know, focusing on scholarly pursuits. It was just curiosity, right? And a lot of that came from you, Pete. Because I was like, "Oh, he's asking all these interesting questions, and I want to know — like, why was the Soviet Union so terrible (because I'm from there), or why was Armenia really poor but the US rich?"

So I think there are many different things in the world that I was curious about, so I just wanted to follow those avenues. And also like as a parent too — there's so many interesting questions about children and education that I'm curious about. So now I'm like, "Oh, I'm reading all these books about the brain and child development," because it's fascinating.

And I think that's always been how I was: if I'm curious about something, I want to lean in and follow and see where that path takes you. The student organizing — you remember me when I was in undergrad and I was organizing the GMU Economic Society — that's because I've always been interested in more of these public engagements, organizing, bringing communities together. So that was a part of me.

But what was really great about being at George Mason and with you and all the other professors that kind of inspired these questions is that you guys asked the big questions, and I wanted to lean in and be like, "Hold on, this is really interesting." Because I didn't have this path of being a scholar or researcher in front of me — it's not what I wanted — but because I was surrounded by all of you who were bringing up these questions, making interesting, showing interesting frameworks and perspectives, then I leaned into that. And bam — all of a sudden I'm the scholar, which was not what I anticipated going in.

So instead of seeing these as separate roles, I've always seen them as different ways of exploring parts that you as a person are interested in.

二、为人父母的"研究者本能"与 Emily Oster

Yeah, it's fascinating you brought up child rearing and the way that we approach it versus a lot of other people. We're kind of nerdy types, so what we do is we go and do the research — what's going on with all this?

Yeah, I know way too many papers about like, "Oh, what's the impact of egg consumption on young children and their brain development?"

Do you read Emily Oster a lot?

Yeah. So you recommend her to other new parents?

Yes. I subscribe to her newsletter, ParentData. I highly recommend her. I know you guys had her out at the Markets and Society conference as well. She's another person who — she's an economist, studied one thing, and then all of a sudden became this model for women who are expecting and have young children. I even have friends who are not connected at all to academia or economics who follow her.

I gave my daughter-in-law all her books — you know, I have two grandchildren as well. And I didn't get the reaction from Liz like I hoped because she was juggling so many balls, and then I'm adding on top of it while she's in school getting a degree, having a two-year-old, and then having a new infant. It's like, "Hey, have you taken time to read this yet?" She's like, "No — how about you read it for me and tell me?"

But yeah, I think Emily's doing great work for people to have fact-based issues to look at, because in this particular thing, there's a lot of emotions and a lot of mythologies.

三、Buchanan 的"自我作者性"与 Bateson 的女性观察

The other thing I really liked about what you just said is that you saw yourself as your own composer.

When I was a graduate student, one of the first assignments that Jim Buchanan gave us in class — I had him my first year in graduate school — was: who is the individual in economics? Like, who is this individual that we're actually studying? He was trying to get us to think about the notion of self-authorship — that's like in his "natural and artifactual man" idea, that we are in fact the authors of our own lives and we need to take on that responsibility. And that responsibility may scare the hell out of us at times, but on the other hand, that's what we have to make a free society.

So I remember reading the Mary Catherine Bateson book and this idea of composing a life. One of her arguments is that men are not as improvisational because they're able to have a very linear career path. Many of it's because of child care issues, but you know — you go to school, you get your job, you try to get your promotions. Whereas women are forced to have these punctuated moments, and as a result, they have to think creatively about how they're going to handle all of that. And it gives them an advantage — and that's the ultimate thing. Now I'm probably reading into it, but at the end I think I remember her claiming women were more interesting than men, precisely because of this. And there is a certain aspect of that which is quite fascinating.

Yeah, that's an interesting point. I haven't read her book, but with men you do see them follow this linear path, and I wonder if it's because they're more risk-averse. They don't want to take big risks in their careers — like, "Oh, I went to law school, I'm gonna get this corporate job because it pays well." And maybe it's part of the expectations that are put — where the man has to be the provider, in a marriage and things like that. So maybe they're less risk-averse as well.

Whereas with composing your own life, you have to be able to take some risks and be entrepreneurial — and have the courage to be like, "I went down this path, but it's not really working well — I'm gonna try something else."

四、人口结构与技术变迁下的"创业心态"

So I want to actually — my next question relates to that.

Given the changing demographics — the population decline, especially a younger population — and technological innovations, the advancement of AI and other kinds of things, the traditional academic career path that I followed (graduating in the late 1980s, becoming a professor at that time, and then just continuing on) was fueled a lot more by both more bodies needing to go to school and more money being dumped into schools during that period.

You know, what I've seen — even just at George Mason — when I started graduate school there were 9,000 students. Right now it's close to 30 or 40,000. In my lifespan of being an adult that's happened. It's unclear that that's going to continue uninterrupted; there's going to be distortions.

There's two responses to that. One is a strategic self-defense response — I build in ways in which I can cover myself from this demographic cliff or this technological shift. The other is a more entrepreneurial, creative direction — you use that opportunity to see rooms for creative entrepreneurial growth. Given your history, you think more in this creative entrepreneurial way rather than covering for downside risk. Could you share with the listeners some of the examples?

Startup Mindset

Yeah. I've always approached my career, especially early on, as what I called the startup mindset. When you're a grad student and also early in your career, the analogy is: you're not an established company yet. So you're a startup, and that means you should be taking more risks, not fewer. You should be following one path and then another, and being like, "That path is not working, but this one is paying off some dividends — so let's try this one."

One example was in grad school — focusing a lot on research, but then having this opportunity from Don Boudreaux, who's another professor. He said, "Look, the Wall Street Journal came to me and they want a piece written on the decoupling of wages and productivity since the 1950s. Do you want to write this with me?"

I had a million other things on my plate — courses, exams, research projects. I wasn't a public writing person at that point. And I was like, "Let me just try this. It's going to take — I still have to do all these other things I need to do." So I had to reduce sleeping that week, but I was like, "I'm gonna figure out how to do this."

Just having that opportunity with Don Boudreaux opened up this new path that I didn't even think existed, and I loved it. Then that created more and more opportunities — "Oh, you wrote this piece in the Wall Street Journal, you learned how to write like that" — and it created another opportunity down the road. So you've kind of, as a startup, built in another revenue line, profit line, another business line.

As we get older and more established — just like an established company — you start to specialize. "Okay, I'm not going to be this hardcore empirical economist, but I'm going to do this other method that I'm really great at, or I'm going to do public writing and policy entrepreneurship." It's a natural process where early on you should be open to a bunch of different opportunities, and then just see what works and what doesn't.

Fail Fast

Be okay with the failure. In the startup world, they have this concept called fail fast. It's embracing failure, but "fail fast" meaning: "Oh, I'm gonna try this, and if it doesn't work, I'm gonna drop it and move on to the next thing." You don't sit there and be like, "Oh my goodness, I wrote this terrible paper as a grad student, I'm never going to be an academic researcher again." Which I had — I wrote a really bad paper and I was like, "Oh my god, this is terrible." You have a moment where you're like, "I'm going to close this off." But you learn from the failure and you moved on, and you wrote the next project better and the next paper better.

Kirznerian Alertness Applied to Careers

The defensive approach is to wait until things are safe or perfectly aligned, but by then you've missed a lot of opportunities. I love the Kirznerian alertness to profit opportunities, because I use it in this case. If an opportunity arises, it's not going to stay there forever. So you should jump on that opportunity in the moment — be alert to different career opportunities that you're presented with. If I had said no to Don in the moment because I was like 24 or something, maybe that opportunity would have arisen in the future, and that's a path that I wouldn't have been able to take in that way.

Yeah, there's like these evolutionary adjacent moments — because you did that, then it made possible this.

五、识别"五项全能型"学者，以及给18岁 Liya 的建议

You kind of answered my next question, but I still want to push you a little bit on it.

You and I are involved in this organization, Mercatus, that is dedicated to talent identification and cultivation. You're now doing that with team members in your labor policy group. You're mentoring a lot of young scholars. You yourself were a product of this program, as was I.

The way I've always tried to summarize it — I use a sports analogy. We're trying to identify these five-tool athletes, these five-tool academics. It's an analogy to baseball scouting: a no-miss player for Major League Baseball when they're coming out of high school scores high on all five categories. So I'm always looking for that in academics: being a scholar, teacher, policy analyst, public intellectual, and a program builder — being able to do what you're doing as a director of a program, or what you did when you were at SUNY Purchase, building up a program like you did with your Shark Tank initiative.

You've talked about your own approach — the betting on yourself, the startup firm — which I think is just brilliant. I never thought about it until I heard you say it. But now that you've had some experience in life — and you're no longer 24-year-old Liya — if you ran into 18-year-old Liya, are there things that you would advise to her that you wish you would have known then?

Pete, are you calling me old now?

No, I didn't! Notice I didn't say that. I still remember freshman Liya, so I can't get that image out of my head.

No, that's okay. I'm okay being old and wise now.

韧性与"你不是你的论文"

What would I say to 18-year-old Liya? I think it's really important to have resilience early on. That's like a fundamental deal-breaker personality skill trait. I did have resilience, but it took many weeks to get out of something. When we get some sort of bad criticism or negative criticism, we think, "Oh no," and take it personally.

I remember feeling bad about papers that didn't go well, or being like, "I'm not going to be an academic anymore, because this is the worst thing I've written." So I would tell 18-year-old Liya — or other students, especially in the academic world pursuing grad school or PhD — that you are not your paper. You can take criticism seriously, but don't take it personally.

That's something I wish I would have told myself more, because there were certainly times where I took a lot of the criticism personally and was like, "Okay, maybe I shouldn't be in this world. Maybe I followed the wrong path." That's the fear you have sometimes when you get a lot of negative feedback or criticism — which is essential, right? Because that's part of your development. You need to be challenged. You need to be able to hear, "Hey, this isn't great" — and then that pushes you to make the paper better.

The 24-Hour Rule

Now that I'm old and wise, I've created my 24-hour rule: feel bad for 24 hours and then move on. In the past, it was more like, "Oh no" — sulking for days and reconsidering the path.

自我激励的难题

Another thing that's hard in grad school — you've got a lot of space and free time and you have to self-motivate. That's really difficult, because most people who are 24 don't go to — they're not in a PhD program. They have to go to a job that's 9 to 5, and there's structure, clear expectations, a paycheck that comes every two weeks. That doesn't really happen when you're in grad school.

So it's really important to have some sort of things that you do to self-motivate. There was a quote that I always loved — I used to have it actually in my room in front of my desk. Let me see if I remember it correctly:

"Motivation doesn't last, but neither does bathing. That's why we recommend it daily."

I had those quotes where I would try to look at it every day, because I was like, "I need to self-motivate" — especially after you finish your courses, where you have to be able to get in there and make progress and output today, even though you don't have to work that day in theory.

六、Grit、Elinor Ostrom，以及看不见的失败

I love several of those quotes that you have, and when you were talking I was thinking about Angela Duckworth's emphasis on grit and how important grit is.

I think one of the problems for young people looking at someone like you — or even professors looking back at someone like you — they see this amazing success story, and as a result they think your life has been smooth and continuous and just always achieving. "You get the 30 under 30, then you should get whatever every decade, and when you talk up at the White House, they should just say, 'Oh, okay, let's follow Liya's advice.'"

They see this smooth, linear progression, but actually along the way there's been an amazing amount of perseverance and grit. Because they see it as smooth and continuous, they underestimate just how tough you are, because of how hard you've had to have it to make what you want.

You wrote early in your career a lot about Elinor Ostrom, and she's a great example of that as well. In retrospect, when you were there, did you understand how difficult it was for her to achieve what she did, or did you see her as just having a linear path?

I saw her as having a linear path — exactly what you just said. Because nobody sees the little failures, and every step is like a potential failure. And then you do fail, and you're like, "Oh man, this didn't happen this way," and then you have to reinvent or have grit and try to follow something else. "Oh, I wrote this paper but it's not great — actually, it was a terrible paper. I'm going to rip it apart and either redo the entire paper or just do a different question."

People don't see that, and I didn't see that about Elinor Ostrom as well. I get it now as we are older and wise — or older and not wise. But I didn't see it at the time. I saw her as having a linear path, and probably most everybody else when I was — and that's probably who I was comparing myself to. I was like, "Oh, I'm not meant for an academic path because, look at them doing so great and amazing, and I can't even get this argument right in this one paper."

It's a very real experience, especially for grad students and PhD students. If you compare yourself to the majority of other people, they're probably in a normal 9-to-5 job pursuing their careers doing something else. So you might question, "Is this the right path?" But it's really important to have that grit.

七、三句金言 + Buchanan vs. Tullock

So I now have some very good phrases from you. Let me sum them up:

  1. Be your own startup firm — you're investing in yourself in your allocations among your different branches of the investment firm.
  2. Fail fast, not fail slow — you're going to constantly be adapting and adjusting.
  3. In the academic realm, you are not your paper.

I think these are just awesome.

But I like little phrases too. I don't know if you remember this — I lost this when we moved from Enterprise Hall over to Buchanan Hall — but I had for years on my door a fortune cookie phrase that reminded me of Buchanan. It said:

"The wise man learns more from the fool than the fool will ever learn from the wise man."

The reason was: when I was in class, there was no stupid question in a Buchanan class, because a question would be asked that the rest of us thought was stupid, and Buchanan would turn it into a genius question in the way he would do it. That did two things: it didn't devastate the student, and it elevated the conversation in the classroom.

I was always amazed by this, because the contrast — and I'm not dissing him — was to Tullock. In Tullock, even if you answered a brilliant answer, he would find a way to destroy it. That was his method — to destroy. It was like intellectual jiu-jitsu with him, and he wanted to see if you could survive the assault. That was a totally different strategy, and having the two of them going back and forth, it was like intellectual ping-pong: how do you answer to each one of these?

I had that phrase for years because it summed up a lot. I really like these phrases that you have — especially "you are not your paper" — because so much of our job is to write arguments that are subject to contestation, that then get attacked, and that's the only way they can get better.

一个100美元的悬赏故事

I have a story for you, Pete, actually on another quote that you gave me. It's a quote that you gave me that I used to have hanging up on my wall too:

"The worst thing that can happen to a good cause is not to be skillfully attacked, but to be ineptly defended."

Do you remember the story of that quote? I'm going to tell you. You were — you were an undergraduate, and you said, "I cannot find the source of this quote, and I'll give —" I don't know how much you offered, but you offered some money if someone would be able to find it. And you found it in literally five minutes. I had spent like a month looking for it. So just user failure on my part.

Kirznerian alertness to profit opportunity.

Yeah, I remember it. It was unbelievable. I couldn't believe that you answered me so fast.

Well, I was like, "I'm an undergrad. This is $100. I'm going to find a way to find the quote."

That's awesome.

八、研究主线：自治、法经济学、古典自由主义、零工经济

I wanted to switch gears a little bit and talk about your own research. Most of the people listening know about the work that you're doing right now on the gig economy, and I don't want to short-shrift that. I think that is such a vital aspect of the entrepreneurial opportunity economy, and it's one of the ways we can address rigidities in our current labor markets.

One of the things that we've seen — like Casey Mulligan laid out in The Redistribution Recession years ago — is that our labor markets have become more gummy than they used to be. As a result, people can't get on the bottom of the economic ladder to climb up. Offering this alternative, where you're not having to go through the standard rules and regulations of the labor market, might end up being a freeing-up.

But what I wanted to ask about was some of the stuff that you did earlier on theory and application — you studied self-governance and development economics. You contributed to the field of law and economics. You have studied political economy, and you're editing — when is that due out by the way? It's a major book.

Summer, in July. Editing the Handbook on Classical Liberalism.

So maybe tell us a little bit about that, and then also this issue of the entrepreneurial society, which you've taken from a variety of different angles — the gig economy (Uber and Lyft), portability of benefits, and flexibility in labor markets.

"Teach a man to fish" —— 框架，而不是桶

That's a great question, Pete, but I want to highlight mentors for creating some of these — because when you're an early grad student, I didn't know where to start. Working with you, I got the self-governance bucket and the institutional analysis bucket, and then moving forward.

I think that's the phrase — "teach a man to fish, and he'll never come back." The greatness of good mentors like you is that you teach the man to fish. So you're not giving me the buckets — you're not being like, "Here's some food, here's some food." If you're doing it right, like you did with me, you're teaching me how to find the buckets and invest and do the research.

That's the most important thing — in grad school with you and our program, I learned really well the framework and different ways to apply the framework, and many different ways to think about trade-offs and perspective. Then that becomes really easy to apply, because as you used to say, look out the window. That was one of your quotes — "What are you interested in? Look out the window." Then you see a question or an interesting thing, and you apply the framework or the lens — because you used to say, "Put on your economic eyeglasses," and then you can see that thing in depth, and really fascinating.

用经济学眼镜看零工经济

So the gig economy question — someone who's not wearing an economist or institutional analysis, comparative analysis framework — whatever different lenses, Austrian framework, whatever different lenses we have — they're going to be looking at these workers as, "Oh my god, they're in these terrible jobs that they only work a couple hours a week, and they don't pay a lot, and there's no benefits, no security." They're not going to see the truth. They're not going to see the thing in all of its dimensions if they're not wearing some sort of framework.

Whereas when we look at it, we're like, "Oh, this is really interesting. This worker chose to work in this gig economy space. Why did he choose to do that? What were the choices that he was faced with that he ended up going to the gig economy or self-employment bucket?"

一个移民家庭的视角

I'll give you a little context and story too. When my parents first moved to the United States, I was seven years old. But they moved here with basically zero language, no transferable degrees — nothing of that sort. So they're not going to be candidate number one at Microsoft or whatever company is hiring. They're not even going to be candidate number one at Walmart, because you need to speak the language. So you need to put yourself in the shoes of those people who are choosing and working in these different jobs.

My dad and my mom, for example — they started off working as self-employed. My dad was a construction worker. That was his first job. And then you slowly build up, you learn the language, you get skilled.

People have this framework where they're thinking of a gig job as your career. And a lot of times, by the way, there's that perspective about minimum wage jobs as well. They're like, "Oh, this is a minimum wage career." But for most people, minimum wage jobs are not their careers. I worked a minimum wage job when I was a high school student, and then you move on and you build up.

So it's really important to understand the choices that people have when they go into these jobs — that it's a stepping stone to build skills, to learn, and then they move up over time.

数据告诉我们什么

That's one side of the self-employment/gig economy/independent contractor side. The other angle, which is what the data is actually showing about Uber and Lyft and DoorDash drivers, is that the majority of them actually have W-2 jobs. So they're literally doing this as an after-5pm or on-the-weekend thing.

Put yourself in their shoes — do they want another W-2 job? Probably not. That's why they're doing their gig economy job.

DoorDash data, for example: 90% of Dashers work less than 10 hours a week. That's not your full-time job — that's your gig. And when you look at the data on when they work, they're working after 5pm, evenings and weekends. So they're truly treating it as a gig. In that perspective, it's a value-add for that person, because they have their 9-to-5 job, they want to meet their financial goals and needs, so they pick up the side job.

If you don't have the lens and the framework to look at the situation in all of its dimensions, you're going to look at that worker and be like, "Oh wow, they're in this vulnerable position, and this is terrible for them." But you haven't even asked: "Do they have a full-time job already? What else is going on? Do they have health insurance through their full-time W-2 job?"

三个 Uber 司机的故事

One of the things I think a lot of people might not do enough of is talk to the Uber drivers actually.

So it's funny — I was just in Charleston, South Carolina, and one of my Uber drivers couldn't speak English. She actually used her Apple iPhone constantly to use AI to translate and then talk back to us. She had just moved to the country, and she was driving Uber to get the beginning of things.

Another time I was in Phoenix and the Uber driver was a golf pro. He was trying to get on the circuit. He had bags that I had to put in the back of his trunk, because I was going to the airport, and he was all upset because we had to move his golf bag out of the way. He was talking to me — he's trying to be a golf pro, trying to get into tournaments. He was your 22-year-old that you're talking about. He probably didn't have the sponsorship he was hoping to have, and so this was his way to try to pursue his dream.

And then my wife and I were in an Uber in Charleston, and it was a retiree that took us around. He said, "No, I retired to Charleston. I do this to pick up some extra money." Then he laughed and said, "I have very strict limits. I refuse to pick up any college kids after 10pm at night, because I don't want to be stuck with drunk college kids. I'm a retiree — I don't have time for that."

I was amazed at the flexibility, and what they each had as a little bit of — I don't want to romanticize it, but this is what they're doing, it's their choice. Going back to your composing-or-authorship idea.

九、可携带福利：从员工身份中解耦福利

Can you just say very briefly about what you're trying to do with the flexible benefits stuff, because you're able to get quite a nice platform for that — whether it gets accepted or not is a different question.

So the idea — I made up a little phrase for it: flexible benefits for a flexible workforce, although it's called portable benefits right now.

I'm going to show you the big vision thing, and then what we're doing as well.

大愿景：把福利从W-2雇佣中解耦

As you know, in economics research, the coupling of benefits to a W-2 job has been shown by a bunch of different economists as creating all these labor market mobility issues — a lot of inefficiencies. Even Jonathan Gruber at MIT — that was his whole research portfolio: showing that the employer-sponsored health subsidy connected to employment has actually done a lot of damage and bad outcomes to the US labor market.

He just has a different solution than you.

Yeah, he has a different solution, but we both see the problem. The easily identified problem is that the benefits are stuck with a W-2 job — very specific to a W-2 job.

So the North Star for the vision of labor market dynamism is to decouple benefits from employment. What we're working on now is a small stepping stone on that: first, how do we get benefits to independent workers that are portable? Then once we figure out this portability question, we can think about the 10- or 20-year question of how we make benefits and labor markets a bit more efficient — better labor market dynamism.

Labor market dynamism is really important for the things going on in our world right now. We need to have labor markets that are resilient to technological shock — AI and other shocks as well. The tech shock is going to be a big one. So having dynamic labor markets is a way to have resilience for our labor market economy.

具体问题：自愿安排为什么不被允许

The specific thing going on here, Pete, is that currently, if you hire a freelance graphic designer and you want to say, "I'm going to give 90% of your income as cash, and 10% into a benefits account that you can hold — maybe it's tax-advantaged, maybe not, whatever" — you can't currently do that. Because if you did, then the IRS and state regulators and the government will come after Pete and be like, "Well, Pete, you just misclassified your freelance graphic designer. You hired her for the last 20 years — you're going to have to pay back fines and fees and penalties for misclassifying this worker."

So that voluntary arrangement between a worker and a client or a company can't occur. It's bad for the business, but it's also bad for the worker — because the independent worker who's self-employed doesn't have the infrastructure for benefits, for savings. There's no infrastructure in the same way that you would as a W-2 employee.

What we're trying to create is the ability for independent workers, gig workers, self-employed workers to have that infrastructure — where they can put money in if they want to (all voluntary), and if the company or client wants to put money in on their behalf, they should be able to do so as well. It's pretty simple. I call it legalized access to benefits for independent workers.

The reason I love this idea and the reason I think it's taken off is because there's no real downside. You're not mandating Pete Boettke to give benefits to his freelance graphic designer who he hires for five hours a month. It's just that if it's a voluntary arrangement that benefits both parties, they should be able to do that.

为什么这件事现在变得紧迫

The other reason this is important is because policymakers are already starting to see the trend we alluded to earlier — that a lot of younger people are going more into self-employment and entrepreneurial careers than older generations in their same age group.

Right now, if you look at some surveys, they're like, "Oh, I'm not applying for an entry-level job. I'm going to go start my YouTube channel, or I'm going to pursue self-employment opportunities." So that workforce is going to keep growing.

I actually think it's going to keep growing because of AI as well. I think AI is going to be what I call a transaction cost shock — another framework we learned in grad school that I'm applying to this. I think that's going to increase more people going into self-employment, having these small startupy solo self-employed businesses, because they can use the tools of AI as a complementary instead of relying on teams of workers and employees at a company. To do the copy editing, you can rely on AI, and things like that.

A Democratic policymaker from Nevada reached out a month ago because she said she's mentoring young people, and she noticed that a bunch of them are not applying to jobs like normal jobs, but going into entrepreneurial endeavors, self-employment, gig work. She was interested in the portable benefits policy solution as a way to help and empower those workers.

That's part of the reason we're seeing it take off — it's a low-hanging fruit solution. No real downsides. It's legalizing access to benefits for independent workers, allowing for these voluntary arrangements to occur. And people are seeing the handwriting on the wall — self-employment is going to continue to grow.

交易成本下降与企业家社会的扩展

The way I read your stuff is that it relates to your transaction cost point. You're trying to find ways — when we take this reduced transaction cost — that allows us to have a greater scale and scope for the entrepreneurial society. In the 1950s you got organizational individuals — you went and worked in a corporation, you were tied to all of that. The transaction cost of being in an entrepreneurial economy in great scope might have been very high at that time, and now we're finding ways to lower it so we can have more and more of this kind of activity. A large part of it is precisely because many parts of the supply chain can have transaction costs reduced because of technology.

Yeah, you said it better than I did, but that's my argument.

十、修辞、措辞与"政策语言学"

I have a question for you, because you're very good at phrases. How much time and effort have you devoted to the rhetoric of the argument — the way you said, "I need flexible benefits in order to have a flexible labor market"? How much time and effort have you done in your thinking — not your writing itself, but the thinking about the writing — to the rhetoric that you've developed in talking to people in Washington, D.C., or to corporations?

I also developed legalized access to portable benefits, because you can't argue with legalizing access to benefits.

It's really important. If you're doing something new, and if it's slightly complicated and complex — because when we first started talking about this, people would say, "Well, benefits for independent contractors? Why do independent contractors need benefits? They're self-employed, they're all entrepreneurs. What are you talking about?" And then, "What is portable benefits? That's the number one question we get. What do you mean by portable benefits? Where does the benefit stay? Does it not stay with the company? Where would it exist?"

Because there's a lot of complexity in this new idea — it's not like we're doing something people have heard about for the last 50 years — you do have to devote time. And part of it is just fail fast. I've had really bad conversations with people, and I was like, "Okay, that didn't land well. I need to rethink that."

It's still happening now. I was in DC a couple weeks ago, and 9 out of 10 of the conversations I had were really great — we're talking to policymakers, explaining the research, explaining the ideas. But I had one where it just was not landing right. That's a learning opportunity too.

You might say it's better when you start early on, because you're failing a lot, so you're learning a lot. In the beginning when we were talking about this — seven years ago — a lot of the stuff wasn't landing well. Especially terms like calling people gig workers. They're not always gig workers — gig workers are a subset of the self-employed and independent workforce. A construction worker is an independent contractor, but he wouldn't think about himself as a gig worker.

Seven years ago, I was learning a lot more and developing the [framing], because we were seeing a lot more failures. "Okay, had this conversation, it was not great, didn't land well. What's the new phrase?" Flexible benefits for a flexible workforce — I can't remember how I came up with that, but I think it was through multiple conversations. I love it — when I see a quote, I save it in a note.

Mercatus actually has had some pretty good ones. Pathologies of privilege is a very good phrase. Permissionless innovation is another one — a great phrase.

Mainline economics — that hasn't always landed as well as it was hoped to land.

But I really do like flexible benefits for a flexible labor force.

十一、最后一个问题：什么让你夜不能寐？

I have one last question. I'm mindful of your time. So besides your little ones, what keeps you up at night at the moment — that you cannot sleep about, you have to wake up to think about, but then in the morning you can't wait to wake up to think about it? What's the most curious thing going on at the moment?

Okay, it's like a deep thing. But you're probably the best person to talk to about this.

I'm thinking about the tension you have between having a vision for how the world should work, but making sure that I'm not [just] seeing evidence that confirms that vision.

Do you know what I mean, Pete?

Yeah. The easiest person for us to fool is ourselves.

Yeah. Obviously we all need some kind of framework — we just spent this whole conversation talking about the importance of frameworks and perspective, because it helps us make sense of complex systems and even decide which questions are worth asking in the first place. But at the same time — does that framework quietly shape how we interpret what we're seeing, including which evidence we find more persuasive or not?

That's something that literally keeps me up at night. How do I square that tension? Because perspective and vision of how the world should be has always been my guiding light. But then I'm like, "Am I following confirmation bias? How do I make sure that I'm not?"

Nozick、Feynman 和 Dennett

No, it's 100%. It's one of the questions that — you're not there yet, but Robert Nozick, the philosopher, used to say that sometimes the best philosophy can only happen when you have gray on your temples. You're not there yet. But it's a great question: how do I make sure that I'm not fooling myself, and constantly asking what arguments or evidence would persuade me to the opposite position?

Yeah. Like you're not reinforcing a story you believe. Because I've studied the gig economy/independent worker stuff so much, I might see a piece of evidence and think, "Oh, this is really great, it confirms what I already think." But that evidence might be bad. How do I hold it lightly enough so that I'm still open to updating it?

That's a great piece of advice to young scholars: one of the key things is to not allow your eyeglasses to be completely rose-colored. You want to use the eyeglasses, but they can be distortionary as well as illuminating. How do I ensure that I'm not getting the distortion, but instead getting the more illuminated reading?

I'm not sure that there's a scientific answer to that as much as an art and self-discipline. It's an attitude. Richard Feynman, the great physicist, used to say:

"Don't ever fear asking a question which you can't answer, but always run away from answers that can't be questioned."

If in your peer group people are putting forth arguments that can't ever be questioned, you should maybe say, "I'm not so sure that's working." But if they're willing to weigh the other side of an argument, allow the other side to talk and listen, and then disagree with it — sometimes vociferously disagree with it, but nevertheless give it a fair hearing — then you kind of know as a third party looking at it, "Okay, that's the right attitude to take." Obviously all of us have some degree of success or failure, because we all suffer from these biases of confirmation and cognitive dissonance.

You know why it made me think of it too? Many years ago — you probably don't remember — you gave me, I can't [remember] — was it Daniel Dennett's book?

Yes, on the rules of engagement. Yeah.

Something like that. And there was that quote he used to say:

"If all you have is a hammer, then everything begins to look like a nail."

He had that quote in the book. I actually still have the book on my bookshelf, and I was looking at it a couple months ago. I was like, "Oh, this is a really great read," because it makes you think you need to be a bit more thoughtful and careful. I want to apply it to myself with the work I'm doing, because I'm so in it that I'm like, "Maybe I'm not seeing something, and I need to hold it a little bit more lightly and step back to make sure I'm not missing some evidence."

收尾

Well, Liya, I want to thank you so much for taking the time here. You are a great example to all of our current students and future students about what they can do with their economic education — as long as they allow curiosity and commitment to guide them. So thank you very much and keep up the good work.

Thank you so much, Pete. And thank you for being a mentor to me — because of you.

I appreciate that.

Thank you for listening to the Hayek Program Podcast. To learn more about the research, scholars, and work of the Hayek Program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. For more information about graduate student fellowship opportunities for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org. We hope you recommend students to our programs or consider applying yourself.
