But then all of this led to Coke Disruptive Technologies, which we talked about, which is an innovation platform for Coke to see around corners. So that one move made one large business much better and also created a whole new thing.

And so I always think about — we have 130,000 employees. What if that one principle, comparative advantage — what if everyone deeply understood that and redesigned their role to where they are truly in their power alley? And what are the results of the business if we could do that? You're never going to be perfect, but that's the vision that we have and how we apply it.

二十六、个人如何自我实现

Thinking about individuals, right? How do I self-actualize? How do I find my path of purpose, happiness, success in life by leveraging these sorts of principles in a world that feels radically transforming and continuously constrained? I don't have infinite flexibility. I'm not on the board of Coke. I'm not on the board of my company that I'm employed by. How do I find a path in this world? Because I do think many people today are struggling for that sense of purpose, for that sense of identity, for that sense of realizing their potential and feeling fulfillment in work today.

Well, that's critical. We have, what, 20-some thousand supervisors in the company, and so this is one of their top jobs — is making sure that each employee is in the right role. For example, they're working hard and trying, and part of the work they're doing well in and others not. Well, rather than beating up, "You've got to keep doing better" — like, there are certain things, if you told me I had to do, I mean, I'm good at concepts and logic and math, and to go do something else, that's quite different. I'd be a total failure. And you could whip me till I was a grease spot on the floor and I couldn't do better.

And so that's the role of the supervisors — to not go tell them, "Okay, your role is to go do this." And they're just trying harder and harder. And so you're making them miserable. And so they hate you, they hate the company.

And so that's the way you empower them. That's what Maslow said — he said that everyone has capability. And if you don't develop it and apply it in a way that creates value for others, you may be successful monetarily or in some way, but you'll be deeply unhappy your whole life because you won't be fulfilling your nature.

And I know what mine is. People say, "Well, you're 90. Why don't you go out and lie on the beach?" I said, "What? You want me to die? I'd be dead in a week." It's not your nature. No, it's not my nature. My nature is — I have this gift. And a lot of our people here may say, "Yeah, you're damn right. You use it too damn much."

二十七、教育与五个维度

What keeps most people from realizing their gift?

Well, I think part of it is an education system. So the schools need to be set up: "Okay, we're going to help you find your gift and what you're passionate about and what is motivating to you." So the whole system demotivates you. And that's the way businesses are managed — you're demotivated.

Our whole deal — that's why we have five dimensions in the way we apply this:

  1. Vision — you've got to get the right vision, capabilities of creating value for others.
  2. Virtue and Talents — we've talked about that.
  3. Knowledge — that's Republic of Science, creative destruction, all those things.
  4. And then the final one is Motivation.

And so that's what we need to do. And Joe Lamont has created the schools. He says 80–90% is motivation. And so you have games, you have other things that the kids can do that they learn from and enjoy doing. They want to do more.

And I raised our kids with these principles, and I made them do it. So I was piss poor at applying my principles in teaching them this. And he's doing it. He makes a game out of it. He has them reading the book and parts of it. And then they have competition on who can do it better, who's living up to this better, who's applying this principle better. And they're loving it.

Yeah, I'm not having my kids listen to books on tape from Milton Friedman when they're 10 years old. No — Aristotle. Aristotle. There's plenty of them.

二十八、亚里士多德磁带与五年级论文

But let me just — you were told or suggested or encouraged to listen to Aristotle on tape as a kid?

Yeah, yeah, yeah. No, he wasn't encouraged. No. We'd go Sunday evenings — Elizabeth and Chase and I would go over to my library, and I would play these tapes. I'd only play it for 10 minutes because I knew Chase's attention span. Elizabeth was on it. Boy, she was on it. She ran circles around me. She was getting straight A's and everything. And Chase would fall asleep, and then after 10 minutes, I'd wake him up. "Okay, what was his point here?" It's a test every 15 minutes.

And then — you may have seen on, where was it where you talked about, we worked together to do your airstream term paper. Yes. And why don't you tell that story?

Okay. Well, I mean, we had a fifth grade paper. "Choose your philosopher. Write about it. It's got to be 500 words or whatever." So I came up and I was like, "I don't know. What philosopher am I going to write about?" And so he's like, "You're going to write about Aristotle. We're going to work together on it."

But I learned a hell of a lot about Aristotle in fifth grade. And I turned in the paper. And the professor had a lot of red ink all over it and said, "F. You did not write this." And so I'm like, "Oh God, this is so embarrassing." So I took it back to him and I told him, I said, "Pop, we got an F on our paper." — "I know you said you got an F."

Yeah. And then I didn't think he was going to pick up the phone and call the teacher. So he said, "You know, Dr. Cohen, I'll never forget this." I was hiding under the table during this phone call. He said, "Dr. Cohen, I helped my son write this paper, and he learned a lot from it. And yet, you know, do you not want me to help my son? Do you not want any parents to help their kids learn?" And he said, "You know, I think you have a point, Mr. Cohen." And so the next day I come back and it had 99 written on the top. See? I made a contribution.

二十九、Stand Together 与教育的使命

Let me go back to something I think you were getting at — around how do you remove more barriers for more people. And this is really kind of the Stand Together story.

He mentioned education. I just want to use one example of how important we feel like transforming education is. Then we also feel like it's a movement with tremendous opportunity right now.

And so our vision for education is to go from a teach-to-test model — teacher at the front of the classroom, talking at kids — to one that's individualized education. Because we all, as we talked about, we all learn differently. Everyone learns differently. Yeah, and all of our kids learn differently as well.

So we did the research at Stand Together — and Stand Together, for those that don't know, comes from my father's efforts on social change, which he's been working on for 60 years. And Stand Together in 2003 was created — we had the insight of, "We can do a lot more together versus do it alone." If we operate in philanthropy and social change in silos, we're just not going to get the leverage to drive the change that we want to.

And so Stand Together is made up of close to a thousand business leaders. It's just like the name describes. It's a community of business leaders that align on vision and values on where we want to see the country. And it's really around this pretty simple idea that every human has a gift, but there's so many barriers across all of our institutions — education, a broken education system, a broken criminal justice system, bad policy holding people back so you can't chase the American dream and build a business, all of these things, right? So we're very broad in terms of the issues that we focus on, but education is one of the biggest ones.

And so — public opinion. Prior to COVID, roughly 20% of families were open to a new model of education, very low. And then everyone saw during COVID how screwed up the system was. And they saw their kids come home and they learned a hell of a lot more on YouTube learning than they actually did in the classroom. So after COVID, three or four years later, you look at that same data and it's 70 to 80% — families are open to completely transforming the education system. Because everyone now understands that it's just broken.

So where we're supporting — we have these amazing preferred partnerships, as my father described:

  • Joe Lamont with what he's doing at Alpha School — closing the motivation gap, meeting kids where they are, bringing gamification and the principles of Fortnite into education in a way that kids are like — he's taking kids that are failing students to top of the class in three months. Meeting them where they're at and solving the motivation gap and making learning fun and cool.
  • Sal Khan — huge partner with Khan Academy.
  • A really interesting one — the Vela Fund, that we partnered with the Walton family on — basically applying venture capital to education entrepreneurs. So coming out of COVID, there were thousands of pissed-off parents and teachers that were just fed up with the system: "I'm just going to create my own school." These small microschools. And so with a relatively modest amount of money over the last five, six years, we've helped create and seed over 5,000 schools.

So what's happening is there's this huge movement where people see, "My kids are learning the skills of what the future is going to be like." Not this teach-to-test model where you don't know how to interact with people. The reality is it's project-based learning. They need to have exposure to these AI models. You don't ban them — you empower them with these models.

It's one of the most exciting movements that I think Stand Together is really leading on. We need more partners that are willing to get behind this.

三十、Charles 回顾社会变革：得失与原则

I want to go back, though, Charles. This work at Stand Together has taken off. I know a lot of people that are engaging with you, Chase, on this work. Everything you say is so sensible. It always feels so obvious. Can we go back to the work you've done historically in social change and what you got right, what you got wrong? Because the narrow view of the word Koch comes from a broad public perception of political activity that I think has been amplified and the narrative has been written for you. And I don't think I've personally seen a lot of conversation publicly from you about what you did publicly — when, how you were thinking about social change. Maybe you can go back to the origins of the work you started to engage in and try to drive social change. And over time, what you got right, what you got wrong.

Well, it started with these principles. I mean, to me, they're the principles of human progress. But rather than — as Frederick Douglass said, "I'll work with anyone to do right, no one to do wrong" — I was only working with the people who believe in all of these. And so we were limited.

I mean, I worked with the Libertarian Party and that got so narrow and then they started fighting over who has greater, more purity in these principles to get it exactly like. And one brilliant guy, but totally this way — he said, "My libertarianism is plumb line." Anybody who disagrees, he purged. So it's almost like the Communist Party doing the same thing that Lenin did. And they admired Lenin. Yeah, "He had the right strategy." Well, no, you can't go murder anybody that disagrees. So it doesn't work for liberty. It works for totalitarianism.

And then I started reading Maslow, Eupsychian Management and his strategy. And then Viktor Frankl is the one that really got me going. And Viktor Frankl, this tremendous insight, he says, "The problem today is ever more people have the means to live and no meaning to live for."

So if you can't find a path to a life of meaning — which comes from, as I said, finding your gift and using it to succeed by helping others succeed in a way that gives your life meaning — then you have two choices. You can either choose to go for power or you can go for pleasure. And you see this today and you see this through the history of the world.

So if you choose power, then you're always going to want — you become addicted to power. "I want more power. I need more power." And we've seen that in our businesses. But you damn sure see that in politicians. And you see that in all the dictators in the world.

Then, if you say, "I've given up, I'm going to go for pleasure," and you dedicate yourself to pleasure without considering the long-term consequences, then you're going to experience failure. You also have a tendency to become an addict, to become suicidal, and to even engage in crime. And we see both of these today.

So the problem today is when you have a world that is based on power and pleasure, it's a slippery slope to totalitarianism, authoritarianism, and socialism. And that's what we're seeing today.

So the solution is to help people find their gift and a way to apply it that enables them to succeed by helping others to succeed. So what does that mean? That means we need to — all of us — more fully live up to the promise of the Declaration of Independence to better apply these principles of human progress.

三十一、政治介入的错误

And then the other thing that I screwed up on is — for the first, I've been at this more than 60 years, and for the first 50, I avoided politics or major party politics. I mean, being in libertarian party, there was no thought of winning. The only reason I got into that is, "Well, this is a time people are listening. They're interested in politics, so we'll engage in that because people are talking. So we'll throw that in the hopper in hopes it takes on." Well, the way we did it, it didn't take on at all. It blew up in our face.

But we decided we needed to get in because we desperately needed some principle-based policies. Look at all the policies that we have today. They're destructive. They're leading to more and more power and pleasure, socialism and authoritarianism.

And the mistake we made — or I made — is trying to do it through one party. You can't do that. So now we follow Frederick Douglass' advice to work with anyone to do right and no one to do wrong.

三十二、当下的政治循环与社会主义

Where are we in the cycle? So there's a rising number of political leaders around the country — initially it was in local elections, and now it's on kind of the national stage — declaring themselves socialist or some form of socialism. Are we kind of on an upswing? And what's swaying — is there a social…

I thought hell was down, not up. Damn. So where are we? I think we continue this, but we're going to hell in a basket.

It's what Thomas Jefferson said — he had slaves, but what he said about slavery, he says, "If God is just, I despair for the future of our country." That's kind of where I am. The people we're electing — both Republicans and Democrats — and the mistreatment, I mean, you look at what's being done. I mean, occupational licensure, the way they're treating illegal immigrants, you name it, across the board. And the socialists, the way they're treating all the crime, all of that. We've got to elect people who have some principles beyond power and place.

What are your principles for changing people's minds?

It's what Chase says. We find them where they are, just like we have in the company. And we show them this doesn't get results.

Now, if you're a dedicated communist — if you're like Trotsky — "If we can get rid of private property, we'll get rid of greed. And then every man will be a Goethe and a Beethoven. And we will be able to create ever-ready warehouses of all the goods, and everybody can go in because out of their goodwill they'll be making all this stuff." If people still have those fantasies, it's never worked in history. "But it's different this time." Yeah, well, that's right.

三十三、Show vs. Tell：Stand Together 的方法

So that's what we're trying to do at Stand Together. Dave, what we've learned on this is that to change minds and change paradigms, you show versus tell. And go back to what we were talking about before — bottom-up empowerment as opposed to top-down control. I think that's like an overarching umbrella principle on all of these.

So our whole thing was Stand Together, and that's, I think, why it's growing exponentially — because we have stories of people. We believe in people. We don't need top-down to come in and tell people how to solve problems.

That's the book that Brian Hooks and I wrote, which we just published. We have a new edition based on the 250th, and we got Martin Luther King III to write a foreword to it. And that's another book besides this one. If you're interested in these ideas we're talking about on social change, there's much more in that than this book on that, although we have quite a bit on this book.

One of the key concepts in that is, if you believe that people aren't the problems to be solved — the people that are in the problem, they're the ones with the best ideas and they're the source of the solution. That's a totally different mindset, right? And that's what Stand Together does. We talk about venture capital a lot. We bet on people that have found something that works.

I'll just give a quick example. Scott Strode from The Phoenix. We found Scott. His story is amazing. It was about eight years ago. He battled addiction most of his life. He had a mentor that put him in the gym and got him boxing gloves, and exercise was the thing that helped him kick addiction. And what he did is like, "Well, if this helps me, I think I can help other people with this same concept." He built a gym called The Phoenix, combined the power of community with others that are struggling with the same thing, multiplied by the power of exercise. And he was getting insane results — relapse rates that were below 10%.

So what we do is, like, instead of some top-down program to handle addiction, go bet on Scott. And that's what we've done the last seven or eight years. We've gone from — he had a couple of gyms in Colorado when we met him, impacting a couple thousand people — he just hit a million people basically overcoming addiction in the last year. That's a movement, right?

And that's a key difference, I think, of Stand Together. How do you do what you're talking about at scale and help people change paradigms? You show them with stories like Scott — that let's believe in the people and let's bet on them to transform society.

三十四、美国的经济困境

So for addiction and crime, that resonates wholeheartedly. Let me ask about the economic condition of America today. Kids are graduating college. They got hundreds of thousands of dollars in student loan debt. No one can afford to go to the doctor. Grocery bills are too high. People are worried about paying for their next grocery bill. You look at the polling data, the survey data — the average American is really struggling. More than half of Americans, 60 plus percent, maybe 63 percent have negative equity. They have more debt than they have assets. And everything's getting more expensive and no one's moving up the economic ladder, there's no mobility anymore. People are really struggling. And then they're looking on TV and they're seeing spaceships launching up to space holding flowers out the window smiling and laughing. And it's a really dark time and it really leads people down this path of "I need the government to help me. I need support. I need help. I need to elect the people that will fix this for me." How do we address the economic crisis that's facing the average person in America — the lack of economic mobility — the principles around these distraught situations… fantastic, but this fundamental economic crisis that we're facing?

That's a damn good question. It's like — here's the question. Okay, the eggs have been scrambled. It's your job to unscramble them. So that's the problem. Once you create these entitlements, I mean, you almost can never get rid of them. You almost need — well, maybe Argentina, if he can pull off what he's doing, because they were in worse shape. But they went through it. We haven't gone through it. Well, maybe we will get there.

三十五、资本主义是否长期有效

Well, let me ask a question. Does capitalism work long term? And let me give you an argument why it might not. The success at Koch Industries is built on an algorithm, your principles, and you've been able to adapt that algorithm. And as a result, scale your business, generate cash, reinvest that cash, generate more cash, reinvest that cash, and so on. And you've scaled 9,000X. You've built a compounding advantage in your business. As is the case with all successful capitalists, you build a compounding advantage. The problem with compounding in any system is it eventually eats all of whatever is in the system, or it makes it hard for others to compete in that system or participate effectively in that system. And that's the argument that's being made today against capitalism. How do we counter that idea and share that capitalism should be more accessible to all, that everyone can participate and everyone can benefit, that it doesn't end up in this monopolistic end state where no one…

Well, it starts from removing the barriers. That's what I said. We've got to work for a system where we remove the barriers that are holding people back from realizing their potential, finding their gifts, realizing their potential, and succeeding by contributing.

Okay, so — like occupational licensing. There are hundreds and hundreds, as you know, of occupations where they make it so tough. All the local people who are in that business make it impossible for anybody who starts with nothing to do it.

And then the way we're treating illegal immigrants — the ones here working and contributing, we harass them and are going to kick them out. No, we ought to welcome them and kick some of the others, the bad ones, out. But I mean, so some of this is basic.

And then we've got to reward people who want to contribute. That's why you've got to get people to be contribution motivated. Then you're going to have a life of meaning. And that's what Viktor Frankl was trying to tell us. And if we don't, we're going to fail. And we're failing because we're taking away the chance for most people to have a life of meaning because we're setting up all these obstacles. And I'm not picking on one party or the other — they're both doing it.

And then set up all these tariffs which undermine the division of labor by comparative advantage, which makes everything more expensive. So everywhere you look, it's a tragedy.

三十六、AI 与人类潜能

There's a debate happening at this moment on AI, regulating AI. How does AI become an enabler of self-actualization, giving every individual the capacity to develop themselves, accelerate and succeed, versus making a fewer number of people even more wealthy and taking jobs away from the masses? What's your view on where AI is taking us?

Well, it depends on how it's done. I mean, that's why we back Cosmos, who is doing — backing people who do AI based on these principles of market-based management, based on these principles of human progress.

And why don't you tell everybody what you've done in the book with AI?

I mean, just one principle on our approach to AI is just the simple concept of permissionless innovation. The cost of AI to get it in people's hands is dropping to an incredibly cheap level — that hopefully everyone can have access to that and then combine that with their gifts to unlock their potential and learn 10x, 100x faster. From an AI standpoint, that's our mindset on it.

And that's what we're doing internally at Koch as well. I think one of the most exciting innovations that we have is all around human empowerment, back to bottom-up empowerment with principles. So not only are we trying to make sure that we have the right supervisors that are helping people understand their principles so they can apply them, you know, with the book, we've created an app. I sent it to you, Dave. I don't know if you played with it or not, but it's called Principle Companion.

You can download it in the App Store. And it's really taking off within Coke because it's just another way to engage with principles in a very simple way and meeting people where they are — whether it's ChatGPT or Claude — and solving a problem in five to 10 minutes that otherwise would have taken a long time. We're basically powering it with the principles that are in the book. Any problem — so it's one field — whatever your problem is in business and philanthropy, and if you run a sports team, or you're having problems with your kids. It helps you with that, right?

And it doesn't give you an answer. You can't say, "Well, I got this problem. What's the answer?" No, it asks you questions. "Okay, given that, have you thought about this? Have you thought about that?" So it's a Socratic method. And we know what happened to Socrates.

三十七、合作写书：父与子

Okay, listen, we're going to need to wrap in a minute, but before we do — Chase, what was the experience like writing the book with your dad? What's it like working with your dad? Yeah. What are the biggest lessons learned from your dad?

Absolutely incredible. I'd say it's probably the most important project that I've ever worked on. Being invited into writing this book — and I've said to many people, I've learned more in the last 18 months than I probably have the last 18 years. Just because, when you write something, as you know, the depth of learning and going back into the details of the stories — figuring out how to tell that story in a book in a way that connects with as many people as possible — I always say like you really don't know something until you have to teach it. And writing a book is a form of trying to teach others with it. So my depth of learning, because I got to be alongside this guy and then many others across Koch that contributed to this book as well — learned a tremendous amount.

The other thing of just doing this with him — he applied the principles to the book writing process. So everything is so consistent. It almost drives you nuts in terms of applying principles to everything. But principles like openness, creative destruction. He's written — this is his fifth book, this is my first. But he wanted to drive creative destruction of his first four. And so I think bringing me into it, just bringing a fresh perspective, bringing technology to it, and telling stories in a different way — that's an open mindset. He could have easily said, "Look, I'm the boss. I've done this before. What the hell do you know?" But he had that mindset, applied the principles to it.

But I will say, he's such a stickler with words. And you should talk to my mother about this and how it drives her effing nuts. So I got an 18-month window on what it's like to be mom, maybe. We had one chapter in there on stewardship and I think we were on version 27 of it or something. Yeah, I rewrote it at least 15 times myself. "You are not applying the principle of marginal analysis. Version 26 was pretty damn good."

And so, anyway, but we had a lot of fun with it. Yeah, of course, there was tension in terms of how you'd write something or whatever. But overall, most important project I've ever worked on.

Yeah, now, words have meaning. People say, "The proof is in the pudding." No — "the proof of the pudding is in the eating." For God's sake, that means nothing — "the proof is in the pudding"? What, you stuck your foot in it? He corrects the Coke leader on that in about every meeting we're in. Oh, and then I used to give grammar lessons at our discovery board.

三十八、Charles 谈与 Chase 合作

What's it like working with Chase? What's it been like writing the book?

I thought he'd help some with the book and would give us a different perspective on how to reach young people and do some things and improve it from his perspective. But he's taken it to a whole level — like bringing in AI and having AI as a Principle Companion to the book. And all of these — that are way beyond me. So he's taken — as he has in everything — taken it to a whole level beyond where I am.

三十九、留下什么遗产

You've written a lot of books. You've built an unbelievable business — one of the greatest on earth. You've engaged in extraordinary social and civic engagement. What do you want the legacy to be?

I want our country to more fully live up to the promise in the Declaration of Independence.

Charles Koch, Chase Koch. Thank you.

Thanks, sir.

All in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：科赫的"私有性"本身就是一种产品
表面上，科赫工业是"没有上市的最大私营公司"。但真正的洞见是——**他们的私有结构不是结果，而是核心产品**。
注意 Charles 说的那句话："如果我们上市，**没有人会相信这个故事**。"这暴露了一个深层悖论：他们最有价值的资产是**"不被市场理解的权利"**。
公众市场本质上是一台**叙事压缩机**——它要求每个企业把自己压缩成一句话（"我们是石油公司"、"我们是消费品公司"），以便分析师能定价。一旦你被压缩，你就必须生活在那个标签里。Georgia Pacific 在做木业时市盈率只有 6 倍，因为市场只能用"商品"这一个标签理解它。
科赫拒绝压缩。"能力边界 vs. 行业边界" 这个区分听起来像管理学口号，但它真正的含义是：**他们买下了"模糊性"的版权**。在一个所有人都被迫清晰自我定义的世界里，"无法被简单描述" 本身成了护城河。
## 二、跨领域连接：科赫的组织模型 ≈ 进化生物学中的"亲缘选择"
Chase 反复强调他们不是 Berkshire Hathaway（独立运营、互不相干），而是 "*

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Which Human Traits Are Evolving Fastest? – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

1. 一个让人不安的隐喻：基因组在"震颤"，而非"沉睡"

Reich 用了一个极其反直觉的措辞——genome is vibrating with natural selection。我们通常把进化想象成一条缓慢上升的曲线，但他描述的图景更像是布朗运动下的弦：每一个 DNA 位点都被某种张力轻轻拉扯，只是大多数张力太弱，被我们的统计显微镜过滤掉了。

这里藏着一个被忽视的认识论陷阱："没看到选择"几乎从不等于"没有选择"，而是等于"我们的样本量不够"。免疫性状被"选中"上榜，不是因为它最重要，而是因为它的遗传结构最"上镜"——少数大效应基因，容易被检测。行为/认知性状由成千上万个微效应基因构成，它们在选择压力下集体漂移，却几乎不可能在单个位点上留下显著信号。

意外观察：在基因组研究里，"什么被强烈选择"这个排行榜，本质上是一份可见性排行榜，而不是重要性排行榜。这和社交媒体上"什么内容在传播"几乎是同一种偏差结构。

2. 跨领域连接：农业革命 = 人类的第一次"代码热更新"事故

把 Reich 的"演化错配"假说翻译成软件工程语言会非常有趣：

  • 狩猎采集者的基因组 ≈ 为低密度、低病原、间歇性饥饿环境编译好的二进制；
  • 农业革命 ≈ 在没有重启系统的情况下，把它热部署到一个全新的运行时（高密度、共生动物、稳定碳水、慢性病原暴露）；
  • 过去 5000 年的"加速选择" ≈ 系统在运行中疯狂打补丁，patch 集中在 I/O 层（免疫、代谢），因为那里报错最响。

这就解释了一个深层悖论：最剧烈的自然选择，不是发生在我们"野生"的时候，而是发生在我们开始"驯化自己"之后。文化变革不是逃离了生物学，而是重新点燃了生物学。农业不是进化的终点，而是它的二次爆发——人类用文明给自己制造了新的选择压力，再用基因去回应这个压力。

这与经济学中的"创造性破坏"（Schumpeter）惊人同构：剧变期才是真正的演化窗口期。稳态扼杀适应，冲击催生适应。

3. 一个尚未被充分说出的暗示：我们正活在"选择加速期"的中段

Reich 说青铜时代以来选择强度在上升，而非衰减。但他在采访里没把这个推论再推一步——而我觉得值得推：

如果生活方式剧变是选择加速的引擎，那么过去 200 年（工业化、城市化、避孕、抗生素、屏幕、超加工食品、空调、择偶市场全球化）所引入的"环境冲击"，在密度和速率上远超新石器时代的农业革命。这意味着：

  • 当代人类基因组很可能正处于一次比青铜时代更剧烈的"震颤"中；
  • 但因为我们活在它的中段，且文化适应（医学、技术）会屏蔽掉一部分死亡相关的选择信号，我们几乎无法实时感知它；
  • 它的痕迹会在未来 1500 代的考古遗传学家眼中变得显而易见——就像 Reich 现在看农民一样。

第二个意外观察：进化生物学最常说"人类已经停止进化"的，是那些生活在变化最快环境里的人。这本身就是一种认知偏差——我们最不可能察觉的，恰恰是发生在自己身上的选择，因为我们正是被选择的样本，而不是观察者。

一个系统性的尖刺：Reich 的方法论本身，是一种"对过去的特权"——只有当一段历史足够远，DNA 才能告诉我们它的故事。我们对自己当下的演化处境，注定是后知后觉的考古对象。

📝 转录文本

基因组在"震颤"：自然选择无处不在

Multiple analyses we do suggest that the genome is vibrating with natural selection, and there's all sorts of weaker effects that are there that would be picked up in larger studies even than we've done. And in fact almost every position in the DNA is correlated to a position that — and being dragged in one way or the other — by natural selection. Instead of being quiescent, natural selection is everywhere, even though it's only 2% of the frequency change. It's tugging the positions in one direction or the other everywhere.

性状层面的富集分析：免疫与代谢领先

So, we analyzed these positions that we had identified, these hundreds of positions, the ones we were super confident about. And we looked to see whether they were randomly distributed in the DNA or whether they had patterns.

What we did is we looked at maybe a hundred or so traits where there had been genome-wide association studies for all sorts of different traits — like ones associated with immunity or autoimmunity, or behavior, or metabolism, and basically other things. And for each of these we could ask: are the genetic variations that are known to affect these traits from genome-wide association studies — do they have an unusual number of genetic selection signals?

And what we found is there was a vast enrichment, by about a four- or five-fold, for immune traits. That is, there was a super concentration of selected signals in immune traits. Whereas also we saw a strong enrichment for metabolic traits — things that might impact your obesity or fat traits or type 2 diabetes — and really almost no detectable enrichment as far as we could tell for behavioral traits or for psychiatric traits.

澄清：行为性状并非"未被选择"，而是难以被检出

Interviewer: And just to make sure I understand — this is not to say the behavioral traits or psychiatric traits or cognitive traits are not under selection. It's just that the individual sites where such traits are controlled are not especially likely to be among the locations that you've identified as under selection.

Reich: Yeah, that's exactly right. So it might seem from the results of that analysis that in fact immune traits are highly selected and that there's been no selection for behavior in the last 18,000 years in this part of the world. But in fact, that's a wrong conclusion. And in fact, we have evidence that that's a wrong conclusion. And in fact, there's clear evidence of selection also on behavioral traits.

And the reason we think we see — and we have evidence that this is so — much weaker signals for behavioral traits, is that behavioral traits we know from other studies, medical studies, are underpinned by much larger numbers of genes than immune traits, which are underpinned by relatively small numbers of genes of strong effect. Behavioral traits are shaped genetically by very large numbers of genes of weak effect, and we just don't have the statistical power to detect these very weak signals there.

So when we do an analysis where we look at our very strong signals of selection, that collection of very strong results is very effectively querying the immune traits, but is not very effectively querying the behavioral traits. It may still be the case — and I guess it is — that immune traits are the most selected category, but it is not at all the case (and in fact we can prove it's not the case) that behavioral traits are not selected.

调和悖论：为什么我们没看到 100% 的频率差异？

So we think there's two reasons why natural selection has — we've been able to prove really that there's two reasons — why, how to reconcile the previous observations with our new observations.

Remember the previous observation is that natural selection seems to have been quiescent over a time scale of hundreds of thousands or many tens of thousands of years. Reason that you don't see 100% different in frequency variance across Europeans and East Asians.

So now we're seeing hundreds of positions that are rocketing up in frequency with selection rates 1% or more in a lot of cases. So 1% or more selection rates will mean that there'll be rapid doubling over periods of dozens of generations. And so over 1,500–2,000 generations like you see separating Europeans and East Asians — shouldn't you see many genetic variants that are 100% different in frequency across populations?

解释一：我们正处于"加速选择"的时代

So we were able to show that this is explained by at least two factors. So one of them is that we actually, in this part of the world — Europe and the Middle East — are in a period of accelerated natural selection.

And one way to see this is to look at this enrichment pattern that we're observing, where immune traits are unusually associated with these selection signals. And we could compare the last 5,000 years of our time period — what's called the Bronze Age and further onward — to the previous 5,000 years. And what we see is that this intensification of selection around immune traits — similarly, the intensification around metabolic traits — has accelerated over this time period.

So it's not like natural selection has been at the same rate over all places and times. In fact, it's increasing over the time period we're analyzing. And so plausibly the whole time period has increased compared to previous periods. So we're in a period of intensified selection.

解释二：农业革命带来的"演化错配"

That's not implausible, because this is a population that went through a huge shock in terms of the way people live in the culture. So this is a population that almost everybody we're analyzing are farmers or food producers in one way or another.

Farming was invented for the first time anywhere in the world in the Middle East 11 or 12,000 years ago. The people who invented farming exploded into Europe after 8,500 years ago and spread across Europe and expanded rapidly in the Bronze Age. There was an intensification of how people lived — with much higher population densities, people living more and more next to their animals and getting their diseases and exchanging their diseases with them and with each other.

And so this is a period of rapid, rapid change in terms of how people are living, resulting in different biological needs of this population. So it's not surprising perhaps that, in the context of these dramatic changes, the biology of the population might be not in the ideally adapted position. That is, that there might be what some people call an evolutionary mismatch — where you take a genetic variation that's evolved in hunter-gatherers and put it into farmers or pastoralists, and it's not exactly right.

And so what you're seeing is the DNA of this population — which is descended from hunter-gatherers only 10,000 years ago — reacting to the shock of having been moved into an agricultural, and Bronze Age, and high population density, and urban environment. And a hypothesis is that what we're seeing is the adaptation that occurs as a result of that.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

这里藏着一个被忽视的认识论陷阱：**"没看到选择"几乎从不等于"没有选择"，而是等于"我们的样本量不够"**。免疫性状被"选中"上榜，不是因为它最重要，而是因为它的遗传结构最"上镜"——少数大效应基因，容易被检测。行为/认知性状由成千上万个微效应基因构成，它们在选择压力下集体漂移，却几乎不可能在单个位点上留下显著信号。

item.title: [Lex Clips] Fast robot teleoperation (at ultra-low 8ms latency): Kyber explained | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的核心：从"质量优先"到"时间优先"的范式倒转

大多数人讨论视频技术时，本能地想到"画质"。但 Kyber 揭示了一个反直觉的真相：当人类（或 AI）进入控制回路的那一刻，视频的本质不再是"被观看的内容"，而是反馈信号。这是一次从"媒介"到"神经系统"的身份转变。

令人意外的观察是：这位创始人引用了一个被行业长期忽略的事实——时钟漂移（clock drift）。当机器人有 5–10 个摄像头加各种传感器时，真正的瓶颈不是带宽，也不是编码速度，而是"现实是否真的发生在它被记录的那一刻"。换句话说，Kyber 不是在传输视频，它是在维护一个跨地域的、可信的"现在"。这跟金融高频交易里 PTP（Precision Time Protocol）同步的逻辑惊人一致——只是金融保的是钱，Kyber 保的是物理动作的真实性。

二、跨领域连接：Kyber 与高频交易、神经科学、相对论的同构

这里有三个出人意料的跨界映射：

  • 与高频交易的同构：HFT 工程师追逐微秒，因为"先到 1µs"决定盈亏；Kyber 追逐 4 毫秒，因为它决定一台机器人是否会撞到你奶奶。两者都在解决同一个抽象问题——让远端的决策与本地的现实保持因果一致。Forward Error Correction（前向纠错）的"宁可多发一点也不重传"策略，在 HFT 的市场数据多播里几乎是镜像设计。
  • 与人类神经反射的对照：人类视觉-动作回路约 100–250 毫秒。Kyber 的 7ms 意味着远程操作员对千里之外机器人的反应速度，比对自己手的反应还要快。这是一个哲学上的奇点：身体的物理边界第一次被技术超越。
  • 与相对论隐喻：嘉宾说"让距离消失"（make distance disappear）。但物理上距离不会消失——他真正消灭的是主观体验中的"延迟感"。这是技术版的等效原理：只要延迟低于人类感知阈值，远程操作和现场操作在认知上不可区分。

三、商业模式与未来形态的隐藏闭环：从"远程操作"到"远程观察"

最被低估的洞见藏在那句轻描淡写的话里："未来所有远程车辆都会被一个 AI 模型 teleobserve"。

这意味着 Kyber 的真正终局不是"人操控机器人"，而是"AI 监督一支由 AI 驾驶的机器人舰队，而人只在异常时介入"。这是一种三层架构：自主 AI → 监督 AI → 人类。在这个模型里，Kyber 不是一个传输协议，而是"AI 监管 AI"的物理基础设施——一种新型的、可审计的"机器治理层"。

再加一个意外联结：他选择 AGPL + 商业双许可的模式，本质上和 MongoDB、Grafana 的策略一致，但应用场景特殊得多——当机器人控制协议成为开源公共品时，整个机器人产业的"操作系统层"会被压平。这可能比软件领域的开源化影响更深远，因为它直接决定了未来几十年里谁有权远程触碰物理世界。换句话说，Kyber 正在写的，可能是机器人时代的"HTTP"——只不过这次，传输的不是文本，是动作和因果。

📝 转录文本

从 FFmpeg 到 Kyber：为什么"每毫秒都重要"

Speaking of networking and latency, so your new effort as we mentioned is Kyber, which is uh aimed at ultra-low latency. As you say, every millisecond counts, and uh you're applying that to remote control machines like robots, drones, computers. Can you tell me about it?

Sure. Um, if you start from where we used to be, right? You used to use FFmpeg to encode files, right? And then we used FFmpeg and VLC to encode in streaming services, right? And then you need to go lower and lower. And the question was where to — where we can — can we go?

Mhm.

Um, and this question is a very important because there are many use cases where you need to be fast. And it's when you have feedback interaction, right? We're not just listening to something. You're actually controlling it, right?

反馈回路：视频作为控制信号

Because — and that's the biggest difference that compared to what we've done so far — is that I need video to have a feedback on something that is happening live. Whether it's a drone flying, whether it's um controlling a humanoid robots from distance, whether it's controlling a rover, whether it's playing a video game in the cloud gaming — because this is um what I did on a previous job, right? I was CTO of a cloud gaming startup.

Um and this is a very interesting topic because you push to the limits the network. You need to be to care not about the quality like we've done on video and we've talked about with x264. You care about latency because a milliseconds is meaningful when you're controlling a car, right?

Well, you you've seen you use Waymos, right? When Waymos don't work, and that happens even if 1% of the time, there is someone that is basically remote controlling that. Um and this is exactly the stuff that we're building. It's a really an SDK um platform uh to do end-to-end control of machines.

单一连接，多路流：QUIC、同步与时钟漂移

So, this comes up quite a lot in a lot of different contexts in robotics. So, obviously teleoperation, teleop is becoming more and more important. Um including for training uh robots via machine learning.

Yes. And what we do is a bit different from an everyone else is that we take only one socket, one connection, which is a quick uh protocol based on UDP, um which is interesting because it's done for low latency. It doesn't have two of the what we call the TCP end-of-line problem and HTTP end-of-line problem. It's ciphered by default, but on the same wire we send multiple streams like multiple tracks. We send audio, we send video, but we also send the comments, right? Uh mouse, keyboard, gamepad, and so on.

And we do that while maintaining coherence, right? Synchronization. Because what people don't realize is is that clocks actually drift. And when you're controlling a robot, a robot is going to have like two cameras, five cameras, 10 cameras, a ton of captors, GPS, and so on. Um and if you want to train correctly your robotic AI model, you need to have all those that are in sync and currents.

And what we've done, and it's all the stuff that we learn on VLC in broadcasting real time and MPEG-TS that Kiran knows well, is that we account for clock drifting. And so when I record a Kyber stream — a robot — I am sure that it's going to be predictive in the way you play it back. And so when you're going to do recording and training of your AI model, you need to be sure that every time you retrain based on the data the data is going to stay coherent. And clocks actually drift. Like the existing solution works with one camera. Once you're going to have five or six, it's more complex.

So, you want to make sure that the visual snapshot perfectly matches the time it actually happened.

Exactly. And also if you're going to control, right? I do something on robot, I need to be sure that it is actually happening at that precise time, right? And so we have on the the server, which would be a robot, a time of like real-time stamping mechanism accounting for clock drift for that, right?

应用场景：让距离消失

So, that's one of the use case of Kyber — to control robots. I also think like remote drones, remote uh whether it's defense or non-defense, remote cars, remote submarines. There is many places in industry or remote surgery where the expert cannot go everywhere the machine is because it's dangerous or it's too costly, right? So you allow people to have machines next to you, right?

The goal of Kyber is to make distance disappear um because it's either projection of skills or projection of power, right? So, imagine we we are all like — you've seen the Meta ribbon and everyone else, right? You need to stream there, right? Because you're not going to run anything over there, right? So, you need GPU power that's on the cloud on the phone to stream that.

And so all of these use cases needs to be not about extremely low latency, but real-time latency for video. And so that means you need — we are toying with the encoders so that the encoders encode a frame in 4 milliseconds. And Kiran with his company also goes under those type of of of latency because you need to optimize at max the local latency, right? Because it's the decoder, the encoder, um and so on um because this time is going to be added to your networking time.

可靠性：前向纠错与跨洋演示

Um so and it's not just about low latency, it's also about like reliability. We do clever things like uh forward error correction, right? So, forward error correction is you over-transmit a bit of data, right? A few percent. Um and while over-transmit you allow to lose some packets because all of that is very difficult over uh internet network uh where you're going to do things very far away. Um and if you check that all packets are delivered, you add a ton of latency. If you don't want latency, what we do is that we over-transmit some data that you can re-trans- reconstruct on the client side when there is um things that are broken, right?

So, um and we um — if you — if you days weeks ago we were doing the demo around uh Las Vegas for the CES about we had a rover that is fully 3D printed. It's very simple. It's a car, right? It's a small car with a um telescopic arm. And it was actually controlled from France, right? And the the video uh was uh with a webcam and and a very small server, right? A small a small PCB but basically running and sending that to someone that is on the other side of the planet.

Uh and so there is so many use cases. You can also think about having AI who are going to control many drones and so on. And the technically we need to be amazing in video. We need to be amazing at networking. We need to care about any milliseconds in networking, in encoding time, in decoding time. And also you need to integrate very low level.

"Glass-to-Glass" 延迟：4 毫秒的圣杯

So, syncing everything together well, but how — like what kind of latency can you get to? Like what when you say milliseconds is what — what's the goal?

So, my goal is 4 milliseconds glass-to-glass latency. Um —

What's glass-to-glass mean?

So, it's easy, right? You have a computer which is running a program, right? Probably a video game. And this one is actually running, right? It could be — it's an example of a robot, right? And you have the replicate that is done through the network. And you want — if you take a 1,000 Hz camera, you can take a picture and you want that to be at 4 milliseconds. 4 milliseconds means 240 Hz. Right?

Yes, nuts.

Um so far we we achieve um 7 milliseconds from a Windows to Windows or Windows to Mac. Um and if you look in the timing, most there is around 3.5 milliseconds inside the Nvidia uh hardware encoder and around 2 milliseconds on the Intel decoder, right? So, like the encoder plus the decoder is already 6 milliseconds, right? So, in order to go down, we'll need either to have some other type of codecs um or some better encoder that are faster. Uh but 4 milliseconds is — would be the grail. That's pretty nuts. I love it though. I I don't think there's anyone's ever achieved that, right? That's fast.

Achieve that with custom hardware. With SDI, with professional hardware, but I want that to work over the internet. I want that to work with any robot where you're going to have a small Jetson Nano in it or or N150, right? I want that because there is going to be millions of robots or drones — or just rolling robots or flying robots or or swimming robots, right? It's just you — a machine that you control.

Teleoperate → Teleobserve：AI 监督的未来

And in order — either you need to teleoperate them or when everything will be fully autonomous, you need to teleobserve them, right? You need to check what's happening.

Yeah. And in my view in the future, like all those remote cars will be teleobserved — observed by an AI model, which is just going to say, "Well, everything is go- good." And when it's not good, say, "Hey, there is a problem." And then you have an operator, right? And this is going to be about safety, right? When you have your humanoid taking care of your grandma or my grandma, I want to be sure that everything goes well and I'm not in those type of horrible scenarios where the robots is dangerous. Or when I'm driving — I want like the car to to stop when it should stop and if needed someone takes care of that right?

[snorts]

And so there is so many cases — scenarios about real-time. And so the goal of Kyber is to make real-time control of machine distance disappear.

开源、双许可与一个热爱机器人的工程师

That's that's incredible. Some of the same technology, some of the same ideas we've been talking about is is connected to what you're doing.

And for me it's amazingly challenging, right? Because I would say that on video I'm doing okay, but networking I'm — I have so much more to learn, right? It's about like congestion protocols, bit rate adaptation in real time, but it's it's quite funny.

And so I created this project and we have fundraisers in the US, of course. But it's open source, right? This is important, right? Like we've not said that, right? But everything on Kyber is open source.

Well, how do you make money?

It's a dual license, commercial and AGPL, right? You remember what you said about about uh licenses. Basically, if you want to use Kyber in your product, you must have your full product open source. If you want to use this amazing technology, but not open source, you pay the commercial license, right? So the small people or the the the hobbyist and the the very small guys who want to do that, they can use the technology, they build something that is open source and cool.

That's awesome.

And if you're a large company, you're going to have the support, all the IP, the right to modification and so on. So yeah, it's really cool. And also I'm building robots and I love that, right? So like the robot we have is 3D printed. We are finishing a demo with an actual wing, right? Like it's type of drone wings that is also fully 3D printed. We are trying to do a sailboat that is 3D printed. And we'll work on some humanoid. Of course, they're not going to be very good robots, right? It's not our job, but we're here for everyone to make robots cool.

Uh you're talking to the right guy. I love robots. There's a bunch of them upstairs. Uh teleop is going to be really really important, especially as the number of robots scales across the world, so 100%.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的核心：从"质量优先"到"时间优先"的范式倒转
大多数人讨论视频技术时，本能地想到"画质"。但 Kyber 揭示了一个**反直觉的真相**：当人类（或 AI）进入控制回路的那一刻，视频的本质不再是"被观看的内容"，而是**反馈信号**。这是一次从"媒介"到"神经系统"的身份转变。
令人意外的观察是：这位创始人引用了一个被行业长期忽略的事实——**时钟漂移（clock drift）**。当机器人有 5–10 个摄像头加各种传感器时，真正的瓶颈不是带宽，也不是编码速度，而是**"现实是否真的发生在它被记录的那一刻"**。换句话说，Kyber 不是在传输视频，它是在维护一个**跨地域的、可信的"现在"**。这跟金融高频交易里 PTP（Precision Time Protocol）同步的逻辑惊人一致——只是金融保的是钱，Kyber 保的是物理动作的真实性。
## 二、跨领域连接：Kyber 与高频交易、神经科学、相对论的同构
这里有三个出人意料的跨界映射：
- **与高频交易的同构**：HFT 工程师追逐微秒，因为"先到 1µs"决定盈亏；Kyber 追逐 4 毫秒

item.title: [Business of Sport] Beating Man United, The Push for Promotion & The Cutting Edge Transfer Strategy of Grimsby Town
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：Grimsby 不是在"经营足球俱乐部"，而是在运营一支"伪装成足球俱乐部的资产管理公司"

表面上这是一个英乙球队冲超的励志故事，但你仔细听 Polly 描述的财务结构，会发现一个被刻意低估的事实：俱乐部正在用"持续亏损 130-190 万英镑"作为一种受控的研发预算。她说"我们亏损在收窄，但同时排名从第 16 上升到第 7"——这不是经营失败，这是 VC 式打法在足球场上的精准复制：用可承受的烧钱换取资产的估值跃迁（球员、品牌、社媒粉丝）。

更隐藏的细节是：她明确说"球员阵容的 asset value 正在显著增强"。注意她用的词不是"实力"而是"资产价值"。Jamestown Analytics 在这里的真正作用，不是帮他们赢比赛，而是 把每一次签约都转化为一笔可估值、可转售的金融头寸。Grimsby 实际上是 Tony Bloom 数据帝国的一个"实验性下游分仓"——他们不只是数据的使用者，而是这套模型在英乙生态位的"压力测试样本"。

二、跨领域连接：足球俱乐部的"维基百科结构"与"乐高品牌策略"

你有没有注意到她处理 Man United 比赛时说的那句最反常识的话？"我让员工准备好赢的预案。" 这不是足球语言，这是 灾难应急管理（disaster preparedness） 的逻辑——为低概率高影响事件预先布局供应链、媒体动线、零售产能。

她说赛后零售订单从印尼、马来西亚涌入；社媒涨 40% 并且留存住了——这就是惊人之处。大多数体育"病毒事件"的半衰期不超过 72 小时。她留住流量的秘诀是什么？用"战术板磁铁恶搞 Amorim"那条视频——一条比赛事本身（900 万观看）更广泛传播（1000 万观看）的二次创作内容。

这恰恰是乐高品牌方法论：核心产品（积木/比赛）只是入口，真正的护城河是基于核心 IP 的幽默化、可二创、参与式内容生态（电影、迷因、社群作品）。Grimsby 已经无意识地学会了：真正稀缺的不是球迷，是"球迷愿意主动转发的瞬间"。

三、被忽视的系统性真相：英国低级别联赛正在变成"反 Sky 体"

最让我意外的是 Polly 说的一段冷数据：周中比赛上座率掉三分之一，Sky 转播扩大让客队上座率进一步下降。这看起来是个运营痛点，但拉远一格看——这是英国足球商业模型的内部矛盾正在显性化：转播权收入越涨，对低级别俱乐部"现场经济"的吞噬越严重。

而 Grimsby 的所有应对策略——社区基金会创造 400 万英镑社会价值、和地方政府合作引入寄养家庭、靠 Lloyd Griffith 解释吟唱给美国人听——本质上是在构建一个 "无法被 Sky 转播替代的体验层"。

这里有个跨领域联结很有意思：这跟独立书店在 Amazon 时代的存活逻辑完全同构。独立书店活下来的不是卖书的，是把自己重塑为"社区第三空间"的；Grimsby 活下来的，将不是"播放足球的场地"，而是"地方身份认同的物理基础设施"。

她那句 "All Town, aren't we?" 听起来像情怀口号，但放在这个框架里，这是一句精确的市场定位声明——她声明的不是俱乐部归谁，而是这个俱乐部归属"地方"这个比足球更大的产品类别。

啊哈时刻

Polly 真正的隐藏 USP，不是数据分析、不是 Man United 经验、也不是性别身份——是她是英格兰极少数同时理解"球队是金融资产"和"球队是公共基础设施"双重账本的人。她在用资本市场的语言和投资人对话，用社区福祉的语言和议会对话，用幽默的语言和球迷对话——而这三套话术指向的是同一份资产负债表，只是用不同的会计科目记账。这才是为什么"四位女性 CEO 中的一位"这件事被持续低估：她代表的不是性别多样性，而是英国低级别足球生存所需的认知多样性。

📝 转录文本

开场：从击败曼联到冲击升级

We started off this season with an amazing win against Manchester United in the League Cup. The whole town was absolutely celebrating the football club.

In our final operational meeting before the game, I'd said to the staff, "Make sure you're prepared for if we win." They looked at me like, "Is she alright?"

Being a chief exec of a football club is just absolutely amazing, and it wasn't really an option that I saw growing up. There weren't many visible female role models at the top end of the game. I see it as my duty to shine a light on the opportunities that there are for women in the game, and I'll do as much as I can to hopefully show women that they can do this role.

We see up to a third drop off on a Tuesday night or a midweek game, which is really quite hard for us when a third of our income comes from match day. Now with the Sky deal and some games being more accessible to watch from home, that also affects the away attendance.

We've scenario-planned for League One and for League Two. I think what's really important is that our recruitment has actually just got one plan. So we're looking at players that will fit within our budget but can perform in League One. So regardless of what league we're in, we'll look to go again and perform again next year.

进入正题：赛季回顾与攀升联赛的愿景

Charlie: Polly, it is so great to have you in the studio. Welcome to the Business of Sport.

Polly: Thanks Charlie, good to be here.

Charlie: It is the perfect time because you've had a hell of a week — well, hell of a season, let's say — a really, really great time for you in League Two, now making it into the playoffs. How was it at the weekend when that was all secured and tied up?

Polly: It's fantastic. I'm just so pleased for the players, the staff, and most importantly the fans as well. They've been on such a journey with Grimsby Town. I think we're one of the clubs with the most promotions and relegations, so it's absolutely been a rollercoaster to be a Town fan.

When I joined the club, the men's team had just finished 16th, the women's team had just got relegated. So to finish 9th last season, and now secure playoffs, and the women's team have been promoted — it's been a brilliant season across the club.

Charlie: How much of that at the beginning of the season do you try and iron out?

Polly: It's so easy to say, "what's your goals?" — and I'm sure you could say, well, we would like to get promoted, or we'd like to win the league. The vision or the strategy that I laid out when I joined the club was to climb through the leagues. And that "S" on "leagues" is important because it applies to the men's and the women's team.

I think to give the staff all of that the clarity around the direction in which we're traveling in — invariably, everyone and every department wants to get better each year or each month. We always want to progress. And we're really fortunate to have a growth mindset across the club, which allows people to constantly review what we're doing and find out ways in which we can do that better. Whether that's in retail or in nutrition — across the club, we're constantly looking to improve, obviously trying to balance that with being sustainable at the same time.

But I think, you know, setting that vision out to climb through the leagues shows people in which direction we're traveling.

三大战略支柱：球场表现、商业收入与社区角色

Charlie: You spent a long time — I was having a look — the recent history of Grimsby, there was a long time the club was out of the Football League. So that's relatively quick progress to be going 2022 back into League Two, now playoffs, trying to push to League One. But how do you balance the work that you're putting in off the pitch while ensuring that the club's moving forward on it?

Polly: Yeah, so we've split our strategy into three core areas.

The first one is on the pitch and trying to gain a competitive advantage. Secondly, we look at our commercial revenues, but also recognize the power of the badge and the importance of the club within the community — so trying to give back to the community, but also open the gates of Blundell Park and welcome the community in.

So if I touch primarily on the performance side and how we're looking to gain a competitive advantage: we're working with Jamestown Analytics on our men's first team player recruitment. So they've got a great track history and record of identifying talent, developing players, and then selling them on for a profit — whether that be at Brighton, Union Saint-Gilloise, look at this season and the impact that Hearts are having in the SPL.

Charlie: Tony Bloom.

Polly: Yeah. And we're really fortunate to be a service user of Jamestown Analytics. The staff that work there as well as the data are absolutely brilliant, and we formed a great partnership with them. We've been through three or four windows now of using that, and we're starting to see the asset value of the squad really enhanced.

So in terms of recruitment, but then blending that with the skills of our head coach — on the men's, on the women's side, but particularly more on the men's side — in terms of player development. So if you're combining smart recruitment with player development, primarily we want to use that to achieve ambitions for Grimsby Town in terms of climbing through the leagues. But that might also then turn into player transactions as well.

英乙级别罕见的数据驱动转会策略

Charlie: How unusual is it at League Two level to use data to that level in a transfer strategy?

Polly: I can't comment on all of the other teams in League Two, but I think we're the only team in our division currently using that data from Jamestown Analytics. Other people might be using paid-for subscriptions, but essentially they'll be using the same type of data. We've got different type of data that we're utilizing and unable to access. And yeah, we see that as one of our USPs.

Charlie: Well, am I right in saying that you could look at someone like Kavio, who gets a hat-trick at the weekend — that would fall right into the smart recruitment because he came from Truro. Is that right?

Polly: Yeah, exactly. So two divisions beneath us — Truro, they did get promoted, so you could argue maybe one division below — but he's a great example. We've also had players from Iceland, from the Faroe Islands join us and do exceptionally well as well. So it's primarily domestic. We're allowed two international slots at League Two, so there is an international angle to it as well.

But yeah, amazing. Jay's a brilliant example, and again, credit to him for scoring 23 goals in his first season in the EFL, and also the staff for supporting Jay's and his integration into EFL football.

Polly 的个人故事：从草根足球到俱乐部 CEO

Charlie: I want to come probably back into this and we'll have a look at the club. But firstly, before we go any further, I really want to know a little bit about you and your story. It's a unique one. It's a really cool one. So rather than putting words in your mouth, can you just tell everyone a little bit about what it's been like to kind of go on that journey and make it to Chief Executive of Grimsby now?

Polly: Sure, yeah. So I've worked in football for over 20 years. Started playing grassroots football, went into coaching, studied at university, and then found jobs in football.

I think to have parents as role models and enablers — my dad took me to watch Nottingham Forest, and we were season ticket holders. So to experience football from a really young age. And my mum also encouraged him to get his coaching badges. So he set up the first girls' football team in our local area, and then he went on to be chairman of the grassroots club.

So to have that support from my parents when I was growing up was absolutely fantastic. And then to be, yeah, a few years later, being a chief executive of a football club is just absolutely amazing. And it wasn't really an option that I saw growing up. There weren't many visible role models, certainly female role models at the top end of the game. So I feel very fortunate to be in this position now. I think it's four female chief executives in the 92.

从曼联女足到 Grimsby：身份的延续与跳跃

Charlie: What I was really interested in here was the fact that you came from Manchester United Women into the job.

Polly: Yeah, I think I've always considered myself to be a woman in football. I've been surrounded by the men's game and the women's game throughout my whole life. And I had a fantastic experience at Manchester United, hybriding the football and the business part there. So I was able to bring that experience here to Grimsby Town.

It's not a well-trodden path from Manchester United to Grimsby. Some people may want to go the opposite way. But I think there are similar scale of budget, similar operation in terms of the average attendances that Man United Women were getting. We've probably outgrown that now at Grimsby Town, but the scale wasn't too big of a difference. Obviously gone from a global brand to a more of a local or regional brand in Grimsby Town.

But the experience of working across the club from everything from negotiations to commercial and Champions League operation — got to Wembley, won the FA Cup there with Man United. So had some amazing success on the pitch there, but also the experience of working behind the scenes on the operational part I've brought with me to Grimsby.

So yeah, fortunate that I've got now the men's and the women's under my remit, which is really important to me. And I see it as my duty to shine a light on the opportunities that there are for women in the game. I'm a member of Women in Football, and I do as much as I can to hopefully show women that they can do this role.

I wasn't an expert on League Two when I joined the club. I wasn't an expert on Grimsby Town when I joined the club. But I think being open to learning, and also knowing what my role is — my role isn't to know all of the players in the league. I can't begin to reel off a whole 148-year history of the club. I'm trying my best, but I don't have that. But what I do bring is knowledge and experience from football and the business.

倾听本地、融入社区

Charlie: How have you found the fan interactions and building relationships on that level?

Polly: Yeah, brilliant. I think I'm really respectful of the fact that I'm not from Grimsby, and I don't have that 140-year history to be able to call upon. But I think I was really mindful to listen effectively — both to the staff (some staff have worked there 42 years) and obviously fans that have been attached to the club for their whole lives.

So I had to be open to understanding the context. I couldn't apply what had worked at Brighton or at Manchester United or at Forest, where I've been on the board, directly into Grimsby. I had to really open up and listen to what was unique and what was important to them, but also then blend that with my experience at the same time.

Charlie: Was it a big difference where you're going from Man United Women's, who — while Man United as a brand is very old, the women's team famously wasn't. So you were part of an early stage of the development of that, where I would imagine you were probably trying to create a lot of that fandom as well if it wasn't directly translating from the men's team — to then go into a club which has 140 years of history. Those two are very different skills. They're very different ways of approaching a job. Did you feel that?

Polly: Yeah, certainly at United. So I think the women's team was five years old when I joined, and I was first head of women's football. So a real honor to be the first one there to formalize some of their professionalization.

And you're right, it was a startup within an establishment, which allowed us to copy a lot of the processes, some of the documentation from the men's side and apply it. So the club secretaries, for example, absolutely brilliant — what they didn't know about disciplinary history, some of the fines, the regulation. They'd obviously worked on the Champions League for decades with the men's team to apply that knowledge to the women's game. What a great resource to be able to call upon.

Similarly with marketing and commercial. But it wasn't about copying and pasting. It was about understanding what the women's game needed, what the fan base was, the fact that it was a new audience. It wasn't a copy and paste of the men's. And actually, rather than having a waiting list of fans for the men's team, we needed to go out and grow an audience for the women — tell them where the fixtures were, who the opponent was, tell them about the WSL, about the players. So it was a different approach that we needed.

And then here at Grimsby, similarly, we don't sell out every game. So there is an element of still promoting some of the matches, putting on different offers, thinking about how we're going to maximize the attendance. What does the match day experience look like for different generations that come to the games? So yeah, lots of learnings across all of the clubs that I've worked at.

But yeah, this season actually with Grimsby, we've had the biggest attendance since '91-'92 — biggest home attendance. And the average attendance, I think, is the highest since '98-'99 as well.

季后赛与上座率的史诗时刻

Charlie: For Grimsby in the league, or was that the Man United game?

Polly: So the Man United was absolutely packed to the rafters, understandably so. And actually for our playoff home leg, it will be similar as well. So tickets have sold faster for the home leg of the playoffs than they did at Man United on the opening days of sales.

Charlie: That's incredible. So just to be clear, we're recording this on Wednesday. First leg is on Sunday. We'll then release this on Tuesday, and then you'll have the second leg on Friday.

Polly: Yes. And I can confidently say that the game will be sold out and we'll sell all of our allocation for the away leg as well. So there's no questioning the appetite.

Charlie: How do you prepare for these games from the business side, from the behind the scenes?

Polly: I mean, it is another match day. It's another home fixture. So we're well-versed in terms of the operation of that. I think the biggest scenario is if we get to the final, and having to already plan for some of the final — some of the logistics, the different categories of tickets, and how we operationalize some of those elements.

So we had a call with the EFL yesterday to go through some of those bits and pieces around branding and marketing and all of those things. So I think it's having to scenario-plan, even though you might not get there.

Charlie: Interesting. So the EFL will, they'll have a call with you and it will talk about the actual match day and everything that goes around it. What's on you and what's on them to organize for that type of thing?

Polly: They give us a framework — whether it's around operations for the Royal Box or for travel, or if we want to do some branding of the changing rooms, all sorts of things. The detail of it is quite thorough.

So yeah, we had our staff from the ticket office, from marketing, from operations, from security all dialing into a call yesterday and starting that preparation with our fingers crossed.

Charlie: Who gets the Royal Box?

Polly: Well, naturally, the owners, the investors, I'll be in there. Yeah, so yes, it'll be a good day out, hopefully, if we get there. Special days.

Charlie: But from a business perspective, I'd imagine they also move a needle as well.

Polly: Yeah, exactly. And I think our home leg will probably sell out of our hospitality. For the away leg, we're looking to do a pizza watch party at Blundell Park so we can capitalize on the enthusiasm for the game, but also that sense of wanting to watch it together and share those moments. Because we won't have enough tickets — or barely enough tickets. We're looking at about only 500 to take away, which is probably one of our smallest allocations throughout the whole league. So we're just looking at ways in which we can try and bring people together and share in what should hopefully be a memorable day.

And then if you do make it to Wembley, what's distributed to the clubs and what factors into, I guess, the broader EFL pool? Yeah, so we're still working through that detail with the EFL. Naturally, they'll take quite a good chunk of the profit for operating, and that's one of their key revenue generators as well. But it provides us with opportunity to do some print-on-demand merchandise where we can take a cut as well.

12 月那场员工大会：把击败曼联的感觉留住

And I think we started off this season with an amazing win against Manchester United in the League Cup. We managed to reach the fourth round of the League Cup and the fourth round of the FA Cup, which has already brought some amazing experiences and memories for our fans and for our players this season.

And I could recognise the atmosphere within the club. So I actually called a staff meeting in December, and I said, "I think we've got a real opportunity here." And I put two dates up on a slide to show to all of the staff. And it was the end of season date — the last fixture for the women's first team and for the men's first team.

And I just asked all of the staff to close their eyes and remember what it felt like beating Man United. So the smell, the anticipation, just the scenes of the fans at full time, running onto the pitch, inflatable Harry Haddox doing the rounds in the Pontoon Stand. All of the impact that it had in the wider community around the window displays that they'd done in the cafes, the bars, the pubs, the restaurant. The whole town was absolutely celebrating the football club.

And I just wanted the staff to hold on to that feeling and remember it, because I could see the great work happening across the club — men's, women's, academy, on the business side. And I thought, you know what, I really think we can do this.

So to have already achieved the women's team running the league, winning the league; the academy team have won the league; we've had record sales in retail. I'm just so proud that the staff were able to harness that. Hopefully that gave them a bit of enthusiasm or encouragement to go on and achieve what we've done this year.

那一夜的曼联：2-2、12-11 与 Henry Brown

Charlie: I read an article you did with, I think it was The Athletic, just before the Man United game, but obviously just after you'd been drawn against them. I'd imagine that was a pretty special draw alone for you, having Manchester United come to you — albeit maybe you would have liked to have gone to them a tiny bit for the commercial side of it. But as part of it, I think you said at the end, "and dare I say, maybe we win."

I remember watching it. I can remember watching it at home. The most incredible game — was a 2-2, right? You were ahead. They scored a lot. I thought, oh no. And then what ensued was the most ridiculous penalty shootout. 12-11 or something.

I mean, that must have been — and just to put for people who didn't watch it and know — that means that basically the players who took them first, the whole team's taken them, and then it started again, back from the first penalty takers. That's how many were scored.

Polly: Yeah, it's just the most special evening. And I'm really fortunate in my career that I've been to the Men's and Women's Champions League finals, to Euro finals, to FA Cup finals. But that day was just so, so special for the fan base primarily.

It's one of those amazing August evenings where the sky was pinky purple. You're right, we went two-nil up, scored quite early on in the game, but the noise at kickoff was just deafening. It was amazing.

In fact, there was someone that sits on the row in front of me. And he says he's actually a Man United season ticket holder and a Grimsby Town season ticket holder. And he wasn't sure until that point who he was going to support, until he got to his seat. And then he said there was no question. He was black and white through and through.

I think, unless you were in the ground and hearing the atmosphere and the real support for the team — because we had no right to win that game. There was no expectation on us to win, but we'd also started the league pretty well. We were in a good place as a club culturally. And we thought, why not?

And just some of the features — so they arrived on two coaches, and the way that they kind of filtered into down the tunnel and into the changing room was a bit like the opposite of Mary Poppins's bag. So many people going into such a small space, and they just ended up having to come back out again because there just wasn't enough space for all of their staff and all of their players. So just little things like that, you know, that you remember.

But yeah, just the noise after those two goals went in — and then you're right, they pegged it back to 2-2 quite late on. There's like a monsoon-type flash flood, so much rain that came down during the second half. And at one moment the ball was holding up in the wet grass. I was thinking, "Oh God, the match better stay on and we better be able to complete this."

And then obviously no extra time, straight to penalties. And the fifth penalty on our side was a first-year pro, Henry Brown. So he stepped up, you know, he's carrying the ball from the halfway line to the penalty spot. And I'm thinking, "Oh gosh, Henry Brown." And I think the whole ground, the home town, knew — we're in a first-year pro here, Grimsby born and raised, one of our own stepping up. And he slotted his penalty away absolutely beautifully. My heart — I was so relieved for him.

And then that took us out into the knockout phase of the penalty shootout. And to see Mbeumo hit the bar, and then the pitch invasion that ensued was just amazing.

But actually, in our final operational meeting before the game, I'd said to the staff, "Make sure you're prepared for if we win." They looked at me, like, "Is she all right?" But I just knew that if we won, we'd be global news.

And we saw retail sales come in from Indonesia, Malaysia — global retail sales off the back of it, but also the amount of media interest as well, straight away at full time, piling out onto the pitch to get the kind of hot interviews after full time. And I think our local BBC Humberside were there till like 2am, doing the recordings of what had happened all day. The next day, our wide forward, Charles Vernon, was on This Morning with Rylan Clark. So just like incredible scenes that wouldn't normally happen to a club like Grimsby Town. But yeah, incredible scenes.

病毒时刻之后：把瞬间转化为长期价值

Charlie: It's about moments, isn't it? But it's also about them capitalizing on those moments and trying to make them last as long as you can. And that's incredible to have merch sales out to Indonesia. I'd imagine for your supply chain, that's probably a slightly different challenge to meet. Yeah. But did you see a conversion service over a longer term of interest in the club? Yes, after that result that's kind of stuck to now — what, recording in beginning of May?

Polly: Yeah. So we've seen a 40% increase in our social media channels.

Charlie: 40%?

Polly: That we've been able to retain as well. So I think 9 million people watched the game. That was, I think, 5 million domestically, 4 million internationally.

Now, the Saturday after, we played Bristol Rovers at home, and our media team did a bit of a play on Ruben Amorim with his tactics board and the magnets. So I don't know if those watching the game remember that where he's…

Charlie: How could we forget?

Polly: Yeah. Playing with the magnets. Poor Ruben.

Now, if I take this back to a strategic perspective, some of the work that we did around our brand and our purpose and who we are dows into our sense of humour. And they wanted to make a bit of a play and actually announced our team using a tactics board and magnets sat in the dugout.

Now that piece of content got 10 million views. So it's more than the people that actually watched the game watched that piece of content. So it's just an amazing reach. But yeah, that led to a 40% increase in our followers, and we've been able to sustain that with our engagement this year.

当一支英超球队来访：媒体接待的量变

Charlie: It's so good. I mean, I can only imagine the environment around the stadium after that. What's it like for you building up to a game like that from your job perspective? Does it significantly change? Are there things that you have to suddenly consider — things, requirements that you have to meet in the stadium — when you have a big Premier League team coming?

Polly: Yeah. Other than it being a normal match day in terms of those operations, which we've got in hand, it was the media interest. So normally I think we get half a dozen media reps in the ground. That day we had over 100. So from a scale perspective, trying to then schedule in all of the interviews.

And we really wanted to capitalize on that opportunity. So our owners made themselves available for the media. Men's head coach, David Artell, made himself really good, did so much media in the lead-up to it as well, because we recognized that it was an opportunity that we wouldn't normally get.

So we wanted to talk about the club. We wanted to talk about the work that we're doing in the community and that really close-knit relationship that we've got between the club and the fans. We wanted to talk about the good work that we're doing in the community and how we're trying to operate sustainably as well. So we recognized it as an opportunity and took full advantage.

财务结构：六百万营收、两百万损失之间的精细调度

Charlie: Let's go in there because it's our favorite topic. You mentioned sustainability a few times. My favorite definition of sustainability comes from football, obviously — still in the context of losing money. But all jokes aside, there's been a huge amount of work going on, and there continues to be a huge amount of work to try and make football clubs better businesses.

I'd be really interested just to hear from your side, from the Grimsby perspective: financially as a business, how you're set up, how you're structured, the core kind of revenue drivers. And if you're able to, an understanding of the ownership and their support of the team.

Polly: Sure. So I think if we talk in terms of losses, which is quite normal, unfortunately, for this industry. So last year we announced our accounts and we lost 1.9 million. That was over a 13-month reporting period. Prior to that, it was 1.67. This year we're forecast to do 1.3, 1.4 million loss. So it's a fairly steady loss. But within that period of time, we've moved from 16th to 9th and now 7th. So we've been able to retain the level of loss — or in actual fact close the level slightly — but also perform on the pitch as well.
