Charlie: So because you're losing well a third less there from this year than you would have done last year — 1.3 to 1.9 is a very good jump while you're also moving up the league. Was there a really clear indicator of that as to how you were able to achieve it?

Polly: Yeah, so last year was over a 13-month period, so 1.9 is a longer period. It also included two Junes, which is notoriously quite difficult in football because you've got your normal outgoings, but you haven't got as much incoming because you're not playing matches over that period.

Really, we're trying to move the dial in every area. So a third of our contribution or our income comes from central distribution from the EFL. Another third comes from match day — so primarily from tickets and some from hospitality. Then on the commercial side: our turnover is 6 million. 2 million comes from central distribution, 2 million from match day, a million from commercial.

This year, we've just done a million pounds in retail for the first time ever in the club's history, which is actually on a par with some Championship clubs. So we're punching above our weight there. And then we've got a small amount from non-match day and streaming as well.

So we try to move the dial in every area possible. We've done a small amount of increase on our season tickets and our match day prices, a small amount on our retail. But we've really tried to focus particularly on the retail around items that sell well.

When I joined the club, we had like a bright yellow away kit, which from an identification perspective on the pitch worked great. But in my mind, there's not many people that would wear a bright yellow shirt down the pub, down the seafront.

Charlie: Speak for yourself.

Polly: Sorry for any yellow-wearing fans out there, but I just didn't feel that there were strong retail items to be worn as leisure items at the same time. So we really focused on what else could sell optimally — not just look great on the pitch, but off the pitch as well.

商业合作：从本地工程公司到冠名权

Charlie: Central distribution, as we said, is really important. But as you go lower down the leagues and it becomes less important, it puts far more of the onus on you to create more of that value. Do you see the direct correlations we've just been talking about with social movement — a 40% increase in media? And then if you're looking at building out something like commercial, I would imagine — and correct me if I'm wrong — a huge amount of the commercial revenue you drive is local. Do you now see, or has there been very early indicators of a broader social reach maybe opening doors to do commercial deals in a broader geography and range as well as obviously keeping that core local association?

Polly: Yeah, I think the core businesses are key for us. And it's not just in hard cash or in branding of the front of shirt, but actually what they're able to give in terms of services as well.

So our front-of-shirt sponsor for next season is a local company called Blackrow. They're an engineering firm, and they're also working with us on our steel around the ground. They helped us to redesign some of our gates at Blundell Park as well, which are really iconic.

So we're really looking at smart ways in which we can partner with local, regional, international companies that align with what we're trying to do as a club. Whether that's in sustainability, environmental purposes, or just trying to help us be competitive on the pitch, we're open to various types of conversations.

We've still got some assets available that are big-ticket items that we'll continue to go after. If anybody's listening out there, then happy to have a conversation. But things like naming rights for Blundell Park — we're looking at a new training ground, which is a huge asset as well. So there's still some areas to go at that will, in my opinion, help us close the gap again whilst also being competitive on the pitch.

Charlie: How's the balance you have to strike when talking about something like naming rights for a stadium built in 1899, holding such deep-rooted associations with the community and the club, to then bring a commercial element to it? Is that a challenge that you have to manage?

Polly: Not with the conversations that we're having at the moment, I think, because of the local interest that we've got from a commercial perspective in the club. And a lot of the people from the town are fans of the club would want to work with us around naming rights. So to retain the Blundell Park along with a sponsor title is what ideally we'd like. There'd probably be some more flexibility on the training ground, for example.

So each asset is a case-by-case. But we're fortunate at the moment that there are some really large international companies that are on our doorstep that we're talking to, as well as local companies that are doing well in their own industry that want to work with us and align.

If we take the kit, for example — the companies that we've worked with in front of shirt, back of shirt, sleeve and shorts have worked with us around the design. So they've been flexible on their color palette, for example, so that they'll blend in with the color tone of the kit to make it look more visually appealing. Whilst it might not be directly in their core colors for their own brand, they've worked with us to get a nicer overall piece.

基础设施：球场、训练基地与可持续投入

Charlie: Do you own both assets, the training ground and the stadium?

Polly: We own the ground, we lease the training ground at the moment, but we're looking to acquire a new piece of land for a training ground.

Charlie: And build a brand new property. Do you think that's really important? We talk a lot about that ownership of both those pieces of infrastructure and the ability for a club to then obviously leverage the value.

Polly: Yeah, infrastructure is key, and it becomes a huge asset of the club. My experience at Brighton, and obviously them announcing recently the acquisition of land to develop a new women's stadium — that's really setting the standard for how to operate a club sustainably by investing in infrastructure, whether that's stadium or training ground, then looking at the people and the players.

So that's our ambitions at Grimsby Town as well — to build a training ground that isn't just for the elite arm of the club, but also takes into consideration our foundation's delivery, but also understanding what the community want. So we're in those conversations at the moment to try and blend together funding streams to realize some plans for that training ground that can then become a key part of the community and benefit all.

Charlie: Do the men's and women's team train the same?

Polly: Not at the moment, no. So our women's team will train similarly to our academy team. So we're actually split across five different sites. So we've got Blundell Park, we've got a men's training ground, we've got a location for our academy, and then the women. So yeah, we're quite disparate at the moment.

"All Town, aren't we?" —— 女足、社区与商业逻辑

Charlie: How's the women's team in its evolution? You obviously mentioned great news, going up, getting promoted. How does that position yourself? Where are the demands on the business?

Polly: We have a saying at Grimsby Town: "All Town, aren't we?" And that really encapsulates what we're trying to do. It's for all. So that's men, that's women, that's young, that's old. That's everyone from supporters all the way through to men's first team players. So they're an integral part of what we do.

But also from a business perspective, we're generating revenue from the women's team. The matches that they've played at Blundell Park have either made a profit or broken even. And actually, they're also a catalyst for withdrawing grants as well. So I spoke about the training ground — we've used their inclusion in the club to bid to be a host training ground for the Women's World Cup. And we also know that through grants through the Football Foundation that we need to secure some peak slots for the women and girls to train on. So they're actually integral, not just from being the right thing to do, but also from a business perspective.

业主结构：从两位粉丝创始人到投资集团 1878

Charlie: Tell us about the owners. It's so important in this day and age, particularly for clubs lower down the leagues, to have solid trust and commitment from an ownership group who can provide some of the support and the funding. I've seen from your ownership it looks like they've turned some loans into equity, which will be hugely valuable for the club. But from your perspective — obviously working with them and how they've approached the ownership of the club — how's that been? Where are they so important?

Polly: Yeah, so Jason and Andrew are Grimsby Town fans. They've done exceptionally well in business, and now they're reinvesting that back into the community through the football club. They took over in 2021, so they've had an absolute ride by getting promoted out of the National League at the first time of asking, and now into League Two playoffs. So hopefully they're enjoying themselves as owners.

Two years ago, they diversified their support of the football club by opening up an investment into their holding company, called 1878. So I'm now working with half a dozen or so other investors, both overseas and here, to bring their business acumen and to diversify some of the risk off just Jason and Andrew.

Actually, Jason Stockwood had to step back last year because he took a role with the government. So he's now mainly fan. He's still coming to a lot of the games, but yeah, he's supporting us from afar. So really my interactions on a weekly basis are with Andrew Pettit, and bringing his passion for the club, but also his business acumen to it as well.

Charlie: What have those interactions been like for you? You've got so much legacy in here. You've got so much opportunity on the other side, you're managing other people's money and how that all works, and trying to make sure at the bottom of it, not too much drops out.

Polly: Yeah, and I don't take that role lightly. It's really important that it's their money, and I try to make sure that we get the biggest bang for the buck — whether that's in the player recruitment through Jamestown Analytics, or making sure that we're doing the right thing for the community. The strides that we've been able to make in retail are a key part of that.

But actually, what they've been really open to is ideas about how we can generate more money. So for next season, if that's LED boards, or CRM system, or we're looking to refurbish some of our hospitality areas — because actually I've spotted areas in which we can make a bit more money. It might take a little bit in the capex and opex, but that means we can go again to close that gap. And they've been really open to those ideas.

球迷沟通：信任在银行里

Charlie: How do you communicate with the fans? How is that relationship? And I want to get into the value of this to the community — but how's that relationship between the club and its fan base at the moment?

Polly: I'd say some of the sentiment that we're getting back indicates that we're in a really strong position. And that's taken work and commitment to build that trust between the club and the fans.

We have one of the Supporter Trust chairs on our board. So fully integrated into the operation of the club. And they'll bring some challenge, whether that's around season ticket prices or match day experience. And we'll have those open discussions.

I think one of the key parts we've tried to do is to communicate transparently — or as transparent as we can be whilst I'm running a private business, but one that's also a community asset — to explain some of the decisions.

So two years ago, our loss was 1.67 million, and it felt that the narrative was led around that figure without the context and the nuance behind it. This year we've faced into the figure of the 1.9 million loss to explain that's been over a 13-month accounting period, why it's at that level. The fact that some of it has been mandated to us by needing to do refurbishment on the ground and in the steel and infrastructure at the ground.

So I think just being able to explain some of the decisions. Also some of the rationale for why we needed to increase season ticket prices and match day prices by fronting that up. I did a YouTube video for our fans to just explain that. Also avenues through a fans forum. So I think to try and be approachable and explain some of the decisioning rather than just putting it out in a faceless statement has hopefully earned us a bit of credibility in the bank.

古老球场的现代化：在情感与功能之间

Charlie: Before we came on air, we were just talking about the incredible strength and what makes the kind of English football pyramid so special. And it was when you were talking about six-and-a-half thousand average at League Two, getting eight-and-a-half thousand in for some of the biggest games. It's so unique to be able to do that across so many different clubs — and even, as we're saying, outside the Football League, fifth and sixth tier, you'll see incredible attendances too.

For you looking at that development, that communication, how do you look at something like — we talked about the ground building, you know, developing a core piece of infrastructure that holds such a deep-rooted role within the community — to then go and look at modernizing it, changing it, updating it, basically making it, I guess, fit for purpose in the right way, but keeping those core elements that drive the deep associations that the fans may have with the team?

Polly: I think that's one of the hardest parts of the role, is balancing those different needs across the business, or working out what the priorities are.

So some of the infrastructure work has been mandated to us by our Safety Advisory Group. So we've had to spend a lot of money on steel to make sure that we can keep the place open. Safety is paramount. So that will always be our number one.

Outside of that, we'll work with the Mariners Trust and our supporter groups to listen to what some of the biggest challenges are. I'm also really mindful that it's not always about the bottom line — that we have to do some fun things, and we should do some fun things as well.

So we've done a couple of TIFOs this year. The Mariners Trust have done a surfer banner. We've had some clapper banners, which I know are slightly divisive to some, but worked really well at the right time to help generate the atmosphere in the run-off, in the business end of the season, if you like.

And one of our investors is absolutely brilliant with this. He's really good at reading the room and working out what the team need. And actually a little bit of spend here and there that might not have a direct return on the balance sheet, but affects the mood and the sentiment, can also in the long term pay off too.

Charlie: To make those decisions, is that part of the key conversation you'll have in a weekly meeting with the ownership?

Polly: Yeah, exactly that. There are some non-negotiables — if it's steelwork, that is a non-negotiable, so that has to be done.

Outside of the non-negotiables, it is then a question of prioritization. Some might be "nice to haves," but it's my job to be able to say, "this is how much investment is required, and this is the return that we'll get over this period of time."

So it's putting that business case to them, discussing pros and cons. The owners or some of the investors might have their own ideas from their experience of things that we could look to do together as well. So it's that collaborative approach — all hands to the deck, really.

Blundell Park 的扩容讨论

Charlie: What are the plans with the ground? For those that may not know, what are you looking to do? Again, I mentioned the infrastructure in some of the clubs is incredible, but it is — one of the stands open in 1899. So there's elements that do need to be addressed. But how are you approaching that ground?

Polly: Yeah, so in terms of Blundell Park, we're 8,500 capacity, or operational capacity. We've only sold out twice in the last two seasons — or two times this season, two times the season before.

So until we're getting to the point where we're selling out on a regular basis, or we might need to look at waiting lists — at the moment we are the right fit for the size of the stadium.

If we're fortunate to get promoted next year and the sale of season tickets goes through the roof, it might be that we are looking at some semi-permanent seating areas that we have on a season-long basis. We looked at temporary seating for the Man United game, but it just didn't wash its face financially. We'd have had to charge on £150 per ticket, when the other price was £29 for a match day ticket. So it just didn't stack up. And we also have to think about the egress and the safety operation as well.

So everything is considered in the round. But if we can add to the capacity and there's the demand there, whether that's on a semi-permanent basis or a permanent basis, we'll always look at it.

周中比赛的挑战：上座率掉三分之一

Charlie: What's the — horrible question for you — what's the strategy to sell out every week? Because not only does it build the atmosphere brilliantly, right? If you actually can sell out another 2,000 tickets a game, seriously moves the dial on the commercial and total revenue lens.

Polly: Yeah. Do you know, actually, some of this is actually dependent on the opposition as well. So I think one team brought 64 fans.

Charlie: Really?

Polly: So that does really affect your gate revenue and what you can do on a match day. So we can do as much as we can being the home club. But if the opposition is only bringing 65 fans, it's very difficult to maximize that. Other clubs will sell out their allocation, but we are largely dependent on that too.

I think what we're trying to do from our own, like the home attendance, if you like, is be strategic about it and recognize that we know that midweek games we see a drop-off. So are there things that we can proactively do to bring in students, shift workers, local community — maybe those that don't travel as far as some on a Saturday or on a weekend — to come in?

So we're looking at every quarter, every month, to be able to see, what can we do proactively for each game? At the same time, we don't want to undervalue the price of a season ticket. So we have to make sure that that balance is right as well. But yeah, we're trying to be as strategic as we can.

Charlie: That midweek thing comes up all the time with the executives we speak to and the impact of, you know, well, let's say a Tuesday night at 7:30 kickoff. Is it just, we've got kids in here who will have school the next day, so you're not going to have the same outings? Because it's a really hard thing to balance. Even if you can get as creative and ambitious as possible, there are just some kind of fundamental challenges with a schedule that places X number of games in midweek for you to overcome.

Polly: Yeah, so we see up to a third drop-off on a Tuesday night or a midweek game, which is really quite hard for us when a third of our income comes from match day. Primarily, school is for children of under-14s or whatever — it's really difficult for them to come on a school night. So that's a big hit.

I think the other challenge that you've got is now with the Sky deal and some games being more accessible to watch from home, that also affects the away attendance. We've got Gillingham at home on a Tuesday night. I'm not sure how many people are going to make the journey up from Kent all the way up to north-east Lincolnshire.

So it does impact. Again, if that's where we can be smart in doing some of the focus groups or strategic work to try and make sure we create the atmosphere as well as affecting the bottom line, we will do.

Charlie: Is it too simple to say if you go up, you'll sell more tickets?

Polly: I don't think so. I think that's something that we're forecasting and hoping for if that's the case. What I'm mindful though is if we go up, but perhaps performances don't quite match how we've performed this season, we can't bank on perhaps selling out for the whole season through.

社区的力量：四百万英镑的社会价值

Charlie: Tell me properly — we keep touching on it, but again, something you and I talked about beforehand — was the real proper community value of a football club like Grimsby, and what it actually brings, and what it means to a place in this country that has a kind of a core sporting asset right at the heart of it. Because everything that we're really touching on here — whether it's fans coming in to enjoy it, or commercial partners seeing value in sponsoring with it, or incredible atmospheres — it's all about, as you told me before, I think 120, 130,000 people that live in and around Grimsby.

Polly: Yeah. Our foundation and the community is so important to us. And I'm a trustee of our foundation board. And I see daily, weekly, the impact that that foundation is having — whether that's doing healthy eating workshops, whether that's engaging with people around mental health.

I'm so fortunate to be able to see the impact that the foundation has. We know that in some areas, we have a better impact in reducing crime than perhaps the police do. So it's just — it's not lost on us — the power of the badge and the opportunity to do good with that. And it would be remiss of us not to harness that.

And I see it as it's the way that the club can give back to the community by going into the surrounding areas to engage with them, but also opening the doors to Blundell Park. So some people who might not ordinarily have the opportunity to come into the football club get to experience that.

We just partnered with North East Lincolnshire Council and their fostering family group to enable children in foster care to come in and experience football. What an amazing thing to be able to do. And that's just through a simple partnership with the council. But it's giving back to the community, and we're really proud of doing that.

Charlie: I mean, I saw you said the generator of four million pounds in social value a year. Now, that money is not recognized on a balance sheet or similar, but it's massively influential to a local area. Is this where we have a potential gap kind of arising between all of this regulation trying to come in and prove sustainability of asset and future security, which of course we all want, versus the broader role that these clubs do play in the community, generating really significant social value, but that's coming from a financial perspective as well, that is much harder, again, to demonstrate on a set of accounts that show how successful or not a club has been?

Polly: Yeah. And we actually see some of our commercial partners wanting to work with us or the foundation or buy. So they want to capitalize on the reach of the brand, but they're wanting to give back. So one of our partners has just donated some — they've bought some tickets from the club, but they're donating them to schools to reward attendance. So it's a great opportunity for commercial partners to use as well.

On the regulator point, I believe the regulator are now talking to the EFL in the community. So from a league level around the impact that football clubs are having — to absolutely recognize that point that, yes, we will engage with them around governance and financial flow and sustainability, but actually recognizing that we do more than operate football matches.

Yeah, so exactly that point. And I think the regulator will start to have a think about how we can incorporate that into their regulation and their regime.

关于独立监管者：支持，但要"比例适配"

Charlie: How do you view the introduction of regulator and how that will change also the job of an individual in a club while also looking at the broader system?

Polly: Yeah, I think if we take a step back and think about why has this come about — and it's really to try and recognise the finances in football and across the industry and how we can safeguard clubs in the future. So if that remains the core aim, then we're a supporter of the regulator.

I think at the moment we're looking into areas around governance. And of course we champion good governance. It needs to be proportional to the resources that are available at each club. We have one person in finance, one person in HR. We don't have an in-house legal team. So I think the regulator needs to be cognizant of the size and the scale that each club is operating at.

But they really do need to look at the core of the financial flow and distribution of funds in collaboration with the Premier League and the EFL and the National League. And we believe that if there's an opportunity to ring-fence funding into infrastructure, into services to players, into staff development — that that could really help sustain clubs for the long term. So that's been our consultation, or that's been the topics of our consultation with them.

Charlie: Is building a team behind the scenes with the available funds that you have — is that actually kind of a hidden challenge you have to face? You say one person in this department, one person in this department, no legal team, always play a game. Content team, we got a media and content team. If we're looking at those small ways that are actually hugely influential, that can move the dial, more money to be able to do that makes a huge difference to how you can operate as a club.

Polly: Yeah, absolutely. Yeah, for sure. And I think being able to demonstrate that and show the return that we can have by operating sustainably, as I've alluded to over the last two or three years — we've proved that we can do it whilst keeping a modest player salary budget as well.

But we generated 6 million in revenue last year. The cost was 7.9. So the loss of 1.9. And the majority of that cost is in football. So 4.8 of our 7.9 has gone on football expenditure — just under 3 million on player salaries, a million on football operations, travel, your accommodation, staff fees, those types of things. So football isn't cheap to run.

We're trying to be sustainable in terms of our football finance and our expenditure there, but also generate money to match up. So we're trying our best, but it would help if we were given a bit more from the regulator — not from the regulator directly, but enabled by the regulator into some of those larger expenses that we see in it to enable us to be more sustainable.

Charlie: If you could move one dial, what do you see as somewhere where you could really change the business in the short term?

Polly: I think a training ground — to be able to own your own asset of the training ground and to be able to generate revenue from external parties using that, so that becomes a sustainable business in its own right. I think that will be a key driver for us.

转播收入与现场体验的拉扯

Charlie: Is it kind of a strange one though as well, where you're looking at some of that central revenue and we talk about the media deal — and you may get better media deals because you get more games on TV — but those games on TV, the extra revenue that you're getting for the games on TV, is actually reducing the money that you're getting in the gate receipts etc. because your attendance reduces by a third. It's not just like, oh, we've upsold a media deal and you're getting more from Central. Isn't that great for everyone? Because there's a really hard relationship to manage throughout all of these different revenue streams.

Polly: Yeah, well, so we've lost 50K on our streaming because of the EFL deal, but I think we're up 300K — or net 250K. So we're up broadly.

What we have to be really mindful is making sure that the matchday experience still gets people off the sofa and coming into the gates or into the ground. And I don't know about you, but you can't beat match day for me. An opportunity to get off your phone, get off the laptop, get off the sofa or away from your desk and, in and experiencing something live with your family, with your friends, and to go through the emotion. For me, you can't beat — but we need to retain that.

Whether that's, again, making sure that we've got a new audience in that are engaged perhaps primarily through content first and then taking them on the journey, so that they come into the stadium and start to experience it there. But I think making sure that people are aware of the match day and then experience it and come back is primary for us.

内容策略：从战术板到 Lloyd Griffith

Charlie: How have you found the development of content as an opportunity to speak to people? It can be a really exciting one, but also a really difficult one. Sorry for saying the word — like the authenticity in what you're doing. You mentioned the humor. I think humor is a really great way to do it. And doing something like the tactics board to announce a lineup is great, right? It draws people to it. But again, a continued content strategy versus one-off opportunities — it's the hard thing to do, but it's also a great way for you to stand out.

Polly: It's so much fun. And what we've been able to do, again because of the brand work that we've done and around our purpose — so all of our player announcements for this season have been done out into the community. So we've taken our players to the seafront, to the pier, to a local windmill, to parks, to show off the town. Because we're proud to be there, and we want to take the players out there to show the community and the fans that it matters to us as well.

Another piece of content that we've done in collaboration with Lloyd Griffith — comedian and a Grimsby Town fan — is, in collaboration with the EFL, around explaining some of our chants to an American audience. So we've done three short videos that are on our Instagram. You know, "we only sing when we're fishing," that type of thing. Some of the rude versions are available too. But that got us 500K of views.

So it's an amazing alignment and collaboration there between the club, a comedian, the EFL, and a US-based audience. So all of those things together again is raising the profile of the club, poking a bit of fun at some of our chants, but also raising the profile too.

Charlie: When you're doing this, and you're looking at these kind of moments, these social strategies, how do you see that kind of developing now moving forward? How do you see these international markets who maybe are starting to engage with you through these clips building consistency in their viewership or their attention to you of like the core product, not just some of the social channels?

Polly: We're really, really fortunate that we had a documentary series — just a three-part series that saw the promotion out of the National League into the Football League — called "All Town, Aren't We?" And I think it was on Prime. I don't know if it's still available on Prime, but it was also featured in airlines as well. So when you're on a flight and you can't do anything else, there was this documentary series about Grimsby Town. And that's done an amazing job in terms of increasing awareness. It's also led to new investors as well into the holding company. So it's been brilliant by design, but also a bit of fortuitousness as well.

Obviously the EFL have clamped down a little bit on the access and aligned with the Sky Deal of what you're allowed to show. But I think telling the stories around the people at the club — hopefully we've got another promotion to document coming up as well.

But I think just showing that behind-the-scenes content, dialing into our sense of humour. We've been having talks around how do we make our match day experience really unique. This is actually one of Lloyd's ideas, I think, but maybe putting fishing nets or lobster pots on the ground at halftime and getting people to shoot into those — dials into our heritage around the fishing industry, but also modernizes it at the same time. So we're always looking for ideas of why we can blend that sense of humour with engaging content for a new audience.

Charlie: Am I right in thinking that was it in the first season of Welcome to Wrexham when Grimsby went up? They beat Wrexham in the final.

Polly: Yeah, exactly.

Charlie: I think there was a comment there from — I don't know if it was Rob McElhenney or Ryan Reynolds — just saying how much you hated Grimsby for that exact reason.

Polly: Obviously, they've gone on to do okay, but yeah, hopefully one day we'll get back and play them again.

双轨规划：英乙 vs 英甲，一套招募方案

Charlie: So I want to wrap up by looking properly then — well, the next week or so, but really then understanding what it's like having to plan kind of on both sides of it?

Polly: Yeah, so we've scenario-planned for League One and for League Two. I think what's really important is that our recruitment has actually just got one plan. So we're looking at players that will fit within our budget but can perform in League One. So regardless of what league we're in, we'll look to go again and perform again next year.

So we've already had those discussions, that review of our transfer in January, our January transfer review, looking forward to the summer transfer window. So that's already in play. We're already activating on that.

We're in a real purple patch with our commercials as well. So the phone is ringing. We've got multiple people interested in the assets that we've got available. We're doing new activations with LED boards for next season. So regardless of what division we're in, we're already looking at making strides on and off the pitch. So hopefully it won't have to be a significant drop jump, should it be League One, from a business perspective. But hopefully everything's in hand.

Charlie: Is that something you really want to make sure that you balance out, you know, it's not getting kind of drunk on the promotion, where suddenly fundamental changes happen to the business straight away?

Polly: Yeah, exactly that. And if we are to be promoted, which of course we'd love to be — and everything's behind — we want to sustain that. So we don't want to just go up and then come straight back down.

So I think being able to be in a position where we're not pushing an envelope, but we're looking at this in a sustained and strategic way — from recruitment to our hospitality offer to our retail. Everything is considered and strategic. So we don't have to make big jumps to then have big losses should we get relegated the year after.

"Sporting Chief Exec"：跨越董事会与更衣室之间的鸿沟

Charlie: You have a performance background, right? So coming into a chief executive position from a performance background is different to quite a lot of others. Does that mean you bridge the dreaded gap between the boardroom and the part of the performance, or have you put in place a team, should we say, on a more kind of technical director side?

Polly: Yeah, I'd say I'm a sporting chief exec, because I do hybrid the two — the football and the business. So I oversaw the review of the transfer window and the strategy for going into the summer.

We've had some consultants in to work with David on men's first team, either from a kind of sporting director role. We work closely with Gareth Jennings, who's well known across the football industry and has been a key supporter to the owners as they acquired the club.

So there's various people that we can tap into. But I think it's really important actually that anyone in a chief exec role has an understanding of the football. We are football clubs. Football is our primary office. We are in our biggest property. So from my perspective, you have to know the football. You have to know how it operates in order to allow the business side to function as well. And naturally there are overlaps between commercial and football, so trying to get them to marry up I think is really important.

Charlie: Did you bring the data-driven side of this to the club? Was it something you really wanted to instill, or was it already there?

Polly: Yeah, so I'd worked obviously at Brighton and seen how they'd integrated the data on the men's side, and it was starting to be adopted on the women's side while I was there. The owners had already agreed to work with Jamestown Analytics prior to my arrival at Grimsby, but it was certainly a key driver in my reasoning for wanting to join the club.

Charlie: Because it seems like such an influential part of the success that you've had. It's just fascinating that more and more organizations aren't going down the same road. I'm sure they will, particularly if this kind of news goes out wide. I'm just fascinated by it because everyone always talks about it. When you were at Brighton and you see that, was it really as kind of impressive and as different an operation on the recruitment side, on the performance side to what we've seen in other clubs or what you may have seen in other environments?

Polly: I think my experience at Grimsby is probably closer to it. And it just cancels out so much of the noise. It's really effective in what it does. It's got a great track history, but it also does blend experience and character assessment into the process as well.

So I've really designed the process around how we can optimize the use of Jamestown Analytics at Grimsby Town. So there is still absolutely a place for the head coach to be involved in the decisions. But if anything were to happen to David and he gets poached by another club, we've got the processes in place to be able to withstand that. She says, fingers crossed. And I don't wish anything on David to be poached. But this is the football industry, and we have to be prepared for that should it happen.

And I wouldn't be doing my job properly if we weren't robust enough to withstand a change right at the top. So hopefully those processes — and everybody understanding their role and where they fit in — has allowed us to maximize the opportunity of the data that comes through from Jamestown to apply it into the squad.

Charlie: And can you use that for the women's team as well?

Polly: Not yet, no. The women's team have just been promoted from tier six into tier five. I'm not sure which level Jamestown operate on the women's side — it probably just the WSL, maybe WSL2 or Champions League level. But it's not at tier five yet, yeah.

Charlie: Okay. Well, one day the women's team can get out there.

Polly: Exactly, exactly.

收尾：核心价值、长期愿景与"为什么停在英超？"

Charlie: You know, finally, okay, we look at the next few weeks — you mentioned rising through the leagues, you mentioned that for two teams. But I also imagine "rising through the leagues" could also mean the club continues to go through each step, and the continual ambition will be to climb as high as you can. How do you look at these kind of coming years, from where you've gone to, from where you started, to where you want to go to, and how you're actually going to put that plan in place to deliver it? How do you actually deal with not just the idea, but the execution?

Polly: I think it all comes down to your key values and what is the core aspects of a football club. So whether that's around your integrity, thinking about your pride, and that real connection between the club and its fan base.

So regardless of what happens in the playoffs — whether we make it to Wembley or not, or how we finish this season, or what happens next season — I think being able to retain those core beliefs and that alignment, which has been so key at Grimsby between myself, the owners, men's and women's head coach, chief operating officer — everybody's on the same hymn sheet — and being able to communicate that effectively with integrity.

Of course, we'd like to be in the Premier League. Yeah, of course, Champions League. Why stop at the Premier League? Of course, we'd like to climb all the way to the top. But I think doing it in the best way that we can, communicating with fans and maintaining that connectivity, is the most important thing.

Charlie: Have you loved it?

Polly: Yeah, yeah. Honestly, I think because I didn't grow up knowing that I could be running a football club — to now be here and seemingly having a bit of success with it on and off the pitch — it's just been a brilliant time to be part of the club, and really enjoying working with some fantastic staff. We've got a great supporter base. We're one of the biggest supported clubs on the road and at Blundell Park as well. So yeah, bright future. Plenty to be excited about.

Charlie: Look, we'll be watching. I will be watching both on Sunday and the following Friday with fingers crossed. But it's been fantastic — so great to chat through it. Thank you so much, Polly. It's really, really interesting.

Polly: Thanks, Charlie. We'll count you in as the latest Mariner.

Charlie: Please do. All right. Great.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：Grimsby 不是在"经营足球俱乐部"，而是在运营一支"伪装成足球俱乐部的资产管理公司"
表面上这是一个英乙球队冲超的励志故事，但你仔细听 Polly 描述的财务结构，会发现一个被刻意低估的事实：**俱乐部正在用"持续亏损 130-190 万英镑"作为一种受控的研发预算**。她说"我们亏损在收窄，但同时排名从第 16 上升到第 7"——这不是经营失败，这是 **VC 式打法在足球场上的精准复制**：用可承受的烧钱换取资产的估值跃迁（球员、品牌、社媒粉丝）。
更隐藏的细节是：她明确说"球员阵容的 asset value 正在显著增强"。注意她用的词不是"实力"而是"资产价值"。Jamestown Analytics 在这里的真正作用，不是帮他们赢比赛，而是 **把每一次签约都转化为一笔可估值、可转售的金融头寸**。Grimsby 实际上是 Tony Bloom 数据帝国的一个"实验性下游分仓"——他们不只是数据的使用者，而是这套模型在英乙生态位的"压力测试样本"。
## 二、跨领域连接：足球俱乐部的"维基百科结构"与"乐高品牌策略"
你有没有注意到她处
